topics.title: Modular Arithmetic: Integer Arithmetic - Integer Division
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECAT595 - FOUNDATIONS OF SECURITY IN COMPUTING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Modular Arithmetic: Integer Arithmetic - Integer Division

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 1 – Foundations of Security in Computing
## Topic: Modular Arithmetic — Integer Arithmetic & Integer Division

---

### 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

**Integer Division** is the foundational arithmetic operation upon which nearly all of modern number theory and cryptographic security (RSA, Diffie–Hellman, AES field arithmetic) is built. Formally, given two integers $a$ (the **dividend**) and $b$ (the **divisor**, with $b \neq 0$), integer division produces two well-defined integer outputs: a **quotient** $q$ and a **remainder** $r$.

The **Division Algorithm** (also called the *Euclidean Division Theorem*) states that for every integer $a$ and positive integer $b > 0$, there exist **unique integers** $q$ and $r$ such that:

$$a = q \cdot b + r, \quad \text{where } 0 \leq r < b$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 – PECAT595 Module 1):**
> Integer arithmetic forms the *axiomatic base* of modular arithmetic. The two operations — **div** (quotient) and **mod** (remainder) — are the primitive operators from which every higher-order cryptographic primitive is eventually constructed.

#### Conceptual Analogy — The "Leftover Pizza" Intuition

Imagine you ordered **17 pizzas** for a party, and each table can hold exactly **5 pizzas**.

- You fill **3 tables completely** → that's the **quotient** $q = 3$.
- You are left with **2 pizzas that don't fit on a full table** → that's the **remainder** $r = 2$.
- Total: $17 = (3 \times 5) + 2$.

This is *exactly* what integer division does. It is the same logic a 12-hour clock uses: $17 \text{ o'clock} \equiv 5 \text{ o'clock}$ because $17 = (1 \times 12) + 5$, and we only "care" about the leftover 5.

#### The Two Primitive Operations

- **Integer Division (div):** $a \; \texttt{div} \; b = q$ — returns how many *whole* times $b$ fits into $a$.
- **Modulo (mod):** $a \; \texttt{mod} \; b = r$ — returns the *leftover* amount after the last full fit.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **In most programming languages (C, C++, Java, Python):**
> - `a / b` performs **integer division** when both operands are integers, returning $q$.
> - `a % b` (or `a mod b`) returns the remainder $r$.
> - **Python's modulo** behaves differently for negative numbers (floor-based), which is a frequent source of bugs in cryptographic code.

#### Why This Matters for Security

Every time a cryptographic algorithm processes a large prime, computes a modular inverse, or runs the **Extended Euclidean Algorithm**, it is secretly performing repeated integer divisions. RSA's decryption, for instance, requires computing $d = e^{-1} \pmod{\phi(n)}$ — a problem that *cannot be solved without integer division*.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

---

### 2.1 The Division Algorithm — Formal Statement

For any integer $a$ and any positive integer $b \neq 0$, there exist **unique** integers $q$ and $r$ such that:

$$a = q \cdot b + r, \quad 0 \leq r < b$$

**Uniqueness** is the key word here — given $a$ and $b$, there is *exactly one* valid pair $(q, r)$. This uniqueness is what makes modular arithmetic *deterministic*, a non-negotiable property for cryptographic systems.

### 2.2 Divisibility

We say $b$ **divides** $a$ (written $b \mid a$) if and only if the remainder is zero:

$$b \mid a \iff a \bmod b = 0 \iff \exists\, k \in \mathbb{Z} \text{ such that } a = k \cdot b$$

#### Core Properties of Divisibility (for $a, b, c \in \mathbb{Z}$)

- **Reflexivity:** $a \mid a$
- **Antisymmetry:** If $a \mid b$ and $b \mid a$, then $a = \pm b$
- **Transitivity:** If $a \mid b$ and $b \mid c$, then $a \mid c$
- **Linear Combination:** If $a \mid b$ and $a \mid c$, then $a \mid (mb + nc)$ for any integers $m, n$
- **Zero Divisibility:** $a \mid 0$ is always true (since $0 = 0 \cdot a$)
- **Non-zero Divisor:** $0 \mid a$ is true **only if** $a = 0$

> [!TIP]
> The **Linear Combination Property** is the single most-used divisibility fact in KTU exam problems on the Extended Euclidean Algorithm. Memorize it.

### 2.3 The Greatest Common Divisor (GCD)

The **GCD** of $a$ and $b$ is the largest positive integer that divides both:

$$\gcd(a, b) = \max\{d \in \mathbb{Z}^+ : d \mid a \text{ and } d \mid b\}$$

**Bézout's Identity** states that for any $a, b$ (not both zero), there exist integers $x$ and $y$ such that:

$$\gcd(a, b) = a \cdot x + b \cdot y$$

These coefficients $x$ and $y$ are called **Bézout coefficients**, and computing them is the *entire purpose* of the Extended Euclidean Algorithm.

### 2.4 The Euclidean Algorithm

The Euclidean Algorithm computes $\gcd(a, b)$ in $O(\log \min(a, b))$ time by repeatedly applying the division algorithm:

$$\gcd(a, b) = \gcd(b, a \bmod b) = \gcd(b, r_1) = \gcd(r_1, r_2) = \dots = \gcd(r_{n-1}, r_n) = r_n$$

The process terminates when a remainder of **0** is reached; the previous non-zero remainder is the GCD.

### 2.5 The Extended Euclidean Algorithm

This is the **cryptographically essential** version. It tracks Bézout coefficients $(x, y)$ at every step such that $r_i = a \cdot x_i + b \cdot y_i$ for all iterations. The final iteration gives:

$$x \cdot a + y \cdot b = \gcd(a, b)$$

When $\gcd(a, b) = 1$, the value of $x$ is the **modular multiplicative inverse** of $a$ modulo $b$:

$$a \cdot x \equiv 1 \pmod{b} \iff x = a^{-1} \bmod b$$

---

### 📋 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Identity / Formula | Mathematical Form | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Division Algorithm | $a = qb + r,\ 0 \leq r < b$ | Foundational identity |
| 2 | Quotient extraction | $q = a \;\texttt{div}\; b$ | Floor of real division |
| 3 | Remainder extraction | $r = a \bmod b$ | Modular residue |
| 4 | Divisibility | $b \mid a \iff a \bmod b = 0$ | Primality, factorization |
| 5 | Linear combination closure | $d \mid a,\ d \mid b \Rightarrow d \mid (ma + nb)$ | GCD proofs, Bézout |
| 6 | GCD recurrence | $\gcd(a, b) = \gcd(b, a \bmod b)$ | Euclidean Algorithm |
| 7 | Bézout's Identity | $\gcd(a, b) = ax + by$ for some $x, y \in \mathbb{Z}$ | Extended Euclidean |
| 8 | Modular inverse existence | $a^{-1} \bmod n$ exists iff $\gcd(a, n) = 1$ | RSA key generation |
| 9 | Inverse via EEA | $a^{-1} \equiv x \pmod{n}$ where $ax + ny = 1$ | Decryption exponent |
| 10 | LCM relation | $\text{lcm}(a, b) = \frac{\vert a \cdot b \vert}{\gcd(a, b)}$ | Modulus design |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:**
> - **RSA Cryptosystem:** Computing the private exponent $d$ requires solving $e \cdot d \equiv 1 \pmod{\phi(n)}$ via the Extended Euclidean Algorithm.
> - **Hash Functions & Checksums (CRC):** Use polynomial division (a direct generalization of integer division) to detect data corruption.
> - **Elliptic Curve Cryptography (ECC):** Field inversion is performed using EEA on the underlying prime field.
> - **Compiler Design:** Constant folding and strength-reduction optimizations compile repeated modular expressions using these identities.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

---

### 3.1 Worked Example — Plain Integer Division

**Problem:** Compute the quotient and remainder when $a = 274$ is divided by $b = 25$.

**Solution:**

We seek $q$ and $r$ such that $274 = q \cdot 25 + r$ with $0 \leq r < 25$.

$$q = \left\lfloor \frac{274}{25} \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor 10.96 \right\rfloor = 10$$

$$r = 274 - (10)(25) = 274 - 250 = 24$$

**Verification:** $274 = (10)(25) + 24$ and $0 \leq 24 < 25$ ✓

> **Divisor $b$ must always be positive for the standard form.** When $b < 0$, the inequality changes to $b < r \leq 0$.

---

### 3.2 Derivation — The Euclidean Algorithm (Full Trace)

**Problem:** Compute $\gcd(252, 198)$.

**Step 1 — Apply the division algorithm with $a = 252, b = 198$:**

$$252 = (1) \cdot 198 + 54 \quad \Rightarrow \quad r_1 = 54$$

So $\gcd(252, 198) = \gcd(198, 54)$.

**Step 2 — Apply again with $a = 198, b = 54$:**

$$198 = (3) \cdot 54 + 36 \quad \Rightarrow \quad r_2 = 36$$

So $\gcd(198, 54) = \gcd(54, 36)$.

**Step 3 — Continue:**

$$54 = (1) \cdot 36 + 18 \quad \Rightarrow \quad r_3 = 18$$

**Step 4 — Continue:**

$$36 = (2) \cdot 18 + 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad r_4 = 0$$

**Termination:** Remainder reached 0. The GCD is the **last non-zero remainder**:

$$\boxed{\gcd(252, 198) = 18}$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation — The Extended Euclidean Algorithm (Full Trace)

**Problem:** Find integers $x$ and $y$ such that $18 = 252x + 198y$.

We **work backwards** from the Euclidean trace above.

**Step A — From the last non-trivial equation $54 = (1) \cdot 36 + 18$:**

$$18 = 54 - 1 \cdot 36$$

**Step B — Substitute $36 = 198 - 3 \cdot 54$ (from Step 2 above):**

$$18 = 54 - 1 \cdot (198 - 3 \cdot 54) = 4 \cdot 54 - 1 \cdot 198$$

**Step C — Substitute $54 = 252 - 1 \cdot 198$ (from Step 1 above):**

$$18 = 4 \cdot (252 - 1 \cdot 198) - 1 \cdot 198 = 4 \cdot 252 - 5 \cdot 198$$

**Final result:**

$$\boxed{18 = (4)(252) + (-5)(198)}$$

So Bézout coefficients are $x = 4,\ y = -5$. **Verification:** $(4)(252) + (-5)(198) = 1008 - 990 = 18$ ✓

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Tabular EEA (Faster Method)

The **table method** is what examiners expect in KTU board papers. Set up a table with columns $(a, b)$ and track quotients, then work back.

**Same problem:** $\gcd(252, 198) = 18$.

| Step | $a$ | $b$ | $q = \lfloor a/b \rfloor$ | $r = a \bmod b$ |
|------|-----|-----|---------------------------|------------------|
| 1 | 252 | 198 | 1 | 54 |
| 2 | 198 | 54 | 3 | 36 |
| 3 | 54 | 36 | 1 | 18 |
| 4 | 36 | 18 | 2 | 0 |

**Back-substitution table (using $r_i = a_i - q_i \cdot b_i$):**

| Step | Expression for $r_i$ | As $252 x + 198 y$ |
|------|----------------------|---------------------|
| 3 | $r_3 = 54 - 1 \cdot 36$ | $1 \cdot 252 + (-1) \cdot 198$ |
| 2 | $r_2 = 198 - 3 \cdot 54$ | $(-3) \cdot 252 + 4 \cdot 198$ |
| 1 | $r_1 = 252 - 1 \cdot 198$ | $1 \cdot 252 + (-1) \cdot 198$ |

Now solve the system. The final answer $x = 4,\ y = -5$ is obtained by chaining the Bézout coefficients as derived in Section 3.3.

---

### 3.5 Worked Example — Computing a Modular Inverse

**Problem:** Compute $17^{-1} \bmod 23$.

**Step 1 — Verify invertibility via GCD:**

Using the Euclidean algorithm on $(23, 17)$:

$$23 = 1 \cdot 17 + 6$$
$$17 = 2 \cdot 6 + 5$$
$$6 = 1 \cdot 5 + 1$$
$$5 = 5 \cdot 1 + 0$$

So $\gcd(17, 23) = 1$ → the inverse **exists**.

**Step 2 — Back-substitute to find Bézout coefficients:**

$$1 = 6 - 1 \cdot 5$$

$$1 = 6 - 1 \cdot (17 - 2 \cdot 6) = 3 \cdot 6 - 1 \cdot 17$$

$$1 = 3 \cdot (23 - 1 \cdot 17) - 1 \cdot 17 = 3 \cdot 23 - 4 \cdot 17$$

So $1 = (-4) \cdot 17 + 3 \cdot 23$, which gives:

$$17 \cdot (-4) \equiv 1 \pmod{23}$$

**Step 3 — Reduce to the canonical positive residue:**

$$-4 \bmod 23 = 23 - 4 = 19$$

**Final answer:** $\boxed{17^{-1} \equiv 19 \pmod{23}}$

**Verification:** $17 \times 19 = 323 = (14)(23) + 1 = 322 + 1$ ✓

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

---

### 4.1 Flowchart — The Euclidean Algorithm

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Start: input a, b"] --> B["b equals 0 ?"]
    B -- Yes --> C["Return a as GCD"]
    B -- No --> D["Compute q = a div b"]
    D --> E["Compute r = a mod b"]
    E --> F["Set a := b"]
    F --> G["Set b := r"]
    G --> B
    C --> H["End: output GCD"]
```

---

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Integer Division Hardware/Software View

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Dividend a"] --> C["Integer Divider Unit"]
    B["Divisor b"] --> C
    C --> D["Quotient q = a div b"]
    C --> E["Remainder r = a mod b"]
    D --> F["Higher Level Operations"]
    E --> F
    F --> G["GCD Computation"]
    F --> H["Modular Inverse"]
    F --> I["RSA Key Generation"]
    F --> J["CRC Checksum"]
```

---

### 4.3 Sequence Flow — Extended Euclidean Algorithm

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1["Initialize: x1=1, y1=0, x2=0, y2=1"] --> P2["Loop: a mod b not equal 0"]
    P2 --> P3["q = a div b, r = a mod b"]
    P3 --> P4["Update: x = x1 - q*x2, y = y1 - q*y2"]
    P4 --> P5["Shift: a=b, b=r, x1=x2, y1=y2, x2=x, y2=y"]
    P5 --> P2
    P2 -- "b equals 0" --> P6["Output: gcd = a, inverse = x1 mod original_b"]
```

---

### 4.4 Conceptual Map — Integer Division in the Security Stack

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    L1["Integer Division (a = qb + r)"] --> L2["GCD via Euclidean Algorithm"]
    L1 --> L3["Modular Inverse via Extended EA"]
    L2 --> L4["Bezout Identity (ax + by = gcd)"]
    L3 --> L4
    L4 --> L5["RSA Public-Key Cryptosystem"]
    L4 --> L6["Diffie-Hellman Key Exchange"]
    L4 --> L7["Elliptic Curve Field Arithmetic"]
    L1 --> L8["CRC Error Detection"]
    L1 --> L9["Hash Function Mixing"]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📝 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**State and explain the Division Algorithm for integers. Is the pair $(q, r)$ unique? Justify with an example.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Statement (1 mark):** For any integer $a$ and positive integer $b > 0$, there exist unique integers $q$ (quotient) and $r$ (remainder) such that $a = qb + r$ with $0 \leq r < b$.
- **Uniqueness justification (1 mark):** If two pairs $(q_1, r_1)$ and $(q_2, r_2)$ both satisfied the conditions, their difference gives $(q_1 - q_2) b = r_2 - r_1$. Since $0 \leq r_1, r_2 < b$, the RHS lies in $(-b, b)$, forcing both sides to be 0, hence $q_1 = q_2$ and $r_1 = r_2$.
- **Example (1 mark):** For $a = 37, b = 5$: $37 = 7 \cdot 5 + 2$, so $q = 7, r = 2$.

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`
**Define the Greatest Common Divisor of two integers. State Bézout's Identity and explain its cryptographic significance.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **GCD Definition (1 mark):** $\gcd(a, b)$ is the largest positive integer $d$ that divides both $a$ and $b$, i.e., $d \mid a$ and $d \mid b$.
- **Bézout's Identity (1 mark):** There exist integers $x, y$ such that $\gcd(a, b) = ax + by$.
- **Cryptographic significance (1 mark):** When $\gcd(a, n) = 1$, the value of $x$ gives the **modular multiplicative inverse** of $a$ modulo $n$, which is essential for computing the RSA private key $d$ such that $e \cdot d \equiv 1 \pmod{\phi(n)}$.

---

## 📝 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper]`
**(a)** Apply the **Euclidean Algorithm** to compute $\gcd(161, 28)$. Show every step of the division process clearly. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Using the **Extended Euclidean Algorithm**, find integers $x$ and $y$ such that $1 = 161x + 28y$. Hence determine the modular inverse of $161 \bmod 28$, if it exists. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Euclidean Algorithm (7 marks):**

**Step 1:** $161 = 5 \cdot 28 + 21$ (since $5 \times 28 = 140$ and $161 - 140 = 21$)
**Step 2:** $28 = 1 \cdot 21 + 7$ (since $28 - 21 = 7$)
**Step 3:** $21 = 3 \cdot 7 + 0$ (since $3 \times 7 = 21$ exactly)

Remainder reached zero. The last non-zero remainder is $7$.

$$\boxed{\gcd(161, 28) = 7} \quad \text{(2 marks for termination logic, 5 marks for the trace)}$$

**(b) Extended Euclidean Algorithm (7 marks):**

We back-substitute starting from $7 = 28 - 1 \cdot 21$.

**Step A:** $7 = 28 - 1 \cdot 21$
**Step B:** Substitute $21 = 161 - 5 \cdot 28$:

$$7 = 28 - 1 \cdot (161 - 5 \cdot 28) = 6 \cdot 28 - 1 \cdot 161$$

So:

$$1 = \frac{7}{7} = \frac{6 \cdot 28 - 1 \cdot 161}{7} = \left(\frac{6}{7}\right) \cdot 28 - \left(\frac{1}{7}\right) \cdot 161$$

Since the LHS must be an integer combination, we need to scale. However, since $\gcd(161, 28) = 7 \neq 1$:

**Observation:** $\gcd(161, 28) = 7 \neq 1$, therefore the **modular inverse of $161 \bmod 28$ does not exist**. (3 marks for the inverse non-existence conclusion)

To express $7$ as a Bézout combination (4 marks for back-substitution):

$$7 = (-1) \cdot 161 + (6) \cdot 28$$

**Verification:** $(-1)(161) + (6)(28) = -161 + 168 = 7$ ✓

---

### Question B — `[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Supplementary]`
**(a)** Define the **Division Algorithm** and use it to compute the quotient and remainder when $a = -27$ is divided by $b = 5$. Apply the rule for negative dividends carefully. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Using the **Extended Euclidean Algorithm**, find the modular inverse of $19 \pmod{71}$. Show all intermediate steps, and verify your answer with a direct computation. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Integer Division with Negative Dividend (7 marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):** For $a \in \mathbb{Z}$ and $b \in \mathbb{Z}^+$, there exist unique $q, r$ with $a = qb + r$ and $0 \leq r < b$. The *sign* of $a$ does not change the constraint on $r$.

**Computation for $a = -27, b = 5$ (5 marks):**

We need $-27 = q \cdot 5 + r$ with $0 \leq r < 5$.

Since $-27 < 0$, $q$ must be negative. We round $-27/5 = -5.4$ **toward $-\infty$** (i.e., floor), giving $q = -6$.

Check: $(-6)(5) = -30$, and $r = -27 - (-30) = 3$.

**Verification:** $-27 = (-6)(5) + 3$ and $0 \leq 3 < 5$ ✓

$$\boxed{q = -6, \quad r = 3}$$

> **Common Mistake:** Students often take $q = -5$ (truncation toward zero), giving $r = -2$, which **violates** $0 \leq r < 5$.

---

**(b) Modular Inverse of $19 \pmod{71}$ (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Euclidean Algorithm (2 marks):**

$$71 = 3 \cdot 19 + 14$$
$$19 = 1 \cdot 14 + 5$$
$$14 = 2 \cdot 5 + 4$$
$$5 = 1 \cdot 4 + 1$$
$$4 = 4 \cdot 1 + 0$$

So $\gcd(19, 71) = 1$ → inverse exists.

**Step 2 — Back-substitution (4 marks):**

$$1 = 5 - 1 \cdot 4$$
$$1 = 5 - 1 \cdot (14 - 2 \cdot 5) = 3 \cdot 5 - 1 \cdot 14$$
$$1 = 3 \cdot (19 - 1 \cdot 14) - 1 \cdot 14 = 3 \cdot 19 - 4 \cdot 14$$
$$1 = 3 \cdot 19 - 4 \cdot (71 - 3 \cdot 19) = 15 \cdot 19 - 4 \cdot 71$$

So $1 = (15)(19) + (-4)(71)$, meaning $19 \cdot 15 \equiv 1 \pmod{71}$.

**Step 3 — Verification (1 mark):**

$$19 \times 15 = 285 = 4 \cdot 71 + 1 = 284 + 1 \checkmark$$

$$\boxed{19^{-1} \equiv 15 \pmod{71}}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🔴 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Sign Error in Negative Division:** When the dividend is negative, students often use truncation instead of floor division. KTU evaluators deduct **2 full marks** if the remainder does not satisfy $0 \leq r < b$.
> 2. **Forgetting the Modular Reduction:** Reporting $x = -4$ as the inverse without reducing to a positive residue $19$ (mod 23) loses **1 mark**.
> 3. **Skipping the GCD Check:** Jumping directly to back-substitution without first verifying $\gcd(a, b) = 1$ costs the "existence of inverse" step (**1 mark**).
> 4. **Arithmetic Slip in Long EEA Traces:** Examiners allow a single arithmetic slip if the *method* is correct, but two or more errors will cost **2–3 marks**. Always verify your final Bézout identity by direct substitution.
> 5. **Confusing GCD with LCM:** $\gcd$ is *at most* $\min(a, b)$; $\text{lcm}$ is *at least* $\max(a, b)$. Mixing these up is a recurring board-paper error.

---

## 🎯 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Division Algorithm** $a = qb + r$ with $0 \leq r < b$ is the **foundational identity** of the entire module.
- ✅ **Uniqueness** of $(q, r)$ is what makes the operation *deterministic* and hence *cryptographically usable*.
- ✅ **Euclidean Algorithm** reduces GCD computation to $O(\log \min(a,b))$ time by replacing $(a, b)$ with $(b, a \bmod b)$ repeatedly.
- ✅ **Bézout's Identity** guarantees the existence of integers $x, y$ with $\gcd(a, b) = ax + by$.
- ✅ **Extended Euclidean Algorithm** is the **only practical method** to compute modular inverses, which are indispensable in RSA.
- ✅ **Modular inverse of $a \bmod n$ exists iff $\gcd(a, n) = 1$** — a direct consequence of Bézout's Identity.
- ✅ **Negative dividends** require **floor division** for $q$ to keep the remainder non-negative; this differs from C's truncation.
- ✅ **Linear combination property**: $d \mid a$ and $d \mid b$ $\Rightarrow$ $d \mid (ma + nb)$ is used in **every GCD proof**.
- ✅ Always **verify** the final Bézout identity by plugging $(x, y)$ back into $ax + by$.
- ✅ Always **reduce** the inverse to the canonical range $[0, n-1]$ before submitting your final answer.
- ✅ Connection to **cryptography**: RSA key generation, Diffie–Hellman, ECC field operations, and CRC polynomial division are all built directly on integer division.
- ✅ Connection to **theory**: $\gcd(a, b) = \gcd(b, a \bmod b)$ is the *invariant* that drives the Euclidean algorithm — a classic fixed-point reasoning pattern.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Divisibility
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECAT595 - FOUNDATIONS OF SECURITY IN COMPUTING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Divisibility

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In number theory — the mathematical bedrock of modern cryptography and computer security — **divisibility** is a binary relation defined over the set of integers. It is the gateway concept to all cryptographic primitives such as the **RSA algorithm**, the **Diffie–Hellman key exchange**, and **elliptic-curve cryptography**, all of which depend fundamentally on the behaviour of integer division.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (Divisibility):** Let $a$ and $b$ be integers with $a \neq 0$. We say that **$a$ divides $b$** (denoted $a \mid b$) if there exists an integer $c$ such that:
> $$b = a \cdot c$$
> The integer $a$ is called a **divisor** (or **factor**) of $b$, and $b$ is called a **multiple** of $a$. If no such integer $c$ exists, we write $a \nmid b$ and say that $a$ does **not** divide $b$.

**Key vocabulary used by KTU examiners:**
- $a \mid b$ → "$a$ is a divisor/factor of $b$"
- $b$ is a **multiple** of $a$
- $a$ and $b$ belong to the set of integers, i.e. $a, b \in \mathbb{Z}$
- The trivial divisors of any non-zero integer $b$ are always $\pm 1$ and $\pm b$

> [!NOTE]
> **Convention used in KTU textbooks:** The symbol $a \mid b$ must **never** be confused with the fraction $\dfrac{a}{b}$. The vertical bar denotes a *relation*, not an arithmetic operation.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you have **24 identical toffees** that you want to distribute equally among your friends, with no remainder and no broken toffee.

- If you have **3 friends**, you can give each $24 \div 3 = 8$ toffees. So $3 \mid 24$. ✅
- If you have **5 friends**, you cannot distribute them equally — 4 friends get 5, and 1 friend gets 4 (or vice-versa). So $5 \nmid 24$. ❌
- If you have **8 friends**, you can give each 3 toffees. So $8 \mid 24$. ✅

> [!TIP]
> **Real-world security linkage:** When you log into a banking website using a 2048-bit RSA key, the security of the entire transaction rests on the fact that multiplying two huge primes $p$ and $q$ is *trivial* for a computer, but **divisibility testing** — that is, recovering $p$ and $q$ from $n = p \cdot q$ — is computationally *infeasible*. This is the famous "factoring problem." Divisibility is therefore not just an abstract concept; it is the very foundation on which digital trust is built.

## 1.3 Standard Constants and Metrics

- **Zero Rule:** $a \mid 0$ for every non-zero integer $a$ (since $0 = a \cdot 0$).
- **Non-zero Rule:** $0 \mid b$ only when $b = 0$ (otherwise impossible because $b = 0 \cdot c = 0$).
- **Unit divisors:** $\pm 1$ divide every integer.
- **The set of divisors of $n$ is always finite** when $n \neq 0$, and its cardinality is denoted $\tau(n)$ (the *divisor function*).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Fundamental Properties of Divisibility

Let $a, b, c, m, n \in \mathbb{Z}$ with non-zero divisors where required. The following are the **board-exam essential** properties.

### Property 1 — Reflexivity
$$a \mid a$$
*Every non-zero integer divides itself (trivially, $a = a \cdot 1$).*

### Property 2 — Antisymmetry (with sign)
$$a \mid b \quad \text{and} \quad b \mid a \iff a = \pm b$$

### Property 3 — Transitivity
$$a \mid b \quad \text{and} \quad b \mid c \implies a \mid c$$
**Why it matters:** This property is what makes *prime factorisation* possible and is used in almost every cryptographic proof.

### Property 4 — Linear Combination Closure
$$a \mid b \quad \text{and} \quad a \mid c \implies a \mid (mb + nc) \quad \text{for all } m, n \in \mathbb{Z}$$
**Why it matters:** This is the cornerstone of the **Extended Euclidean Algorithm**, which is used to compute modular inverses — the very heart of RSA decryption.

### Property 5 — Divisibility of a Sum
$$a \mid b \quad \text{and} \quad a \mid c \implies a \mid (b + c)$$

### Property 6 — Divisibility of a Product
$$a \mid b \implies a \mid (bc) \quad \text{for all } c \in \mathbb{Z}$$
A particularly useful corollary: if $a \mid b$ and $a \mid c$, then $a^2 \mid bc$.

## 2.2 The Division Algorithm (Theorem)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Division Algorithm (Most Important Theorem in Module 1):**
> Given any integer $a$ (the *dividend*) and any positive integer $d$ (the *divisor*), there exist **unique** integers $q$ (the *quotient*) and $r$ (the *remainder*) such that:
> $$a = dq + r, \quad \text{where } 0 \le r < d$$

This algorithm is the **starting point** of the Euclidean algorithm, which in turn is the workhorse of public-key cryptography. KTU examiners frequently ask for a 7-mark proof or application of this theorem.

## 2.3 Greatest Common Divisor (GCD)

The **GCD** of two integers $a$ and $b$ (not both zero) is the largest positive integer that divides both. It is denoted $\gcd(a, b)$ or simply $(a, b)$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Bezout's Identity:** There exist integers $x$ and $y$ such that:
> $$\gcd(a, b) = ax + by$$
> The integers $x$ and $y$ are called **Bezout coefficients** and can be computed using the *Extended Euclidean Algorithm*. This identity is what makes RSA work — to compute the private key $d$, we solve $ed \equiv 1 \pmod{\phi(n)}$, which is precisely a Bezout-coefficient problem.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Rule | Statement | Exam Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $a \mid b$ definition | $\exists \, c \in \mathbb{Z} : b = ac$ | Definitions (3 marks) |
| 2 | Division Algorithm | $a = dq + r, \; 0 \le r < d$ | Proofs (7 marks) |
| 3 | Transitivity | $a \mid b, \; b \mid c \implies a \mid c$ | Lemma-based proofs |
| 4 | Linear Combo | $a \mid b, a \mid c \implies a \mid (mb+nc)$ | Bezout proofs |
| 5 | GCD via Euclidean | $\gcd(a,b) = \gcd(b, a \bmod b)$ | Algorithm traces |
| 6 | Bezout's Identity | $\gcd(a,b) = ax + by$ | RSA key generation |
| 7 | LCM–GCD Relation | $\operatorname{lcm}(a,b) = \dfrac{\vert ab \vert}{\gcd(a,b)}$ | Numerical problems |
| 8 | Prime Factorisation | $n = p_1^{e_1} p_2^{e_2} \cdots p_k^{e_k}$ | $\tau(n), \sigma(n)$ |
| 9 | Divisor Count | $\tau(n) = (e_1+1)(e_2+1)\cdots(e_k+1)$ | Counting problems |
| 10 | Modular Inverse | $a^{-1} \equiv b \pmod{m} \iff ab \equiv 1 \pmod{m}$ | RSA / DH |

> [!TIP]
> **Engineer's utility:** This exact set of rules is encoded in software libraries like **OpenSSL**, **libsodium**, and **Python's `gmpy2`**. Every time your browser establishes an HTTPS connection, the handshake negotiates prime numbers and uses these divisibility rules — at gigabit speeds — to derive a shared secret.

## 2.5 The Euclidean Algorithm

The Euclidean algorithm computes $\gcd(a, b)$ by repeated application of the Division Algorithm:

**Step 1:** $a = bq_1 + r_1$, where $0 \le r_1 < b$
**Step 2:** $b = r_1 q_2 + r_2$, where $0 \le r_2 < r_1$
**Step 3:** $r_1 = r_2 q_3 + r_3$, where $0 \le r_3 < r_2$
$$\vdots$$
**Step $n$:** $r_{n-2} = r_{n-1} q_n + r_n$, where $r_n = 0$

Then $\gcd(a, b) = r_{n-1}$ — the **last non-zero remainder**.

The **Extended Euclidean Algorithm** additionally back-substitutes to find integers $x$ and $y$ such that $ax + by = \gcd(a, b)$, which is essential in cryptography.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Applying the Division Algorithm

**Problem:** Divide $a = 247$ by $d = 13$. Find the quotient and remainder, and verify.

### Step-by-step derivation

We need to find $q$ and $r$ such that $247 = 13q + r$ and $0 \le r < 13$.

**Step 1:** Estimate the quotient.
$13 \times 10 = 130$, $13 \times 20 = 260 > 247$, so the quotient lies between 10 and 20.

**Step 2:** Try $q = 19$.
$13 \times 19 = 13 \times (20 - 1) = 260 - 13 = 247$.

**Step 3:** Compute the remainder.
$r = 247 - 247 = 0$.

**Step 4:** Verify the bounds.
$0 \le 0 < 13$ ✅

**Step 5:** Final answer.
$$247 = 13 \times 19 + 0 \implies q = 19, \; r = 0, \; \text{and} \; 13 \mid 247$$

This tells us 247 is a multiple of 13 (and hence of its prime factor 13). A direct cryptographic application: $n = 247$ would be a **terrible** RSA modulus because it factors trivially as $13 \times 19$.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Euclidean Algorithm in Action

**Problem:** Compute $\gcd(252, 198)$.

### Step-by-step derivation

**Step 1:** Apply the Division Algorithm with $a = 252, \, d = 198$.
$$252 = 198 \times 1 + 54 \quad (198 \times 1 = 198, \; 252 - 198 = 54)$$
So $r_1 = 54$.

**Step 2:** Take $a = 198, \, d = 54$.
$$198 = 54 \times 3 + 36 \quad (54 \times 3 = 162, \; 198 - 162 = 36)$$
So $r_2 = 36$.

**Step 3:** Take $a = 54, \, d = 36$.
$$54 = 36 \times 1 + 18 \quad (36 \times 1 = 36, \; 54 - 36 = 18)$$
So $r_3 = 18$.

**Step 4:** Take $a = 36, \, d = 18$.
$$36 = 18 \times 2 + 0 \quad (18 \times 2 = 36, \; 36 - 36 = 0)$$
So $r_4 = 0$.

**Step 5:** Identify the GCD.

The last non-zero remainder is $r_3 = 18$. Therefore:
$$\gcd(252, 198) = 18$$

**Step 6:** Verification using the formula $\gcd(a, b) = \gcd(b, a \bmod b)$ chain.
$$\gcd(252, 198) = \gcd(198, 54) = \gcd(54, 36) = \gcd(36, 18) = \gcd(18, 0) = 18 \;\checkmark$$

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Extended Euclidean Algorithm & Bezout's Identity

**Problem:** Find integers $x$ and $y$ such that $\gcd(252, 198) = 252x + 198y$.

### Step-by-step derivation (back-substitution)

**Step 1:** Reuse the Euclidean steps from §3.2.
$$252 = 198 \times 1 + 54 \quad \Rightarrow \quad 54 = 252 - 198 \times 1$$
$$198 = 54 \times 3 + 36 \quad \Rightarrow \quad 36 = 198 - 54 \times 3$$
$$54 = 36 \times 1 + 18 \quad \Rightarrow \quad 18 = 54 - 36 \times 1$$

**Step 2:** Substitute the expression for 36 into the expression for 18.
$$18 = 54 - 36 \times 1$$
$$18 = 54 - (198 - 54 \times 3) \times 1$$
$$18 = 54 - 198 + 54 \times 3$$
$$18 = 54 \times 4 - 198 \times 1$$

**Step 3:** Substitute the expression for 54.
$$18 = (252 - 198 \times 1) \times 4 - 198 \times 1$$
$$18 = 252 \times 4 - 198 \times 4 - 198 \times 1$$
$$18 = 252 \times 4 - 198 \times 5$$

**Step 4:** Identify Bezout coefficients.
$$18 = 252x + 198y \implies x = 4, \; y = -5$$

**Step 5:** Verify.
$$252 \times 4 + 198 \times (-5) = 1008 - 990 = 18 \;\checkmark$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Cryptographic context:** In RSA, the equation $ed \equiv 1 \pmod{\phi(n)}$ is rewritten as $ed - k\phi(n) = 1$, which is a Bezout equation. The Extended Euclidean Algorithm solves it in $O(\log n)$ time — a critical performance property for secure key generation.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — When to Use Each Tool

| Problem Type | Recommended Tool | Typical Marks |
|---|---|---|
| Show that $a \mid b$ for a given pair | Direct substitution with an explicit integer $c$ | 3 |
| Prove a divisibility statement | Use transitivity + linear combination closure | 7 |
| Compute $\gcd(a, b)$ | Euclidean algorithm | 3 to 7 |
| Find modular inverse | Extended Euclidean algorithm | 7 to 14 |
| Count divisors of $n$ | Prime factorisation + $\tau(n)$ formula | 3 |
| Prove $n$ is prime | Trial division up to $\sqrt{n}$ | 3 |
| Express $\gcd$ as linear combo | Back-substitution in Euclidean | 7 |

## 3.5 Proof Sketch — Uniqueness of the Division Algorithm

**Claim:** If $a = dq_1 + r_1 = dq_2 + r_2$ with $0 \le r_1, r_2 < d$, then $q_1 = q_2$ and $r_1 = r_2$.

**Proof:** Subtract the two expressions:
$$dq_1 + r_1 = dq_2 + r_2 \implies d(q_1 - q_2) = r_2 - r_1$$
The right-hand side is bounded:
$$-(d-1) \le r_2 - r_1 \le d-1$$
The left-hand side is a multiple of $d$. The only multiple of $d$ in the interval $[-(d-1), d-1]$ is **zero**. Hence:
$$d(q_1 - q_2) = 0 \implies q_1 = q_2 \quad (\text{since } d \neq 0)$$
Substituting back: $r_1 = r_2$. $\blacksquare$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Concept Map — Divisibility and Its Cryptographic Descendants

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["Divisibility a divides b"] --> nodeB["Division Algorithm a = dq + r"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["Euclidean Algorithm gcd a b"]
    nodeC --> nodeD["Extended Euclidean Algorithm Bezout Coefficients"]
    nodeD --> nodeE["Modular Inverse a^-1 mod m"]
    nodeB --> nodeF["Prime Factorisation n = p1^e1 ... pk^ek"]
    nodeF --> nodeG["Euler Totient phi of n"]
    nodeG --> nodeH["RSA Key Generation"]
    nodeE --> nodeH
    nodeH --> nodeI["Digital Signatures HTTPS Secure Banking"]
    nodeG --> nodeJ["Diffie Hellman Key Exchange"]
    nodeJ --> nodeI
    nodeF --> nodeK["Primality Testing Miller Rabin"]
    nodeK --> nodeH
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Every arrow here represents a *theoretical dependency*. If a student does not master divisibility and the Division Algorithm at the root, the entire security stack (RSA, Diffie–Hellman, HTTPS) becomes shaky. This is precisely why KTU places this topic in **Module 1**.

## 4.2 Flowchart — Executing the Euclidean Algorithm

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start(["Input two positive integers a and b"]) --> stepA["Ensure a is greater than b"]
    stepA --> stepB["Compute q = floor of a divided by b"]
    stepB --> stepC["Compute r = a minus b times q"]
    stepC --> decisionC{"Is r equal to 0"}
    decisionC -- Yes --> stepD["Output b as the GCD"]
    decisionC -- No --> stepE["Set a = b and b = r"]
    stepE --> stepB
    stepD --> finish(["Stop algorithm"])
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Divisibility Subsystem in Cryptography

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph USER["User Side"]
        uiA["Plaintext Message M"]
        uiB["Public Key e, n"]
    end
    subgraph CRYPTO["Cryptographic Engine"]
        cA["Modular Exponentiation C = M^e mod n"]
        cB["Divisibility and Primality Library GMP / OpenSSL"]
    end
    subgraph SEC["Security Layer"]
        sA["Encrypted Ciphertext C transmitted"]
        sB["Receiver applies private key d = e^-1 mod phi of n"]
    end
    uiA --> cA
    uiB --> cA
    cA --> cB
    cB --> sA
    sA --> sB
    sB --> sC["Decrypted Plaintext recovered"]
```

> [!TIP]
> **How to read this block diagram:** The "Divisibility & Primality Library" sits at the **center** of the cryptographic engine. Every RSA operation — key generation, encryption, decryption — funnels through it. The Euclidean Algorithm (which we derived in §3.2 and §3.3) is the most-called sub-routine in this block.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question Paper]`
**State and prove the Division Algorithm.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Statement:** For any integer $a$ and any positive integer $d$, there exist unique integers $q$ and $r$ such that $a = dq + r$ with $0 \le r < d$.
>
> **Existence proof sketch:** Consider the set $S = \{a - dk : k \in \mathbb{Z}\}$. By the Well-Ordering Principle, $S$ contains a non-negative least element. Call it $r = a - dq$ for some $q$. Then $a = dq + r$ and $0 \le r < d$ (if $r \ge d$, then $r - d$ would be a smaller non-negative element of $S$, contradiction).
>
> **Uniqueness proof:** Assume $a = dq_1 + r_1 = dq_2 + r_2$ with $0 \le r_1, r_2 < d$. Then $d(q_1 - q_2) = r_2 - r_1$. The right side lies in $[-(d-1), d-1]$, so it must be zero. Hence $q_1 = q_2$ and $r_1 = r_2$. $\blacksquare$

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – July 2023, Supplementary]`
**Define GCD. Using the Euclidean algorithm, find $\gcd(465, 255)$.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Definition:** The greatest common divisor of two integers $a$ and $b$, not both zero, is the largest positive integer that divides both. It is denoted $\gcd(a, b)$.
>
> **Computation:**
>
> $465 = 255 \times 1 + 210$
> $255 = 210 \times 1 + 45$
> $210 = 45 \times 4 + 30$
> $45 = 30 \times 1 + 15$
> $30 = 15 \times 2 + 0$
>
> The last non-zero remainder is $15$. Therefore $\gcd(465, 255) = 15$.

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice Provided)

### Question A (Choice 1) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Main]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Prove that if $a \mid b$ and $a \mid c$, then $a \mid (mb + nc)$ for all integers $m$ and $n$. Hence show that $\gcd(a, b)$ can be expressed as a linear combination of $a$ and $b$.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** Since $a \mid b$, there exists an integer $k_1$ such that $b = a k_1$.

**Step 2:** Since $a \mid c$, there exists an integer $k_2$ such that $c = a k_2$.

**Step 3:** Form the linear combination $mb + nc$:
$$mb + nc = m(a k_1) + n(a k_2) = a(m k_1 + n k_2)$$

**Step 4:** Since $m k_1 + n k_2$ is an integer, the right-hand side is of the form $a \cdot (\text{integer})$, proving $a \mid (mb + nc)$.

**Step 5 — Application to GCD:** Let $d = \gcd(a, b)$. Then $d \mid a$ and $d \mid b$. By the lemma we just proved, $d$ divides every linear combination $ma + nb$. In particular, $d$ divides the *smallest positive* such combination, which by definition equals $\gcd(a, b)$. Hence $\gcd(a, b)$ is expressible as $ax + by$ for some integers $x, y$. This is **Bezout's Identity**. $\blacksquare$

**(b) [7 Marks]** Using the Extended Euclidean Algorithm, find integers $x$ and $y$ such that $\gcd(161, 28) = 161x + 28y$. Hence find the modular inverse of $28$ modulo $161$.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Euclidean chain:**
$$161 = 28 \times 5 + 21 \quad (28 \times 5 = 140, \; 161 - 140 = 21)$$
$$28 = 21 \times 1 + 7 \quad (21 \times 1 = 21, \; 28 - 21 = 7)$$
$$21 = 7 \times 3 + 0$$

Therefore $\gcd(161, 28) = 7$. **Note:** $\gcd \ne 1$, so a true modular inverse of $28 \pmod{161}$ does not exist. However, we can still compute the Bezout coefficients as a demonstration.

**Step 2 — Back-substitution:**
From the second line: $7 = 28 - 21 \times 1$
From the first line: $21 = 161 - 28 \times 5$
Substituting: $7 = 28 - (161 - 28 \times 5) = 28 \times 6 - 161 \times 1$

**Step 3 — Identify coefficients:**
$$x = -1, \quad y = 6$$
$$161 \times (-1) + 28 \times 6 = -161 + 168 = 7 \;\checkmark$$

**Step 4 — Modular inverse discussion:**
The modular inverse of $28$ modulo $161$ exists only if $\gcd(28, 161) = 1$. Since $\gcd = 7 \neq 1$, **no inverse exists**. This illustrates a critical security principle: RSA key generation must choose $e$ such that $\gcd(e, \phi(n)) = 1$, otherwise decryption will fail.

---

### Question B (Choice 2) `[KTU University Exam – July 2024, Main, Modified]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** State the fundamental properties of divisibility. Prove the transitivity property: if $a \mid b$ and $b \mid c$, then $a \mid c$.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** Since $a \mid b$, there exists an integer $k$ such that $b = ak$.

**Step 2:** Since $b \mid c$, there exists an integer $m$ such that $c = bm$.

**Step 3:** Substitute (1) into (2):
$$c = bm = (ak) m = a(km)$$

**Step 4:** Since $km$ is an integer, we have $c = a \cdot (\text{integer})$, which is precisely the definition of $a \mid c$. $\blacksquare$

**Real-world security relevance:** This transitivity property is exploited in the construction of **certificate chains** in PKI (Public Key Infrastructure). A root CA's signature on an intermediate CA's certificate, combined with the intermediate CA's signature on a user's certificate, transitively vouches for the user's certificate. The same transitivity underlies **Merkle trees** used in blockchain.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Find the prime factorisation of $360$ and hence compute (i) the number of divisors $\tau(360)$ and (ii) the sum of divisors $\sigma(360)$.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Prime factorisation:**
$$360 = 2^3 \times 3^2 \times 5^1$$
**Verification:** $2^3 = 8$, $3^2 = 9$, $5^1 = 5$, and $8 \times 9 \times 5 = 360$. ✓

**Step 2 — Number of divisors:**
Using the formula $\tau(n) = (e_1 + 1)(e_2 + 1)\cdots(e_k + 1)$:
$$\tau(360) = (3+1)(2+1)(1+1) = 4 \times 3 \times 2 = 24$$

**Step 3 — Sum of divisors:**
Using the multiplicative formula:
$$\sigma(p^e) = \frac{p^{e+1} - 1}{p - 1}$$

For each prime power:
$$\sigma(2^3) = \frac{2^4 - 1}{2 - 1} = \frac{15}{1} = 15$$
$$\sigma(3^2) = \frac{3^3 - 1}{3 - 1} = \frac{26}{2} = 13$$
$$\sigma(5^1) = \frac{5^2 - 1}{5 - 1} = \frac{24}{4} = 6$$

**Step 4 — Combine (multiplicativity):**
$$\sigma(360) = 15 \times 13 \times 6 = 1170$$

**Step 5 — Final answer:** $\tau(360) = 24$ divisors and $\sigma(360) = 1170$.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Sign error in Bezout coefficients** — Students frequently get the sign of $x$ or $y$ wrong during back-substitution. Always verify by substituting back into $ax + by = \gcd(a, b)$ before submitting.
> 2. **Forgetting the uniqueness part of the Division Algorithm** — A 7-mark question asking for the Division Algorithm *requires both* existence and uniqueness proofs. Mentioning only existence will cost you a minimum of 3 marks.
> 3. **Confusing $\tau(n)$ with $\sigma(n)$** — $\tau(n)$ counts divisors; $\sigma(n)$ sums them. The KTU valuation key explicitly tests this distinction.
> 4. **Missing the "modular inverse exists only if $\gcd = 1$" condition** — This is the most heavily penalised mistake in any RSA-related question. Always state the invertibility condition.
> 5. **Division by zero** — Never write $\gcd(0, 0)$ — it is **undefined** by convention. KTU examiners will deduct 1 mark for every such oversight.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Must Memorise Before Exam**
>
> - **Definition:** $a \mid b \iff \exists \, c \in \mathbb{Z} : b = ac$. Always remember the symbol $a \mid b$ is a *relation*, not a fraction.
> - **Six core properties of divisibility:** Reflexivity, Antisymmetry, Transitivity, Linear-Combination Closure, Sum Closure, Product Closure.
> - **Division Algorithm:** $a = dq + r$ with $0 \le r < d$. The remainder $r$ is always **strictly less** than the divisor $d$.
> - **Euclidean Algorithm:** Repeatedly replace $(a, b)$ with $(b, a \bmod b)$ until remainder is $0$. The last non-zero remainder is the GCD.
> - **Extended Euclidean Algorithm:** Back-substitute to find $x, y$ such that $ax + by = \gcd(a, b)$. This solves $a^{-1} \pmod{m}$ when $\gcd(a, m) = 1$.
> - **Bezout's Identity:** $\gcd(a, b) = ax + by$ for some integers $x, y$. Foundation of RSA.
> - **LCM-GCD relation:** $\operatorname{lcm}(a, b) \cdot \gcd(a, b) = \vert ab \vert$.
> - **Prime factorisation formula:** $\tau(n) = \prod (e_i + 1)$ and $\sigma(n) = \prod \dfrac{p_i^{e_i+1} - 1}{p_i - 1}$.
> - **Invertibility condition:** $a$ has a modular inverse modulo $m$ **iff** $\gcd(a, m) = 1$.
> - **Special cases to remember:** $a \mid 0$ always; $0 \mid b$ only when $b = 0$; $\pm 1$ divide every integer.
> - **Cryptographic hook:** Every RSA private key is a Bezout coefficient, every Diffie–Hellman exchange uses modular exponentiation over prime-order groups, every digital signature relies on the difficulty of factoring — all of which is rooted in the divisibility properties you have just studied.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Greatest Common Divisor (GCD)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECAT595 - FOUNDATIONS OF SECURITY IN COMPUTING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Greatest Common Divisor (GCD)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Greatest Common Divisor (GCD)**: For two non-zero integers $a$ and $b$, the **Greatest Common Divisor**, denoted $\gcd(a, b)$ or simply $\gcd(a,b)$, is defined as the **largest positive integer $d$** that divides both $a$ and $b$ without leaving a remainder.

In formal number-theoretic notation:

$$\gcd(a, b) = \max \{ d \in \mathbb{Z}^{+} \mid d \mid a \ \text{and} \ d \mid b \}$$

Where $d \mid a$ means "$d$ divides $a$ evenly" (i.e., $a = kd$ for some integer $k$).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Tile-Fitting Analogy**: Imagine you have two rectangular rooms of dimensions **12 m × 18 m** and **18 m × 27 m** that you want to tile with **square tiles of maximum size** so that no tile is cut and no gaps are left. The GCD is exactly the *largest possible side length* of such a tile.
> - For 12 and 18 → GCD = **6** (a 6×6 tile works perfectly in both)
> - Common divisors of 12: {1, 2, 3, 4, 6, 12}
> - Common divisors of 18: {1, 2, 3, 6, 9, 18}
> - Largest common = **6**

In **computer security**, the GCD is the silent workhorse behind **public-key cryptography** (like RSA). When we say "two numbers are *coprime*" (their GCD = 1), we are essentially saying they share no common factor — a foundational requirement for generating cryptographic keys.

## 1.3 Standard Notations and Special Cases

| Symbol / Term | Meaning | Security Relevance |
|:---:|:---|:---|
| $\gcd(a, b)$ | Greatest common divisor of $a$ and $b$ | Key-pair validity check |
| $d \mid a$ | $d$ divides $a$ exactly | Divisibility primitive |
| $\gcd(a, 0)$ | $= \vert a \vert$ | Identity element of GCD |
| $\gcd(a, b) = 1$ | $a$ and $b$ are **coprime** (relatively prime) | **Required for RSA modulus inversion** |

## 1.4 Prime Factorization Perspective (Foundational View)

Any positive integer $n$ can be uniquely expressed as:

$$n = p_1^{e_1} \cdot p_2^{e_2} \cdot p_3^{e_3} \cdots p_k^{e_k}$$

For two numbers $a$ and $b$:

$$\gcd(a, b) = \prod_{i=1}^{k} p_i^{\min(e_i^{(a)}, \ e_i^{(b)})}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in Security**: The entire hardness of **RSA** relies on the fact that computing GCD is *easy* (Euclidean algorithm in polynomial time), but recovering the prime factorization — which would break RSA — is *computationally hard* for large numbers.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Fundamental Properties of GCD (The "Rule-Book")

1. **Commutativity**: $\gcd(a, b) = \gcd(b, a)$
2. **Identity Property**: $\gcd(a, 0) = \vert a \vert$
3. **Divisibility Rule**: If $a \mid b$, then $\gcd(a, b) = \vert a \vert$
4. **Recurrence (Euclidean Core)**: $\gcd(a, b) = \gcd(b, a \bmod b)$
5. **Multiplicative Property**: $\gcd(ka, kb) = k \cdot \gcd(a, b)$ for any $k > 0$
6. **Linearity**: $\gcd(a, b) = \gcd(a + b, b) = \gcd(a, b - a)$
7. **Coprime Criterion**: $\gcd(a, b) = 1 \iff \exists \ x, y \in \mathbb{Z}$ such that $ax + by = 1$

## 2.2 Bézout's Identity (The Bridge to Cryptography)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Bézout's Identity (Critical for KTU)**: For any integers $a$ and $b$ (not both zero), there exist integers $x$ and $y$ such that:
> $$ax + by = \gcd(a, b)$$
> These coefficients $x$ and $y$ are called **Bézout coefficients** or the **extended GCD**.

In RSA, we use this in *modular inverse* form: finding $x$ such that $ax \equiv 1 \pmod{b}$ is exactly solving Bézout's identity when $\gcd(a, b) = 1$.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Property | Expression | Used For |
|:--:|:---|:---|:---|
| 1 | Euclidean Recurrence | $\gcd(a, b) = \gcd(b, a \bmod b)$ | Reducing problem size |
| 2 | Bézout's Identity | $ax + by = \gcd(a, b)$ | Modular inverse (RSA) |
| 3 | Modular Inverse Condition | $a^{-1} \bmod \ n$ exists $\iff \gcd(a, n) = 1$ | Cryptographic key validation |
| 4 | Prime Factorization GCD | $\gcd(a, b) = \prod p_i^{\min(e_i, f_i)}$ | Theoretical computation |
| 5 | Identity | $\gcd(a, 0) = \vert a \vert$ | Base case of Euclidean |
| 6 | Multiplicative | $\gcd(ka, kb) = k \cdot \gcd(a, b)$ | Scaling arguments |
| 7 | Coprime Defn | $\gcd(a, b) = 1 \Leftrightarrow$ coprime | RSA primality context |
| 8 | Ternary Distributive | $\gcd(a, \gcd(b, c)) = \gcd(\gcd(a, b), c)$ | Associativity |

## 2.4 Why and How — The Logical Flow

**Why does the Euclidean Algorithm work?**
Because any common divisor of $a$ and $b$ must also divide $a - qb$ for any integer $q$ (since $a - qb$ is a linear combination). So the set of common divisors of $(a, b)$ equals the set of common divisors of $(b, a \bmod b)$. Hence the GCD is preserved.

**How is this used in real engineering?**
- **RSA Cryptosystem**: Choose $e$ such that $\gcd(e, \phi(n)) = 1$, then compute $d = e^{-1} \bmod \phi(n)$ via **Extended Euclidean Algorithm**.
- **Hashing / Checksums**: Modular arithmetic on GCD-based polynomials.
- **Network Routing**: Computing common cycles in graph protocols.
- **Error-Correcting Codes**: GCD-based polynomial rings in Reed–Solomon codes.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example — Classical Euclidean Algorithm

**Problem**: Compute $\gcd(252, 198)$.

We apply the recurrence $\gcd(a, b) = \gcd(b, a \bmod b)$ repeatedly.

**Step 1**: Divide 252 by 198.

$$252 = 1 \times 198 + 54$$

So $252 \bmod 198 = 54$. Hence $\gcd(252, 198) = \gcd(198, 54)$.

**Step 2**: Divide 198 by 54.

$$198 = 3 \times 54 + 36$$

So $198 \bmod 54 = 36$. Hence $\gcd(198, 54) = \gcd(54, 36)$.

**Step 3**: Divide 54 by 36.

$$54 = 1 \times 36 + 18$$

So $54 \bmod 36 = 18$. Hence $\gcd(54, 36) = \gcd(36, 18)$.

**Step 4**: Divide 36 by 18.

$$36 = 2 \times 18 + 0$$

So $36 \bmod 18 = 0$. The base case is reached.

**Conclusion**:
$$\gcd(252, 198) = 18$$

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Tabulated Euclidean Steps

| Step | Dividend $a$ | Divisor $b$ | Quotient $q$ | Remainder $r$ | Result |
|:--:|:--:|:--:|:--:|:--:|:--:|
| 1 | 252 | 198 | 1 | 54 | $\gcd(198, 54)$ |
| 2 | 198 | 54 | 3 | 36 | $\gcd(54, 36)$ |
| 3 | 54 | 36 | 1 | 18 | $\gcd(36, 18)$ |
| 4 | 36 | 18 | 2 | 0 | $\gcd(18, 0) = 18$ |

## 3.3 Extended Euclidean Algorithm — Full Derivation

**Problem**: Find $x, y$ such that $252x + 198y = \gcd(252, 198) = 18$.

**Step 1 — Rewrite the Euclidean steps backward**:

From Step 1: $54 = 252 - 1 \times 198$

From Step 2: $36 = 198 - 3 \times 54$

From Step 3: $18 = 54 - 1 \times 36$

**Step 2 — Back-substitute Step 3 into Step 2**:

$$18 = 54 - 1 \times (198 - 3 \times 54)$$

$$18 = 54 - 198 + 3 \times 54 = 4 \times 54 - 198$$

**Step 3 — Back-substitute Step 1**:

$$18 = 4 \times (252 - 198) - 198$$

$$18 = 4 \times 252 - 4 \times 198 - 198$$

$$18 = 4 \times 252 - 5 \times 198$$

**Step 4 — Read off the Bézout coefficients**:

$$x = 4, \quad y = -5$$

**Verification**:

$$4 \times 252 + (-5) \times 198 = 1008 - 990 = 18 \ \checkmark$$

## 3.4 Generic Proof that $\gcd(a, b) = \gcd(b, a \bmod b)$

Let $a = qb + r$ where $0 \le r < b$ and $r = a \bmod b$.

We want to show: $\gcd(a, b) = \gcd(b, r)$.

**Proof**:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Let } d &= \gcd(a, b). \text{ Then } d \mid a \text{ and } d \mid b. \\
\text{Since } r &= a - qb, \text{ and } d \mid a, \ d \mid qb, \\
\text{we have } d &\mid (a - qb) = r. \\
\text{Hence } d \text{ is a common divisor of } b \text{ and } r. \quad &\Rightarrow d \le \gcd(b, r)
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Conversely, let } d' &= \gcd(b, r). \text{ Then } d' \mid b \text{ and } d' \mid r. \\
\text{Since } a &= qb + r, \text{ and } d' \mid qb, \ d' \mid r, \\
\text{we have } d' &\mid (qb + r) = a. \\
\text{Hence } d' \text{ is a common divisor of } a \text{ and } b. \quad &\Rightarrow d' \le \gcd(a, b)
\end{aligned}$$

Therefore $\gcd(a, b) = \gcd(b, r) = \gcd(b, a \bmod b)$. $\blacksquare$

## 3.5 Cryptographic Application — RSA Modular Inverse

**Problem**: Compute $d = 7^{-1} \bmod 187$ (i.e., find $d$ such that $7d \equiv 1 \pmod{187}$).

First, check that $\gcd(7, 187) = 1$ (7 is prime and does not divide 187). So inverse exists.

Apply Extended Euclidean on $(187, 7)$:

$$187 = 26 \times 7 + 5$$
$$7 = 1 \times 5 + 2$$
$$5 = 2 \times 2 + 1$$
$$2 = 2 \times 1 + 0$$

Back-substitute:

$$1 = 5 - 2 \times 2$$
$$1 = 5 - 2 \times (7 - 1 \times 5) = 3 \times 5 - 2 \times 7$$
$$1 = 3 \times (187 - 26 \times 7) - 2 \times 7 = 3 \times 187 - 80 \times 7$$

So $-80 \times 7 \equiv 1 \pmod{187}$, i.e., $7^{-1} \equiv -80 \equiv 107 \pmod{187}$.

**Answer**: $d = 107$.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Classical Euclidean Algorithm Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Start: Input a, b"] --> B{"Is b equal to 0?"}
    B -- Yes --> C["Return gcd = a"]
    B -- No --> D["Compute r = a mod b"]
    D --> E["Update: a becomes b"]
    E --> F["Update: b becomes r"]
    F --> B
    C --> G["End: Output GCD value"]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Extended Euclidean Algorithm (Traceback)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Start: Input a, b"] --> B["Apply Classical Euclidean"]
    B --> C["Record all quotients qi and remainders ri"]
    C --> D{"Is the last non-zero remainder reached?"}
    D -- No --> C
    D -- Yes --> E["Initialize: x1=1, y1=0 for a; x2=0, y2=1 for b"]
    E --> F["For each step in reverse: compute new x, y using x_new = x_prev2 - q * x_prev1"]
    F --> G["Obtain Bézout coefficients x, y such that ax + by = gcd"]
    G --> H["End: Output gcd, x, y"]
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture — GCD in a Cryptographic System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["User Inputs Plaintext M"] --> B["Encrypt Module"]
    C["Public Key e, Modulus n"] --> B
    B --> D["Ciphertext C = M^e mod n"]
    D --> E["Secure Channel / Storage"]
    E --> F["Decrypt Module"]
    G["Private Key d, Modulus n"] --> F
    H["Extended Euclidean Algorithm"] --> G
    H -->|"Requires gcd(e, phi_n) = 1"| G
    F --> I["Recovered Plaintext M = C^d mod n"]
```

## 4.4 Hierarchical Mapping — GCD Concept Layer

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Number Theory Foundations"] --> B["Divisibility Primitives"]
    A --> C["Prime Factorization"]
    A --> D["Modular Arithmetic"]
    B --> E["Greatest Common Divisor"]
    C --> E
    D --> E
    E --> F["Euclidean Algorithm"]
    E --> G["Extended Euclidean Algorithm"]
    F --> H["Coprimality Test"]
    G --> I["Bezout Coefficients x, y"]
    H --> J["RSA Key Generation"]
    I --> J
    J --> K["Secure Public Key Cryptosystem"]
```
