| $C = C_1 \Vert C_2 \Vert \ldots \Vert C_m$ | Ciphertext concatenation | Used in trace-based questions |
| $m = \lceil \vert P \vert / n \rceil$ | Number of blocks for plaintext of length $\vert P \vert$ bits | Padding & block-counting problems |
| $2^n$ | Total possible plaintext-ciphertext block pairs | Avalanche / brute-force questions |
| Padding: $\text{PKCS\#7}(r) = r \Vert r \Vert \ldots$ (r bytes) | Pad with *r* bytes, each of value *r* | Padding scheme questions |
| Error propagation: 1 bit-flip in $C_i$ | Affects **only** $P_i$ (full block garbled) | Distinguishing ECB from CBC |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Distinction for KTU:** When a board question asks "How does an error in one ciphertext block affect the decrypted plaintext?", the answer for ECB is: **only the corresponding plaintext block is affected** — typically the entire block is corrupted beyond recognition due to block-cipher diffusion. This is *opposite* to CBC, where one bit-error corrupts two blocks.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Despite its cryptographic weaknesses, ECB retains niche engineering applications:

- **Single-block messages:** Encrypting a fixed-length cryptographic key (e.g., a 128-bit AES key) for key-wrapping — where there is *only one block* and pattern leakage is irrelevant.
- **Randomized data:** If the plaintext is already high-entropy (e.g., a random session token), ECB provides confidentiality with minimal overhead.
- **Parallel hardware implementations:** Disk encryption accelerators, RAID controllers, and embedded systems with strict latency budgets may use ECB internally when paired with **tweakable block ciphers** (e.g., XTS-AES) to mitigate pattern leakage.
- **Benchmarking:** ECB serves as the performance baseline for measuring block-cipher throughput without mode-of-operation overhead.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Encryption Walkthrough on a Sample Plaintext

Consider a 32-bit block cipher (toy example) with $n = 8$ bits (one byte), and key *K*. Suppose the plaintext is:

$$P = \text{``SECURITY''} = \text{0x53 0x45 0x43 0x55 0x52 0x49 0x54 0x59}$$

This 64-bit message is partitioned into 8 blocks of 8 bits each:

$$P_1 = 0x53, \quad P_2 = 0x45, \quad P_3 = 0x43, \quad P_4 = 0x55$$
$$P_5 = 0x52, \quad P_6 = 0x49, \quad P_7 = 0x54, \quad P_8 = 0x59$$

**Step 1 — Block Isolation:** Each $P_i$ is independent. No block $P_j$ for $j \neq i$ participates in the encryption of $P_i$.

**Step 2 — Apply the Block Cipher:** Let the (hypothetical) block cipher $E_K$ produce the following ciphertext bytes:

| Block Index $i$ | Plaintext $P_i$ | Operation | Ciphertext $C_i$ |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 1 | 0x53 | $C_1 = E_K(0x53)$ | 0xA1 |
| 2 | 0x45 | $C_2 = E_K(0x45)$ | 0x7C |
| 3 | 0x43 | $C_3 = E_K(0x43)$ | 0x9B |
| 4 | 0x55 | $C_4 = E_K(0x55)$ | 0xD4 |
| 5 | 0x52 | $C_5 = E_K(0x52)$ | 0x88 |
| 6 | 0x49 | $C_6 = E_K(0x49)$ | 0xE2 |
| 7 | 0x54 | $C_7 = E_K(0x54)$ | 0x06 |
| 8 | 0x59 | $C_8 = E_K(0x59)$ | 0xF7 |

**Step 3 — Concatenation:** The final ciphertext is:

$$C = \text{0xA1 0x7C 0x9B 0xD4 0x88 0xE2 0x06 0xF7}$$

**Step 4 — Demonstrate the Pattern Leakage:** Now consider a second plaintext where block 1, 3, 5, 7 happen to be identical to the corresponding blocks of the first plaintext:

$$P' = \text{(0x53)} \text{ 0x33 0x43 0x11 0x52 0x77 0x54 0xAA}$$

Because ECB is deterministic, blocks 1, 3, 5, 7 will produce *exactly the same ciphertext bytes* 0xA1, 0x9B, 0x88, 0x06 — immediately revealing to an eavesdropper that "the same byte appeared at those positions in both messages." This is the **deterministic pattern leak**.

## 3.2 Mathematical Proof of Pattern Leakage

**Claim:** For any two plaintexts $P$ and $P'$ encrypted under the same key $K$ in ECB mode, if $P_i = P'_i$ for some index $i$, then $C_i = C'_i$.

**Proof by Direct Substitution:**

$$\text{Given:} \quad P_i = P'_i$$

$$\text{By ECB definition:} \quad C_i = E_K(P_i) \quad \text{and} \quad C'_i = E_K(P'_i)$$

$$\text{Substituting} \quad P'_i = P_i:$$

$$C'_i = E_K(P'_i) = E_K(P_i) = C_i$$

$$\therefore \quad C_i = C'_i \qquad \blacksquare$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Mark Allocation:** In 14-mark questions, the examiner expects the candidate to (1) state the claim, (2) write the two ECB equations, (3) perform the substitution, and (4) conclude with the symbol $\blacksquare$. Skipping any one step typically costs 2 marks.

## 3.3 Error Propagation Analysis

**Scenario:** A 1-bit transmission error corrupts $C_i$ to $C'_i$ at the receiver.

**Step 1 — Identify affected decryption:** Only the block $C_i$ is decrypted into a corrupted plaintext block. All other $C_j$ for $j \neq i$ are unaffected.

**Step 2 — Quantify the corruption within block *i*:** A 1-bit error in $C_i$, when decrypted, produces a plaintext block $P'_i$ that is — on average — **completely garbled**. This is due to the **avalanche effect** of the underlying block cipher: a single bit change in the input statistically flips approximately **50% of the output bits** after sufficient rounds.

$$\text{If } C_i \text{ has 1 bit error} \Rightarrow P_i \text{ exhibits } \approx \frac{n}{2} \text{ bit errors}$$

**Step 3 — Conclusion:** Total corrupted plaintext bits $\approx n/2$, confined to block *i*. No adjacent blocks are damaged.

## 3.4 Padding Computation — Worked Example

Suppose the plaintext has length $\vert P \vert = 38$ bits and the block size is $n = 8$ bits. The number of full blocks is:

$$m_{\text{full}} = \lfloor 38 / 8 \rfloor = 4 \text{ full blocks} = 32 \text{ bits}$$

The remaining unfilled bits are:

$$r = 38 - 32 = 6 \text{ bits} \Rightarrow 2 \text{ bits are missing from the 5th block}$$

Using **PKCS#7 padding** measured in *bytes*, the pad length in bytes is:

$$p = 1 \text{ byte} = 8 \text{ bits} \quad \text{(since only 6 of 8 bits are used, 2 bits must be padded)}$$

Wait — PKCS#7 works in bytes, so if the message length is **38 bits**, this is not a byte-aligned example. Let us re-express it with a byte-aligned example: suppose $\vert P \vert = 38$ **bytes** and $n = 8$ **bytes** (64 bits). Then:

$$m_{\text{full}} = \lfloor 38 / 8 \rfloor = 4 \text{ full blocks}$$
$$r = 38 - 32 = 6 \text{ bytes remain unencrypted in the 5th block}$$
$$\text{Pad length } p = 8 - 6 = 2 \text{ bytes}$$

The padded 5th block becomes:

$$P_5 = (\text{original 6 bytes}) \Vert (0x02) \Vert (0x02)$$

Total padded length = $(4 \times 8) + 8 = 40$ bytes, which is a clean multiple of 8. The receiver detects $p = 2$ by reading the last 2 bytes and verifying they both equal 0x02.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students frequently confuse bit-padding with byte-padding. PKCS#7 (and PKCS#5) operate on **bytes**, not bits. If a question specifies "pad to 8-bit block boundary", use bit-padding — typically a single 1-bit followed by zero or more 0-bits (ISO/IEC 9797-1 Method 1).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 ECB Encryption Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    PT1[Plaintext Block P1] --> E1{Encryption E sub K}
    KEY1[Secret Key K] --> E1
    E1 --> CT1[Ciphertext Block C1]

    PT2[Plaintext Block P2] --> E2{Encryption E sub K}
    KEY1 --> E2
    E2 --> CT2[Ciphertext Block C2]

    PT3[Plaintext Block P3] --> E3{Encryption E sub K}
    KEY1 --> E3
    E3 --> CT3[Ciphertext Block C3]

    PT4[Plaintext Block P4] --> E4{Encryption E sub K}
    KEY1 --> E4
    E4 --> CT4[Ciphertext Block C4]

    CT1 --> ASSEM[Ciphertext C equals C1 concat C2 concat C3 concat C4]
    CT2 --> ASSEM
    CT3 --> ASSEM
    CT4 --> ASSEM
```

**Diagram Interpretation:** Each plaintext block $P_i$ enters an *identical* encryption primitive $E_K$ fed by the *same* secret key $K$. The arrows reveal the defining property of ECB: **no information flows between the four parallel encryption branches**. Outputs are simply concatenated to form the final ciphertext $C$.

## 4.2 ECB Decryption Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    CT1[Ciphertext Block C1] --> D1{Decryption D sub K}
    KEYDEC[Secret Key K] --> D1
    D1 --> PT1[Plaintext Block P1]

    CT2[Ciphertext Block C2] --> D2{Decryption D sub K}
    KEYDEC --> D2
    D2 --> PT2[Plaintext Block P2]

    CT3[Ciphertext Block C3] --> D3{Decryption D sub K}
    KEYDEC --> D3
    D3 --> PT3[Plaintext Block P3]

    CT4[Ciphertext Block C4] --> D4{Decryption D sub K}
    KEYDEC --> D4
    D4 --> PT4[Plaintext Block P4]

    PT1 --> REASSEM[Plaintext P equals P1 concat P2 concat P3 concat P4]
    PT2 --> REASSEM
    PT3 --> REASSEM
    PT4 --> REASSEM
```

**Diagram Interpretation:** Decryption is the structural mirror of encryption. The same secret key $K$ is reused across all four decryption primitives, and block independence is preserved. This symmetric structure enables **hardware-level parallel decryption** — a property heavily exploited in storage-controller ASICs.

## 4.3 ECB Pattern Leakage — Conceptual Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    MSG_A[Message A: identical first block]
    MSG_B[Message B: identical first block]

    MSG_A --> ECB_A[ECB Encrypt with Key K]
    MSG_B --> ECB_B[ECB Encrypt with Key K]

    ECB_A --> CIPH_A[Message A Ciphertext: first 8 bytes = X]
    ECB_B --> CIPH_B[Message B Ciphertext: first 8 bytes = X]

    CIPH_A --> ATTACK[Eavesdropper Detects Duplicate 8-byte Pattern X]
    CIPH_B --> ATTACK

    ATTACK --> LEAK[Conclusion: Block 1 of Message A equals Block 1 of Message B]
```

**Diagram Interpretation:** When two different messages share a common plaintext block, ECB produces two ciphertexts with an identical block at the corresponding position. An eavesdropper performing **traffic analysis** can immediately infer that "the first block of both messages is the same plaintext" — even without breaking the cipher. This is the iconic ECB weakness famously illustrated by the **penguin image encryption** demonstration.

## 4.4 ECB Mode — Functional Architecture Summary

| Architectural Property | Implementation Implication |
|:------------------------|:----------------------------|
| **Stateless operation** | No IV, nonce, or counter state to manage between blocks |
| **Parallelizable** | All $m$ blocks can be processed simultaneously on multi-core CPUs or GPU arrays |
| **Idempotent under re-encryption** | Re-encrypting the same plaintext with the same key yields the same ciphertext — useful for deterministic checksums, dangerous for confidentiality |
| **No inter-block dependency** | Bit-error in $C_i$ affects only $P_i$; no error cascades |
| **Linear time complexity** | Encryption time $= O(m \cdot T_{E_K})$; no quadratic overhead from feedback |
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1: Define Electronic Code Book (ECB) mode. List two of its major disadvantages.
`[KTU University Exam - May 2024 | CO2 | Bloom Level: Remember/Understand]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Electronic Code Book (ECB)** is a block cipher mode of operation in which a plaintext message is divided into blocks of fixed size $n$ bits, and each block is encrypted independently using the same secret key $K$. The encryption and decryption transformations are:
>
> $$C_i = E_K(P_i) \qquad P_i = D_K(C_i)$$
>
> **Two major disadvantages:**
>
> 1. **Deterministic Pattern Leakage:** Identical plaintext blocks produce identical ciphertext blocks under the same key, allowing an attacker to detect repeated patterns and perform traffic analysis.
>
> 2. **Vulnerability to Substitution and Reordering Attacks:** Since blocks are independent, an attacker can rearrange, duplicate, or delete ciphertext blocks without detection, enabling *cut-and-paste* attacks on structured messages (e.g., fund-transfer records).

*(Marks distribution: 1 mark for formal definition with formula, 1 mark for disadvantage 1, 1 mark for disadvantage 2.)*

### Question A2: Explain why ECB mode does not require an Initialization Vector (IV).
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 | CO2 | Bloom Level: Understand]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> ECB mode does not require an IV because its encryption transformation $C_i = E_K(P_i)$ depends *only* on the current plaintext block $P_i$ and the static secret key $K$. There is no chaining, no feedback, and no random state passed from block to block.
>
> Since no randomness or state from a previous block enters the encryption of the current block, there is nothing to "initialize" — each block is a self-contained deterministic function of $(P_i, K)$.
>
> *(Note for advanced students: This very property is what makes ECB deterministic and therefore vulnerable to pattern analysis. Modes like CBC introduce an IV precisely to break this determinism.)*

*(Marks distribution: 1 mark for referencing the ECB equation, 1 mark for stating no chaining/feedback, 1 mark for linking to determinism.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Extended Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - Students frequently **omit the explicit equations** $C_i = E_K(P_i)$ and $P_i = D_K(C_i)$ when defining ECB. This alone costs **2 marks** out of 14.
> - A common mistake is to **describe ECB without naming it as "deterministic"** — the property of identical-plaintext → identical-ciphertext is the single most important concept and must be stated explicitly.
> - For diagram questions, students draw arrows between blocks (indicating chaining). **ECB has no inter-block arrows.** Drawing chaining arrows will be marked *incorrect*.
> - Do **not** confuse ECB with CBC: CBC uses an IV and XORs the previous ciphertext with the current plaintext before encryption. ECB does neither.

---

### Question B-A: Part (a) [7 Marks] and Part (b) [7 Marks]
`[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024 | CO2 | Bloom Level: Apply/Analyze]`

**Statement:**

**(a)** Describe the Electronic Code Book (ECB) mode of operation with a neat block diagram. Explain the encryption and decryption processes mathematically, and discuss why ECB is considered a *deterministic* mode.

**(b)** A 128-bit AES block cipher is used in ECB mode to encrypt the 320-bit plaintext `P = 0x4A6F686E446F6553747564656E742121`. Compute:
   - (i) The number of full encryption rounds performed. (3 marks)
   - (ii) The length (in bits) of the PKCS#7 padding required. (2 marks)
   - (iii) Explain what happens to the recovered plaintext if exactly one bit is flipped in ciphertext block $C_2$ during transmission. (2 marks)

#### Model Solution for B-A:

**Part (a) — [7 Marks]**

**Definition [2 marks]:** ECB mode partitions a plaintext message into $m$ blocks of $n$ bits each, encrypting each block independently with the same key $K$:

$$C_i = E_K(P_i), \quad i = 1, 2, \ldots, m$$

**Decryption [1 mark]:** Each ciphertext block is decrypted independently:

$$P_i = D_K(C_i)$$

**Block Diagram [2 marks]:** (Refer to Section 4.1 diagram.) Four parallel branches show plaintext blocks $P_1, P_2, P_3, P_4$ each entering an identical encryption primitive $E_K$ with the same key $K$, producing ciphertext blocks $C_1, C_2, C_3, C_4$ which are concatenated. **Critically, no arrows connect the branches.**

**Determinism [2 marks]:** ECB is deterministic because the mapping $P_i \mapsto C_i$ is a pure function of $P_i$ and $K$ alone — no random IV, no previous-block dependency. Therefore $P_i = P'_i \Rightarrow C_i = C'_i$. This is provable by direct substitution:

$$P_i = P'_i \Rightarrow E_K(P_i) = E_K(P'_i) \Rightarrow C_i = C'_i$$

**Part (b) — [7 Marks]**

**(i) Number of encryption rounds [3 marks]:**

The plaintext is 320 bits long and the block size is $n = 128$ bits.

$$m_{\text{full}} = \lfloor 320 / 128 \rfloor = 2 \text{ full blocks} = 256 \text{ bits}$$

$$\text{Remaining bits} = 320 - 256 = 64 \text{ bits}$$

Since 64 < 128, the 3rd block is incomplete and requires padding. Hence, **the total number of blocks encrypted = 3**, comprising 2 full blocks and 1 padded block. (AES internally performs 10/12/14 rounds *within* each block encryption, but at the *mode-of-operation* level, 3 block-cipher invocations occur.)

**(ii) PKCS#7 padding length [2 marks]:**

The 3rd block must be filled to 128 bits. Currently it holds 64 bits, so it needs 128 − 64 = 64 more bits = **8 bytes** of padding.

Per PKCS#7, the pad consists of 8 bytes, each with value 0x08. The padded 3rd block becomes:

$$P_3 = (\text{original 64 bits}) \Vert (0x08) \Vert (0x08) \Vert (0x08) \Vert (0x08) \Vert (0x08) \Vert (0x08) \Vert (0x08) \Vert (0x08)$$

**Padding length = 64 bits (8 bytes).**

**(iii) Error propagation [2 marks]:**

A 1-bit flip in $C_2$ causes the *entire* plaintext block $P_2$ (128 bits) to be corrupted upon decryption — the underlying AES avalanche effect scrambles roughly 50% of the bits. Blocks $P_1$, $P_3$, and $P_4$ (after padding removal) are **unaffected**. This is the hallmark of ECB's lack of error propagation across blocks.

---

### Question B-B: Part (a) [7 Marks] and Part (b) [7 Marks]
`[KTU University Exam - Sample Question Bank 2024 | CO2 | Bloom Level: Apply/Analyze]`

**Statement:**

**(a)** Compare ECB mode with a block cipher mode that uses *chaining* (any one example of your choice). Use a comparison table covering at least **four** distinguishing parameters. Identify a real-world use case where ECB is *acceptable* despite its weaknesses.

**(b)** Consider two plaintexts $P$ and $P'$ encrypted with the same key $K$ in ECB mode. Suppose $P_3 = P'_3$ and $P_5 = P'_5$, but all other blocks differ. Describe in detail what an eavesdropper can infer from the ciphertexts $C$ and $C'$ without knowing $K$. Justify your answer using the ECB encryption equation.

#### Model Solution for B-B:

**Part (a) — [7 Marks]**

**Comparison Table [4 marks]:**

| Parameter | ECB Mode | CBC Mode (Chained Example) |
|:----------|:---------|:----------------------------|
| IV required | No | Yes — random IV per encryption |
| Determinism | Deterministic | Randomized (IV varies) |
| Pattern leakage | Yes — identical plaintext blocks produce identical ciphertext blocks | No — IV randomizes the first block, and chaining cascades changes |
| Parallelization | Both encryption and decryption are fully parallelizable | Encryption is sequential; decryption is parallelizable |
| Error propagation | 1-bit error in $C_i$ garbles only $P_i$ | 1-bit error in $C_i$ garbles $P_i$ and $P_{i+1}$ |
| Use of previous ciphertext | No | Yes — $C_{i-1}$ is XORed with $P_i$ before encryption |
| Padding requirement | Yes (last block) | Yes (last block) |

**Real-world acceptable use case [3 marks]:** Encrypting a single 128-bit AES key (a *key-encryption key* or KEK) in ECB mode. Since the input is exactly one block, there is no pattern repetition across blocks, and the determinism is harmless. This pattern is used in key-wrapping schemes like RFC 3394 when the wrapped payload is a single key.

**Part (b) — [7 Marks]**

**Step 1 — State the ECB equations [1 mark]:**

$$C_3 = E_K(P_3), \quad C'_3 = E_K(P'_3), \quad C_5 = E_K(P_5), \quad C'_5 = E_K(P'_5)$$

**Step 2 — Apply the given equality [2 marks]:**

Since $P_3 = P'_3$:

$$C_3 = E_K(P_3) = E_K(P'_3) = C'_3$$

Since $P_5 = P'_5$:

$$C_5 = E_K(P_5) = E_K(P'_5) = C'_5$$

**Step 3 — What the eavesdropper can infer [3 marks]:**

The eavesdropper does **not** know $K$, but observing $C_3 = C'_3$ and $C_5 = C'_5$ at the corresponding positions, they can deduce:
- Block 3 of plaintext $P$ is **byte-for-byte identical** to block 3 of plaintext $P'$.
- Block 5 of plaintext $P$ is **byte-for-byte identical** to block 5 of plaintext $P'$.

This is a **known-plaintext distinguisher**: the attacker has not recovered the key, but has extracted structural information about the message contents.

**Step 4 — Security implication [1 mark]:**

If blocks 3 and 5 represent, for example, a salary field and a bank account field in two different financial records, the attacker has learned that the two records share the same salary and account number — a serious confidentiality breach *without* breaking AES.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — ECB Mode**

- **Definition [MUST MEMORIZE]:** $C_i = E_K(P_i)$; $P_i = D_K(C_i)$. State both equations explicitly in every answer.
- **Determinism:** Same plaintext block + same key = same ciphertext block. This is the single most important property.
- **No IV:** ECB is the *only* NIST-approved mode that does not use an Initialization Vector.
- **No Chaining:** No arrow, no feedback, no XOR with previous block. Each block is a sealed unit.
- **Parallelizable:** Both encryption and decryption can run concurrently across all $m$ blocks.
- **Error Propagation:** 1-bit error in $C_i$ garbles approximately $n/2$ bits *only* in $P_i$ (due to block-cipher avalanche). No cascade to adjacent blocks.
- **Padding Required:** PKCS#7 padding is industry standard. Padding length in bytes equals $n_{\text{bytes}} - (\vert P \vert \mod n_{\text{bytes}})$.
- **Security Weaknesses:** (1) Pattern leakage from determinism; (2) cut-and-paste attacks; (3) traffic analysis; (4) no integrity protection (forgeable).
- **Acceptable Use Cases:** Single-block encryption (key wrapping), randomized plaintext (high-entropy tokens), and as a performance baseline.
- **Forbidden in:** Long messages, structured data with repeated fields, sensitive data of any substantial length. Modern protocols mandate **GCM**, **CCM**, or **ChaCha20-Poly1305** instead.
- **Examiner Triggers:** Whenever a KTU question says *"mode of operation"*, *"block cipher"*, *"encrypt multiple blocks"*, or *"pattern preservation"*, ECB is almost certainly part of the expected answer.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cipher Block Chaining Mode
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECAT595 - FOUNDATIONS OF SECURITY IN COMPUTING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Cipher Block Chaining Mode

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cipher Block Chaining (CBC) Mode** is a block cipher mode of operation in which each plaintext block is XORed with the **immediately preceding ciphertext block** before being passed through the encryption algorithm. The very first plaintext block is XORed with a randomly generated **Initialization Vector (IV)** of size equal to the block length.

Mathematically, the CBC encryption process for the $i$-th block is expressed as:

$$C_i = E_K(P_i \oplus C_{i-1})$$

where $C_0 = IV$ (the initialization vector), $P_i$ is the $i$-th plaintext block, $C_i$ is the $i$-th ciphertext block, and $E_K(\cdot)$ denotes the block cipher encryption with secret key $K$. The corresponding decryption is:

$$P_i = D_K(C_i) \oplus C_{i-1}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):** CBC is the *canonical* example used to illustrate how chaining introduces **inter-block dependency**, which is a foundational concept required for understanding authenticated encryption modes (GCM, CCM) covered in later modules.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Sealed Courier Chain"

Imagine a long confidential document that must be transported in **locked boxes** through multiple couriers, one box per page. Each courier hands the box to the next, but every courier must first **insert a piece of paper with the previous courier's seal impression** before locking their own box. This way:

- A single tampered box breaks the chain — the next courier immediately detects the mismatch.
- The **first courier** has no previous seal, so a unique random **"starting seal" (the IV)** is used.
- All boxes together form an unbroken chain — hence *Cipher Block Chaining*.

> [!NOTE]
> This analogy captures the three pillars of CBC: **(i) XOR feedback**, **(ii) IV for randomization**, and **(iii) error propagation through chaining**.

### 1.3 Key Terminology

| Term | Standard Definition |
|------|---------------------|
| **Block Size (b)** | Fixed length of bits processed by the cipher, typically **128 bits** for AES. |
| **Initialization Vector (IV)** | A random or pseudorandom value, of size equal to the block size, used to seed the first block. |
| **Padding** | A scheme (e.g., **PKCS#7**) used to extend the final plaintext block to the full block length. |
| **Chaining** | The mechanism that makes each ciphertext block dependent on all preceding plaintext blocks. |
| **Block Cipher** | A deterministic, invertible algorithm operating on fixed-size blocks (e.g., AES, DES, 3DES). |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Logic (Step-by-Step)

The CBC mode operates through the following structured sequence for **encryption**:

1. **Padding:** The plaintext message $P$ is padded so its length is a multiple of the block size $b$. This yields blocks $P_1, P_2, \ldots, P_n$.
2. **IV Generation:** A fresh, unpredictable IV of length $b$ bits is generated. The IV does *not* need to be secret, but it **must be unique** for every message under the same key.
3. **First Block Encryption:** $C_1 = E_K(P_1 \oplus IV)$.
4. **Chained Encryption (i ≥ 2):** $C_i = E_K(P_i \oplus C_{i-1})$.
5. **Output:** Transmit or store the tuple $(IV, C_1, C_2, \ldots, C_n)$.

The corresponding **decryption** sequence:

1. **Receive:** Read $(IV, C_1, C_2, \ldots, C_n)$.
2. **First Block Decryption:** $P_1 = D_K(C_1) \oplus IV$.
3. **Chained Decryption (i ≥ 2):** $P_i = D_K(C_i) \oplus C_{i-1}$.
4. **Unpadding:** Remove the padding to recover the original plaintext $P$.

### 2.2 Why CBC Works — The "Why" Behind the Chaining

- **Deterministic Block Ciphers Alone Are Insecure:** Electronic Code Book (ECB) mode leaks structural patterns (e.g., pixel repetition in images). CBC randomizes each block via XOR with the previous ciphertext, so identical plaintext blocks almost always produce *different* ciphertext blocks.
- **The XOR is Self-Canceling on Decryption:** Because $C_i = E_K(P_i \oplus C_{i-1})$, applying $D_K$ gives $D_K(C_i) = P_i \oplus C_{i-1}$, and XORing with $C_{i-1}$ recovers $P_i$ cleanly.
- **IV Uniqueness is Non-Negotiable:** If two messages share the same key and the same first plaintext block with the same IV, the first ciphertext blocks will be identical, leaking information. Using a unique IV per message prevents this.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation | Meaning / Use |
|---|----------|---------------|
| 1 | $C_i = E_K(P_i \oplus C_{i-1})$ | **Encryption recurrence** for $i = 1, 2, \ldots, n$ |
| 2 | $P_i = D_K(C_i) \oplus C_{i-1}$ | **Decryption recurrence** for $i = 1, 2, \ldots, n$ |
| 3 | $C_0 = IV$ | **Initialization** (IV acts as the virtual $C_0$) |
| 4 | $\text{Number of blocks } n = \lceil \vert P \vert / b \rceil$ | Block count after padding |
| 5 | $P_{i, \text{error}} \text{ affects } P_i \text{ and } P_{i+1}$ | **Error propagation** bound (1-bit flip → 2-block corruption) |
| 6 | $\text{Security level} \le \min(2^{b/2}, 2^k)$ | Birthday bound on block size $b$ and key size $k$ |
| 7 | $\text{Total ciphertext length} = \vert IV \vert + n \cdot b$ | Storage / transmission size |

> [!NOTE]
> In KTU board papers, **Equation 1, 2, and 3 are the most frequently tested formulas**, often appearing as 2-mark or 4-mark sub-questions. Memorize them verbatim.

### 2.4 Engineering Utility

CBC is widely deployed in real-world protocols:

- **TLS 1.2** (legacy cipher suites such as `TLS_RSA_WITH_AES_128_CBC_SHA`).
- **IPsec (ESP)** in older configurations.
- **Disk/File Encryption** tools (e.g., older versions of TrueCrypt/VeraCrypt).
- **Kerberos** and legacy **S/MIME** email encryption.

> [!WARNING]
> Modern standards (TLS 1.3) have **deprecated CBC in favor of AEAD modes** (AES-GCM, ChaCha20-Poly1305) because CBC alone provides *confidentiality* but not *integrity or authenticity* — it is vulnerable to padding oracle and BEAST-style attacks if naively implemented.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Decryption Equation from Encryption

**Given:** The encryption recurrence is $C_i = E_K(P_i \oplus C_{i-1})$.

**Goal:** Show that $P_i = D_K(C_i) \oplus C_{i-1}$ is its correct inverse.

**Step 1:** Start with the encryption relation.

$$
C_i = E_K(P_i \oplus C_{i-1})
$$

**Step 2:** Apply the inverse block cipher function $D_K(\cdot)$ to both sides. Since $D_K$ is the mathematical inverse of $E_K$, we have $D_K(E_K(x)) = x$.

$$
D_K(C_i) = D_K(E_K(P_i \oplus C_{i-1}))
$$

**Step 3:** Simplify the right-hand side using the inverse property.

$$
D_K(C_i) = P_i \oplus C_{i-1}
$$

**Step 4:** To isolate $P_i$, XOR both sides with $C_{i-1}$. Recall that XOR is its own inverse, so $x \oplus x = 0$ and $x \oplus 0 = x$.

$$
D_K(C_i) \oplus C_{i-1} = (P_i \oplus C_{i-1}) \oplus C_{i-1}
$$

**Step 5:** The right-hand side simplifies: $C_{i-1} \oplus C_{i-1} = 0$, leaving $P_i$.

$$
D_K(C_i) \oplus C_{i-1} = P_i
$$

**Step 6:** Rearranging gives the canonical decryption equation.

$$
P_i = D_K(C_i) \oplus C_{i-1}
$$

Hence proved. $\blacksquare$

### 3.2 Error Propagation Analysis

A single bit flip in ciphertext $C_j$ (during transmission) causes the following effects during decryption:

**Effect on block $j$:** The decrypted $P_j$ becomes completely garbled (roughly **50% bits flipped** on average), because $D_K(C_j')$ (with $C_j' \neq C_j$) is essentially a random-looking output.

**Effect on block $j+1$:** Although $D_K(C_{j+1})$ recovers the correct value, the XOR with $C_j$ (the corrupted previous ciphertext) produces a corrupted plaintext.

**Effect on block $j+2$ and beyond:** Since $C_{j+1}$ itself is *not* corrupted (only the bit flip was in $C_j$, not $C_{j+1}$), the chain self-heals. $P_{j+2} = D_K(C_{j+2}) \oplus C_{j+1}$ is fully correct.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion:** A single ciphertext bit error in CBC affects **exactly two plaintext blocks** — the corresponding block and the next one. This is the canonical error propagation property tested in KTU exams.

### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — CBC vs ECB

| Property | ECB Mode | CBC Mode |
|----------|----------|----------|
| Identical plaintext blocks produce identical ciphertext? | **Yes** (pattern leaks) | **No** (randomized by chaining) |
| Requires IV? | No | **Yes** (size = block size) |
| Encryption parallelizable? | Yes | **No** (sequential dependency) |
| Decryption parallelizable? | Yes | **Yes** (uses only $C_i$ and $C_{i-1}$) |
| Error propagation per bit flip | Affects **1 block** | Affects **2 blocks** |
| Provides integrity? | No | No (must pair with MAC/AEAD) |
| Common attacks | Pattern analysis, block replay | Padding oracle, BEAST, IV manipulation |

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (Block Size = 4 bits, illustrative)

Let block size $b = 4$, key $K$ such that $E_K(x) = x \oplus 1010$, and $D_K(y) = y \oplus 1010$. Let $IV = 1100$, $P_1 = 0101$, $P_2 = 1010$.

**Encryption of $P_1$:**

$$
P_1 \oplus IV = 0101 \oplus 1100 = 1001
$$

$$
C_1 = E_K(1001) = 1001 \oplus 1010 = 0011
$$

**Encryption of $P_2$:**

$$
P_2 \oplus C_1 = 1010 \oplus 0011 = 1001
$$

$$
C_2 = E_K(1001) = 1001 \oplus 1010 = 0011
$$

**Decryption of $C_1$:**

$$
D_K(C_1) = 0011 \oplus 1010 = 1001
$$

$$
P_1 = 1001 \oplus IV = 1001 \oplus 1100 = 0101 \quad \checkmark
$$

**Decryption of $C_2$:**

$$
D_K(C_2) = 0011 \oplus 1010 = 1001
$$

$$
P_2 = 1001 \oplus C_1 = 1001 \oplus 0011 = 1010 \quad \checkmark
$$

> [!NOTE]
> Observe that in this toy example, $C_1 = C_2$ even though $P_1 \neq P_2$. This is *not* a flaw — the IV would normally be much larger and the key/cipher non-linear, ensuring practical distinctness. The example is purely illustrative of the XOR mechanics.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 CBC Encryption — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    IV["IV C0"] --> XOR1["XOR"]
    P1["Plaintext Block P1"] --> XOR1
    XOR1 --> E1["Block Cipher Encrypt EK"]
    Key["Secret Key K"] --> E1
    E1 --> C1["Ciphertext Block C1"]
    C1 --> XOR2["XOR"]
    P2["Plaintext Block P2"] --> XOR2
    XOR2 --> E2["Block Cipher Encrypt EK"]
    Key --> E2
    E2 --> C2["Ciphertext Block C2"]
    C2 --> XOR3["XOR"]
    P3["Plaintext Block P3"] --> XOR3
    XOR3 --> E3["Block Cipher Encrypt EK"]
    Key --> E3
    E3 --> C3["Ciphertext Block C3"]
    C1 -.feedback.-> XOR2
    C2 -.feedback.-> XOR3
```

### 4.2 CBC Decryption — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    IV["IV C0"] --> XOR1["XOR"]
    C1["Ciphertext Block C1"] --> D1["Block Cipher Decrypt DK"]
    Key["Secret Key K"] --> D1
    D1 --> XOR1
    XOR1 --> P1["Plaintext Block P1"]
    C1 --> XOR2["XOR"]
    C2["Ciphertext Block C2"] --> D2["Block Cipher Decrypt DK"]
    Key --> D2
    D2 --> XOR2
    XOR2 --> P2["Plaintext Block P2"]
    C2 --> XOR3["XOR"]
    C3["Ciphertext Block C3"] --> D3["Block Cipher Decrypt DK"]
    Key --> D3
    D3 --> XOR3
    XOR3 --> P3["Plaintext Block P3"]
```

### 4.3 Error Propagation Flow Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Original Bit-Flip in Cj"] --> B["Block Pj becomes garbled ~50% bits"]
    A --> C["Block Pj+1 corrupted via XOR with bad Cj"]
    C --> D["Block Pj+2 onwards self-heals correctly"]
    B --> E["Error confined to 2 blocks"]
    D --> E
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams:** The dashed feedback arrows in the encryption diagram visually express the *chaining* — every block's input depends on the previous block's *output* ciphertext. The decryption diagram shows that the feedback is *local* (only one previous block needed), which is what enables **parallel decryption**.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**  
*What is the role of the Initialization Vector (IV) in Cipher Block Chaining (CBC) mode? Why must it be unpredictable?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**  
The IV is a random or pseudorandom value, of size equal to the block size, XORed with the first plaintext block before encryption. Its purpose is to **randomize the ciphertext** so that two identical plaintext messages encrypted under the same key produce different ciphertexts. The IV must be unpredictable (and unique per message) to prevent an attacker from deducing whether two messages share the same first plaintext block — if the IVs and first blocks matched, the first ciphertext blocks would also match, leaking information.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**  
*State two advantages and one disadvantage of CBC mode over ECB mode.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**  
**Advantages:** (1) CBC hides plaintext patterns because identical plaintext blocks yield different ciphertexts due to the XOR feedback. (2) CBC provides semantic security when a fresh random IV is used. **Disadvantage:** (1) CBC encryption is **sequential** — block $i$ cannot be encrypted until block $i-1$ is finished, so it cannot be parallelized (though decryption can be parallelized).

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

**Q3(a). [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]** — **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Explain the Cipher Block Chaining (CBC) mode of operation with a neat block diagram. State the encryption and decryption equations. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 256-bit plaintext is encrypted using AES (block size 128 bits) in CBC mode with PKCS#7 padding.  
    (i) How many plaintext blocks are produced after padding?  
    (ii) What is the total length (in bits) of the IV and ciphertext that will be transmitted?  
    (iii) If the third ciphertext block is corrupted during transmission, how many plaintext blocks will be affected on decryption? Justify. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]:**

> [!NOTE]
> The encryption process in CBC mode works as follows. The plaintext $P$ is first divided into $n$ blocks $P_1, P_2, \ldots, P_n$ of size $b$ bits each, after applying appropriate padding. A random Initialization Vector $IV$ of size $b$ bits is generated and used as the virtual $C_0$.

For each block $i = 1, 2, \ldots, n$, the encryption recurrence is:

$$
C_i = E_K(P_i \oplus C_{i-1})
$$

where $C_0 = IV$. The decryption recurrence, derived by applying $D_K$ to both sides, is:

$$
P_i = D_K(C_i) \oplus C_{i-1}
$$

The block diagram (refer to Section 4.1) shows each plaintext block being XORed with the previous ciphertext block, then encrypted under the same key $K$. The output ciphertext is the tuple $(IV, C_1, C_2, \ldots, C_n)$.

> **Mark Distribution (Examiner's Key):** Diagram = 2 marks, Encryption equation = 2 marks, Decryption equation = 2 marks, Explanation of chaining role = 1 mark.

---

**Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]:**

**(i) Number of plaintext blocks after padding:**

Given plaintext length $\vert P \vert = 256$ bits and block size $b = 128$ bits:

$$
n = \frac{256}{128} = 2 \text{ blocks (no extra padding block needed since 256 is an exact multiple of 128)}
$$

Using **PKCS#7**, when the plaintext is an exact multiple of the block size, a *full extra block* of padding is added (16 bytes of `\x10` for AES). Therefore:

$$
n = 2 + 1 = 3 \text{ blocks after padding}
$$

**(ii) Total transmission length:**

The IV is one block (128 bits) and the ciphertext is $n = 3$ blocks:

$$
\text{Total length} = 128 + 3 \times 128 = 128 + 384 = 512 \text{ bits}
$$

**(iii) Effect of corrupting the third ciphertext block:**

According to the error propagation property of CBC, a bit error in ciphertext $C_j$ affects exactly **two plaintext blocks** on decryption: the corresponding block $P_j$ (becomes garbled) and the next block $P_{j+1}$ (corrupted via the XOR with the bad $C_j$). All subsequent blocks self-heal.

With $j = 3$, the affected blocks are $P_3$ and $P_4$ (the padding block, which becomes invalid). Hence **2 plaintext blocks** are affected, and the unpadding step will likely fail, signaling corruption.

> **Mark Distribution (Examiner's Key):** (i) 2 marks, (ii) 2 marks, (iii) 2 marks, Justification with CBC error propagation property 1 mark.

---

**Q3(b). [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Modified]** — **Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice**

**(a)** Compare CBC and ECB modes of operation. Discuss how CBC overcomes the limitations of ECB. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A sender uses CBC mode with a 64-bit block cipher. The IV, $C_1$, and $C_2$ are known, but the IV is *reused* for two different messages.  
    (i) Explain a specific attack an adversary can mount.  
    (ii) Show mathematically how the adversary can detect when the first plaintext block of the two messages is identical. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]:**

In **ECB mode**, each plaintext block is independently encrypted as $C_i = E_K(P_i)$. This means identical plaintext blocks always produce identical ciphertext blocks, leaking structural patterns. The famous "ECB penguin" image is a textbook example — pixel-level repetition is preserved in the ciphertext image.

**CBC mode** overcomes this limitation through the XOR-with-previous-ciphertext step. Specifically:

- **First block randomization:** By XORing with the IV, identical first blocks in different messages yield different $C_1$ values (assuming unique IVs).
- **Subsequent block randomization:** Every block $C_i$ depends on $C_{i-1}$, which in turn depends on $P_{i-1}$. Hence, the same plaintext block appearing in two positions produces different ciphertexts because its "history" differs.
- **Semantic Security:** When a fresh random IV is used per message, CBC provides IND-CPA security (indistinguishability under chosen-plaintext attack), which ECB fundamentally cannot.

Other contrasts: ECB allows trivial block reordering and deletion attacks (an attacker can swap two ciphertext blocks without detection). CBC is *partially* resistant to reordering because the XOR dependency breaks if block order is changed.

> **Mark Distribution:** Comparison table = 3 marks, Explanation of pattern leakage in ECB = 2 marks, How CBC randomizes = 2 marks.

---

**Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]:**

**(i) Attack Description (3 marks):**  
The adversary exploits the **IV-reuse vulnerability** in CBC. If the same key $K$ and same IV are used to encrypt two different messages $P$ and $P'$ in CBC mode, then for the first blocks:

$$
C_1 = E_K(P_1 \oplus IV), \quad C'_1 = E_K(P'_1 \oplus IV)
$$

If $P_1 = P'_1$, then $C_1 = C'_1$ (because the encryption function is deterministic). The adversary simply compares $C_1$ and $C'_1$: if they match, the first plaintext blocks are identical. This is a **plaintext-confirmation / equality oracle** attack.

**(ii) Mathematical Justification (4 marks):**

Given $IV_1 = IV_2 = IV$ (reuse) and the same key $K$:

$$
C_1^{(1)} = E_K(P_1^{(1)} \oplus IV)
$$

$$
C_1^{(2)} = E_K(P_1^{(2)} \oplus IV)
$$

Assume the adversary observes $C_1^{(1)} = C_1^{(2)}$. Since $E_K$ is **deterministic** (same input always gives same output), the inputs must be equal:

$$
P_1^{(1)} \oplus IV = P_1^{(2)} \oplus IV
$$

XORing both sides with $IV$ (self-inverse):

$$
P_1^{(1)} = P_1^{(2)}
$$

Hence the adversary can confirm equality of the first plaintext blocks by simply comparing the first ciphertext blocks. This is a *passive* attack — no decryption key is needed.

> **Mark Distribution (Examiner's Key):** (i) Attack description 3 marks, (ii) Mathematical derivation 4 marks.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the IV:** Students often write $C_1 = E_K(P_1)$, omitting the IV. This loses **2 marks** immediately.
> 2. **Confusing Encryption and Decryption Equations:** Mixing up $E_K$ and $D_K$ is a frequent error. Remember: decryption XORs with the *previous* ciphertext, not the *current* plaintext.
> 3. **Claiming CBC Provides Integrity:** It does **not**. If a question asks about authentication, the correct answer involves MACs or AEAD. Writing "CBC provides integrity" is a guaranteed **mark deduction**.
> 4. **Error Propagation:** Students often say "the entire plaintext is corrupted" — wrong. Only **two blocks** are affected. Memorize this.
> 5. **IV Reuse Attack:** Many students overlook this entirely. In CBC, IV reuse is a serious protocol-level flaw and is a favorite KTU question.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **CBC Encryption:** $C_i = E_K(P_i \oplus C_{i-1})$, with $C_0 = IV$.
- ✅ **CBC Decryption:** $P_i = D_K(C_i) \oplus C_{i-1}$, with $C_0 = IV$.
- ✅ **IV size = block size (b)**, must be **unique per message**, ideally unpredictable.
- ✅ **Encryption is sequential** (no parallelism); **decryption is parallelizable**.
- ✅ **Error propagation:** A single ciphertext bit flip corrupts **2 plaintext blocks** (current and next), then self-heals.
- ✅ **CBC does NOT provide integrity or authentication** — it offers confidentiality only. Pair with a MAC (e.g., HMAC) or use AEAD modes (AES-GCM) for both.
- ✅ **PKCS#7 padding** is the standard; remember the full extra padding block when plaintext length is a multiple of $b$.
- ✅ **Real-world uses:** Legacy TLS 1.2, IPsec ESP, disk encryption (older VeraCrypt).
- ✅ **Real-world attacks to remember:** Padding Oracle (Vaudenay 2002), BEAST (2011, SSL 3.0/TLS 1.0), Lucky 13 (timing attack on CBC-MAC in TLS).
- ✅ **Block size matters:** For long-term security with $b = 128$ bits, the birthday bound allows $2^{64}$ blocks per key before collisions become probable — always rotate keys before this limit.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cipher Feedback Mode
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECAT595 - FOUNDATIONS OF SECURITY IN COMPUTING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Cipher Feedback Mode

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 2 — Cipher Feedback Mode (CFB)

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cipher Feedback (CFB) Mode** is a mode of operation for a block cipher that converts the block cipher into a **self-synchronizing stream cipher**. It generates the keystream by repeatedly encrypting the previous ciphertext block (or portion of it) and XORing the result with the plaintext. Formally, CFB produces ciphertext in units of **s bits**, where **1 ≤ s ≤ b** and **b** is the block size of the underlying block cipher (e.g., **b = 128 bits** for AES).

The mode uses an **Initialization Vector (IV)** of length **b bits** to seed the process and an internal **shift register** that holds the most recently transmitted ciphertext (or a portion of it). CFB does not require a separate decryption algorithm; the **encryption function E_K itself is used during decryption** because the XOR operation is its own inverse.

The variant is typically referred to as **CFB-s** where **s** denotes the number of plaintext/ciphertext bits processed per feedback unit. Common choices include:
- **CFB-1** (1-bit feedback)
- **CFB-8** (8-bit / byte feedback)
- **CFB-64** (64-bit feedback)
- **CFB-128** (128-bit feedback for AES)

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU PECAT595 Module 2):** CFB is grouped under "Modes of Block Ciphers / Confidentiality-Only Modes." The KTU 2024 scheme expects students to derive the encryption and decryption equations, illustrate the shift-register operation, and explain self-synchronization with a block diagram.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Plain-English Analogy — "The Talking Conch in a Noisy Cave"**
>
> Imagine two friends communicating inside a long, dark cave. They cannot see each other, so they use a *talking conch* that whispers one word at a time. The rule is: the next word you speak must be the **last word your friend just finished saying**. The conch is essentially a continuous memory of the most recent audio — if a single word is misheard, the next few words will sound jumbled, but eventually the system "resets" and clear communication resumes.
>
> In CFB, the **shift register is the conch's memory**. The **encryption algorithm E_K acts as the listener's brain**, generating the next segment of a pseudo-random stream (the "next word"). The **ciphertext bits are like the spoken words** — they are both the output and the input to the next round. A flipped bit in transmission will garble a few subsequent plaintext blocks, but synchronization is automatically recovered once the corrupted bits have been shifted out — hence the term **self-synchronizing stream cipher**.

The shift register always contains **b bits** of "context" — this is what makes CFB robust against bit-flipping and bit-insertion attacks at the cost of a bounded period of garbled output.

### 1.3 Key Terminology

| Term | Meaning |
| :--- | :--- |
| **b** | Block size of the underlying cipher (e.g., **128 bits** for AES) |
| **s** | Feedback unit (1 ≤ s ≤ b); the parameter that names the variant (CFB-s) |
| **IV** | Initialization Vector, a b-bit nonce loaded into the shift register initially |
| **S_i** | State of the b-bit shift register at the start of processing block i |
| **O_i** | Output of the block cipher: O_i = E_K(S_i) |
| **T_i** | The leftmost s bits of O_i (the "keystream segment" for block i) |
| **P_i, C_i** | s-bit plaintext and ciphertext blocks |
| **Shift Register** | The b-bit storage of the most recent transmitted ciphertext bits |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic — Step by Step

The CFB mode processes data in **s-bit chunks**, not full b-bit blocks. The following ordered logic applies for **i = 1, 2, 3, …, n**:

### Encryption Algorithm
1. **Initialize the shift register:**  
   $S_1 = \text{IV}$ (a b-bit value, typically a nonce).
2. **Encrypt the register contents:**  
   Compute $O_i = E_K(S_i)$ — the b-bit output of the block cipher using the secret key K.
3. **Extract the keystream segment:**  
   Take the **leftmost s bits** of $O_i$. This is denoted $T_i$ and is the pseudo-random stream for block i.
4. **Generate ciphertext (XOR with plaintext):**  
   $C_i = P_i \oplus T_i$.
5. **Update the shift register (feedback):**  
   $S_{i+1} = (\text{leftmost } (b-s) \text{ bits of } S_i) \;\Vert\; C_i$.  
   Equivalently: shift $S_i$ left by s bits and append $C_i$ as the new rightmost s bits.
6. **Return to step 2** for the next i.

### Decryption Algorithm
1. **Initialize the shift register:**  
   $S_1 = \text{IV}$ (same IV as used in encryption).
2. **Encrypt the register contents:**  
   Compute $O_i = E_K(S_i)$ — *note: the same encryption function E_K is used, not D_K*.
3. **Extract the keystream segment:**  
   $T_i = \text{leftmost } s \text{ bits of } O_i$.
4. **Recover plaintext:**  
   $P_i = C_i \oplus T_i$.
5. **Update the shift register (with ciphertext, not plaintext):**  
   $S_{i+1} = (\text{leftmost } (b-s) \text{ bits of } S_i) \;\Vert\; C_i$.
6. **Return to step 2** for the next i.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why does decryption use E_K and not D_K?**  
> Because XOR is its own inverse: $C_i = P_i \oplus T_i \implies P_i = C_i \oplus T_i$. The keystream segment $T_i$ depends only on $S_i$, and the receiver can compute $S_i$ from previously received ciphertext bits. Therefore the same block-cipher encryption function serves both directions.

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Step

- **Step 1 (IV):** Prevents identical plaintext prefixes from producing identical ciphertext prefixes across sessions. The IV must be **unpredictable** (not merely unique) for CFB to be IND-CPA secure in modern proofs.
- **Step 2 (Encryption of register):** The block cipher acts as a pseudo-random function on the current state. It expands the b-bit context into a fresh b-bit block from which we harvest s bits.
- **Step 3 (Take leftmost s bits):** Any s bits would do, but the high-order bits are conventional.
- **Step 4 (XOR):** Produces a stream-cipher-like output: the ciphertext reveals nothing about the plaintext unless the keystream is known.
- **Step 5 (Shift register update):** Feeds the latest ciphertext into the system, so the keystream evolves with the message. This is what gives CFB its self-synchronization property.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table consolidates **every** equation you will need in a KTU 2024 examination for CFB mode.

| Symbol / Concept | Formula / Definition | Notes / Units |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Block size of underlying cipher | $b$ | e.g., $b = 128$ for AES |
| Feedback unit | $s$ | $1 \le s \le b$ |
| Shift-register initial state | $S_1 = \text{IV}$ | b bits, must be unpredictable |
| Block-cipher output (per round) | $O_i = E_K(S_i)$ | b bits |
| Keystream segment | $T_i = \text{MSB}_s(O_i)$ | s bits (leftmost) |
| **Encryption equation** | $C_i = P_i \oplus T_i$ | s-bit output |
| **Decryption equation** | $P_i = C_i \oplus T_i$ | s-bit output (same T_i) |
| Shift-register update | $S_{i+1} = (S_i \ll s) \;\Vert\; C_i$ | Effectively discards leftmost s bits of S_i and appends C_i on the right |
| Throughput factor | $\dfrac{s}{b}$ | Compared to raw block-cipher speed |
| Self-sync delay (blocks) | $\left\lceil \dfrac{b}{s} \right\rceil$ | Number of blocks garbled by a 1-bit flip |
| Total bits to resync | $b$ | Always b bits must pass through register |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!TIP]
> **Where is CFB used in practice?**
> - **TLS 1.2 (in legacy suites such as TLS_RSA_WITH_AES_128_CBC_SHA):** older cipher suites used CFB-like constructions; modern TLS uses GCM/CCM (AEAD), but the conceptual understanding of CFB underpins these.
> - **Disk / tape encryption (legacy):** When padding plaintexts to a full block is undesirable, CFB and its cousin CTR are preferred because they operate as stream ciphers.
> - **Satellite and serial communication links:** Bit-oriented or byte-oriented stream ciphers (CFB-1 and CFB-8) handle arbitrary message lengths with no padding.
> - **SSH transport layer (older versions):** Used CFB-128 with 3DES or AES for confidentiality.

The conceptual framework built around CFB also paves the way for understanding **OFB (Output Feedback)** and **CTR (Counter)** modes, which are surveyed in the same KTU Module 2.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the CFB Encryption Equation

**Objective:** Derive the relationship $C_i = P_i \oplus \text{MSB}_s(E_K(S_i))$ from first principles.

**Step 1 — State definition.**  
Let the shift register at the start of block $i$ be a $b$-bit vector denoted $S_i$.

**Step 2 — Apply the block cipher.**  
The sender evaluates the encryption function on the current state with secret key $K$:

$$O_i = E_K(S_i)$$

**Step 3 — Truncate to s bits.**  
The "keystream" segment is the leftmost $s$ bits:

$$T_i = \text{MSB}_s(O_i) \quad \in \{0,1\}^s$$

**Step 4 — Stream-cipher XOR.**  
The ciphertext for this $s$-bit segment is the plaintext XOR the keystream segment:

$$C_i = P_i \oplus T_i$$

**Step 5 — Substitution.**  
Substituting the expression for $T_i$ from Step 3 into Step 4:

$$\boxed{\,C_i = P_i \oplus \text{MSB}_s\!\big(E_K(S_i)\big)\,}$$

**Step 6 — Shift-register update derivation.**  
The next state $S_{i+1}$ is constructed by:
- Discarding the leftmost $s$ bits of $S_i$ (they have already contributed to keystream and shift out), and
- Appending the newly produced $C_i$ at the rightmost end.

Mathematically, for $b$-bit state:

$$S_{i+1} \;=\; \big( S_i \;\&\; \underbrace{(2^b - 2^s)}_{\text{mask clearing rightmost s bits}} \big) \;\big\vert\; \big( C_i \;\&\; (2^s - 1) \big)$$

Or more elegantly, using left-shift and concatenation:

$$S_{i+1} = (S_i \ll s) \;\Vert\; C_i \pmod{2^b}$$

> This completes the closed-form derivation of CFB encryption.

## 3.2 Derivation of the Decryption Equation and the Use of E_K

**Step 1 — Same keystream, computed independently.**  
The legitimate receiver knows the IV and has received $C_1, C_2, \ldots, C_{i-1}$. It can reconstruct the same shift-register state $S_i$ that the sender had.

**Step 2 — Same keystream segment.**  
It therefore computes:

$$T_i = \text{MSB}_s(E_K(S_i))$$

**Step 3 — Invert the XOR.**  
Starting from the sender's equation:

$$C_i = P_i \oplus T_i$$

Apply XOR with $T_i$ on both sides:

$$C_i \oplus T_i = (P_i \oplus T_i) \oplus T_i$$

By the properties of XOR ($x \oplus x = 0$ and $x \oplus 0 = x$):

$$C_i \oplus T_i = P_i \oplus (T_i \oplus T_i) = P_i \oplus 0 = P_i$$

Hence:

$$\boxed{\,P_i = C_i \oplus T_i = C_i \oplus \text{MSB}_s\!\big(E_K(S_i)\big)\,}$$

**Step 4 — Why E_K (and not D_K) is used.**  
The decryption function $D_K$ is not needed because the block cipher is only used to **generate the pseudo-random keystream**, not to encrypt or decrypt the data directly. The actual cryptographic transformation on the data is XOR, which is its own inverse. This is a defining property that CFB shares with OFB and CTR modes.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of CFB Variants

| Property | **CFB-1** | **CFB-8** | **CFB-64** | **CFB-128** |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Feedback unit $s$ | 1 bit | 8 bits (1 byte) | 64 bits | 128 bits (= $b$ for AES) |
| Throughput vs. raw block cipher | $\tfrac{1}{b} \approx 0.78\%$ | $\tfrac{8}{b} \approx 6.25\%$ | $\tfrac{64}{b} = 50\%$ | $100\%$ |
| Self-sync delay (blocks) | 128 | 16 | 2 | 1 |
| Self-sync delay (bits) | 128 | 128 | 128 | 128 |
| Padding required | No | No | No | No |
| Latency per block | High (1 bit) | Moderate | Low | Lowest |
| Common use | Bit-oriented links | Stream / character protocols | Bulk data | Approx. equivalent to CBC |
| Cryptanalytic strength (per bit) | Lower | Low | Moderate | High |

> [!NOTE]
> **Observation:** CFB-1 is the most "stream-like" (each bit has its own block-cipher evaluation) but has the worst throughput — $\frac{1}{b}$ of the block cipher. CFB-$b$ (full-block feedback) approaches the throughput of CBC and exhibits the fastest self-synchronization.

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (CFB-1 with Toy 8-bit Block Cipher)

> **Toy parameters:** block size $b = 8$, $s = 1$, key $K$ fixed, encryption function $E_K(x) = \text{ROL}(x, 1) \oplus 0x37$ (rotate left by 1, then XOR constant), IV $= 10110010$.

| $i$ | $S_i$ (8 bits) | $O_i = E_K(S_i)$ | $T_i$ (leftmost 1 bit) | $P_i$ | $C_i = P_i \oplus T_i$ | $S_{i+1}$ |
| :--: | :--: | :--: | :--: | :--: | :--: | :--: |
| 1 | 10110010 | 01100101 ⊕ 00110111 = 01010010 | 0 | 1 | 1 | 01100101 |
| 2 | 01100101 | 11001010 ⊕ 00110111 = 11111101 | 1 | 0 | 1 | 11001011 |
| 3 | 11001011 | 10010111 ⊕ 00110111 = 10100000 | 1 | 1 | 0 | 10010110 |
| 4 | 10010110 | 00101100 ⊕ 00110111 = 00011011 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 00101100 |

> Here ROL($x$, 1) is computed by moving the MSB of $x$ to the LSB. Verify: ROL(10110010) = 01100101, then XOR with 00110111 gives 01010010. The IV ensures the keystream differs for every session.

This is the kind of step-by-step table KTU examiners reward in **Part B** answers.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 CFB Encryption Flow (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    IVreg["Shift Register S1 initialized to IV (b bits)"]
    enc1["Block Cipher Encryptor EK"]
    msb1["MSB-s Selector (leftmost s bits)"]
    xor1["XOR gate (⊕)"]
    pln["Plaintext Pi (s bits)"]
    cph["Ciphertext Ci (s bits)"]
    feed["Shift Register Update: Si+1 = (Si << s) || Ci"]

    IVreg -- "Si" --> enc1
    enc1 -- "Oi (b bits)" --> msb1
    msb1 -- "Ti (s bits)" --> xor1
    pln -- "Pi" --> xor1
    xor1 -- "Ci" --> cph
    cph -. "feedback" .-> feed
    IVreg -. "previous state" .-> feed
    feed -- "Si+1" --> IVreg
```

## 4.2 CFB Decryption Flow (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    IVreg2["Shift Register S1 initialized to IV (b bits)"]
    enc2["Block Cipher Encryptor EK (same as encryption)"]
    msb2["MSB-s Selector (leftmost s bits)"]
    xor2["XOR gate (⊕)"]
    cph2["Ciphertext Ci (s bits)"]
    pln2["Recovered Plaintext Pi (s bits)"]
    feed2["Shift Register Update: Si+1 = (Si << s) || Ci"]

    IVreg2 -- "Si" --> enc2
    enc2 -- "Oi (b bits)" --> msb2
    msb2 -- "Ti (s bits)" --> xor2
    cph2 -- "Ci" --> xor2
    xor2 -- "Pi" --> pln2
    cph2 -. "feedback (ciphertext, not plaintext)" .-> feed2
    IVreg2 -. "previous state" .-> feed2
    feed2 -- "Si+1" --> IVreg2
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note the symmetry:** the decryption diagram differs from the encryption diagram in *one single label* — the XOR inputs swap. The block-cipher encryption box, the shift register, and the feedback path remain **identical**. This is a popular KTU question: "Why is the decryption side of CFB structurally identical to the encryption side?"

## 4.3 Error Propagation Visualization

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    err["Bit-flip in ciphertext Ci (transmission error)"]
    blk1["Affects Pi directly (1 s-bit block garbled)"]
    reg["Corrupted bit enters shift register Si+1"]
    blkN["Affects the next ceil(b / s) plaintext blocks"]
    sync["After b bits shifted out, shift register contains only correct ciphertext bits"]
    rec["Receiver automatically re-synchronizes (self-healing)"]

    err --> blk1
    err --> reg
    reg --> blkN
    blkN --> sync
    sync --> rec
```

> [!TIP]
> **Reading the diagram:** A single bit error in $C_i$ will garble **one** s-bit block $P_i$ plus a tail of approximately $\lceil b/s \rceil - 1$ additional blocks while the bad bit is still inside the shift register. Once it has shifted out, decryption is correct again. This bounded error-propagation window is precisely the **self-synchronization** property.

## 4.4 Summary Block Diagram — Why CFB Behaves Like a Stream Cipher

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    ks["Keystream source: Ti = MSB_s EK Si"]
    plnA["Plaintext Pi"]
    cphA["Ciphertext Ci = Pi ⊕ Ti"]
    chn["Channel"]
    cphB["Received Ciphertext Ci"]
    ksB["Identical Keystream Ti (receiver)"]
    plnB["Recovered Plaintext Pi = Ci ⊕ Ti"]

    ks -- "Ti" --> cphA
    plnA -- "Pi" --> cphA
    cphA -- "Ci" --> chn
    chn -- "Ci" --> cphB
    cphB -- "Ci" --> plnB
    ksB -- "Ti" --> plnB
```

The receiver's keystream is identical to the sender's *as long as* the receiver's shift register is in the same state. The receiver's shift register is rebuilt from the received ciphertext — so as soon as the receiver has b correct ciphertext bits in a row, the two shift registers are once again in lockstep.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — May 2023, Model Paper]**
**Differentiate between Cipher Feedback (CFB) mode and Cipher Block Chaining (CBC) mode.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | CBC | CFB |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Operation type | True block cipher mode | Stream-cipher emulation using a block cipher |
| Plaintext unit | Full block $b$ | Sub-block of $s$ bits ($1 \le s \le b$) |
| Feedback | Previous **ciphertext** XORed into next **plaintext** before encryption | Previous **ciphertext** shifted into the input **register** |
| Padding | Required if message not a multiple of $b$ | Not required |
| Decryption uses | $D_K$ (block-cipher decryption) | $E_K$ (block-cipher encryption) |
| Error propagation | Affects current and next block | Affects $\lceil b/s \rceil$ blocks |

**(Distribution: 1 mark for operation type, 1 mark for padding/feedback difference, 1 mark for decryption function or error propagation.)**

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Supplementary]**
**Define self-synchronization in the context of CFB mode. What determines the duration of the resynchronization?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1 mark):** A stream cipher is *self-synchronizing* if it can recover correct decryption automatically after ciphertext errors or bit insertions/deletions, by relying only on the most recent $b$ bits of correctly received ciphertext.
- **Mechanism in CFB (1 mark):** The shift register is updated from the *received* ciphertext. Once $b$ correct bits have entered the register, the keystream generator on the receiver side matches the sender's keystream generator.
- **Resynchronization duration (1 mark):** Exactly $b$ bits must shift through, i.e., $\lceil b / s \rceil$ plaintext/ciphertext blocks of size $s$ will be garbled before recovery.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Two sub-parts of **7 marks each**, with internal choice between the two complete questions.

---

### ❓ Question A (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam — Sample Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** Explain the working of Cipher Feedback (CFB) mode with a neat block diagram, defining all components: shift register, IV, encryption function, MSB-s selector, XOR, and feedback path. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss error propagation in CFB mode. What is the impact of a single bit-flip in $C_i$ on subsequent plaintext blocks? How does the receiver resynchronize? **(7 marks)**

#### 📝 Model Solution for Q-A (a)

1. **(1 mark)** Define CFB as a stream-cipher emulation that operates on $s$-bit units using a $b$-bit block cipher.
2. **(1 mark)** State initial conditions: $S_1 = \text{IV}$, $b$-bit shift register.
3. **(1 mark)** Encryption: $O_i = E_K(S_i)$, $T_i = \text{MSB}_s(O_i)$, $C_i = P_i \oplus T_i$.
4. **(1 mark)** Update: $S_{i+1} = (S_i \ll s) \Vert C_i$.
5. **(1 mark)** Decryption uses $E_K$ (not $D_K$) and same $T_i$; $P_i = C_i \oplus T_i$.
6. **(1 mark)** Block diagram showing shift register → encryption → MSB-s → XOR with $P_i$ → $C_i$ → feedback.
7. **(1 mark)** Note on IV being a nonce (unpredictable, unique per session).

#### 📝 Model Solution for Q-A (b)

1. **(1 mark)** A single bit-flip in $C_i$ corrupts exactly one $s$-bit block $P_i$ at the receiver.
2. **(1 mark)** The corrupted bit now enters $S_{i+1}$, poisoning the keystream.
3. **(1 mark)** Subsequent decryptions of $P_{i+1}, P_{i+2}, \ldots, P_{i+\lceil b/s \rceil - 1}$ are garbled.
4. **(1 mark)** Total garbled blocks = $\lceil b/s \rceil$ (the current block plus $\lceil b/s \rceil - 1$ trailing blocks).
5. **(1 mark)** After $b$ bits have been received correctly in succession, the bad bit is shifted out and the register holds only good bits.
6. **(1 mark)** From that point, the receiver and sender keystreams coincide — automatic resynchronization.
7. **(1 mark)** Distinguish from CBC: CFB error propagation is *bounded* and *self-healing*; CBC errors persist for one block and then vanish, but CFB may garble a tail of multiple blocks.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Do NOT** write that CFB is identical to CBC. The student must explicitly mention the **shift register**, the **MSB-s keystream extraction**, and the **use of E_K on both sides**.
> - A common mistake is to claim "a 1-bit error affects only one block." This is true **only for CFB-$b$** (full block). For CFB-1, it affects up to 128 blocks. Always state the dependency on $s$.
> - Forgetting to label the **IV** and the **feedback direction** loses 1–2 marks.
> - Examiners penalize diagrams that omit the arrow showing the ciphertext feeding back into the shift register.

---

### ❓ Question B (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam — Sample Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** Compare CFB-1, CFB-8, CFB-64, and CFB-128 variants in terms of throughput, self-synchronization delay, and typical use cases. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Show mathematically that the decryption algorithm of CFB mode uses the **same block-cipher encryption function** $E_K$, not the decryption function $D_K$. Justify with the property of XOR. **(7 marks)**

#### 📝 Model Solution for Q-B (a)

1. **(2 marks)** Define each variant by its $s$ value and compute throughput: $\text{throughput} = s/b$ (mark table: CFB-1 = 1/128, CFB-8 = 1/16, CFB-64 = 1/2, CFB-128 = 1).
2. **(2 marks)** Self-sync delay in **blocks**: $\lceil b/s \rceil$ → 128, 16, 2, 1 respectively; in **bits** it is always $b = 128$.
3. **(1 mark)** Trade-off statement: smaller $s$ = finer stream granularity, higher latency, lower throughput.
4. **(1 mark)** Typical use cases: CFB-1 for bit-oriented channels, CFB-8 for character streams / legacy serial protocols, CFB-64 for moderate-throughput links, CFB-128 for bulk transfer (approaches CBC efficiency).
5. **(1 mark)** Conclude: no single CFB variant dominates; the choice is driven by the data granularity of the application.

#### 📝 Model Solution for Q-B (b)

1. **(1 mark)** Start with the encryption equation: $C_i = P_i \oplus T_i$, where $T_i = \text{MSB}_s(E_K(S_i))$.
2. **(1 mark)** Observe that $T_i$ is a function of $S_i$ and the key $K$ only — **not** a function of $P_i$.
3. **(1 mark)** The receiver can compute $S_i$ from the IV and the previously received ciphertext $C_1, \ldots, C_{i-1}$ (because the shift register is updated from ciphertext, not plaintext).
4. **(1 mark)** Therefore the receiver computes the **same** $T_i = \text{MSB}_s(E_K(S_i))$ using the **encryption** function $E_K$ — no need for $D_K$.
5. **(1 mark)** Apply XOR to both sides: $C_i \oplus T_i = (P_i \oplus T_i) \oplus T_i$.
6. **(1 mark)** Use the XOR properties $x \oplus x = 0$ and $x \oplus 0 = x$ to obtain $C_i \oplus T_i = P_i$.
7. **(1 mark)** Conclude: the decryption side of CFB is symmetric to the encryption side, uses the same primitive $E_K$, and exploits the involutory nature of XOR.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Mandatory diagram:** Either a 2-table comparison (Q-B-a) or a derivation chain (Q-B-b) must be present; the examiner expects a **tabular or equation-based presentation**, not a paragraph.
> - In Q-B-b, students often forget to mention that the receiver reconstructs $S_i$ from **received ciphertext bits** (not plaintext), which is the keystone of the proof.
> - Do **not** confuse with OFB: in OFB, the shift register is updated from $O_i$, not $C_i$, so the same $E_K$ appears for a *different* reason.
> - Always quote the **exact equation** $P_i = C_i \oplus \text{MSB}_s(E_K(S_i))$ in the answer; paraphrasing it loosely loses accuracy marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Final revision checklist for KTU 2024 — CFB Mode:**

- ✅ **CFB converts a block cipher into a stream cipher** by feeding back ciphertext into a shift register.
- ✅ The shift register always holds **$b$ bits** of context (e.g., 128 for AES).
- ✅ **Encryption equation:** $C_i = P_i \oplus \text{MSB}_s(E_K(S_i))$.
- ✅ **Decryption equation:** $P_i = C_i \oplus \text{MSB}_s(E_K(S_i))$ — **same $E_K$ is used, not $D_K$**.
- ✅ **Shift-register update:** $S_{i+1} = (S_i \ll s) \,\|\, C_i$ — discards leftmost $s$ bits and appends the latest ciphertext.
- ✅ **IV is a nonce** that must be unpredictable and unique per session.
- ✅ **CFB-$s$** refers to the size of the plaintext/ciphertext unit; throughput is $s/b$ of the underlying block cipher.
- ✅ **Self-synchronization** is automatic after $b$ correct bits pass through, i.e., $\lceil b/s \rceil$ blocks.
- ✅ A single bit-flip in $C_i$ garbles $\lceil b/s \rceil$ blocks: one directly, and the rest while the bad bit remains in the shift register.
- ✅ **No padding is required** because CFB works on $s$-bit chunks.
- ✅ **CFB is symmetric in structure between encryption and decryption**; only the XOR inputs swap.
- ✅ **Limitations:** CFB is not an authenticated mode (use GCM/CCM/AES-CBC + MAC instead for integrity); throughput penalty for small $s$; a single bit-flip poisons a multi-block tail.
- ✅ **Difference vs. CBC:** CBC encrypts full blocks and uses $D_K$ in decryption; CFB works on sub-blocks and uses $E_K$ in both.
- ✅ **Difference vs. OFB:** CFB feedback is from ciphertext; OFB feedback is from the block-cipher output $O_i$ (independent of plaintext and ciphertext).
- ✅ **Difference vs. CTR:** CTR uses a counter instead of a shift register; CFB's keystream is stateful from ciphertext, CTR's keystream is stateless given a counter value.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Output Feedback Mode
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECAT595 - FOUNDATIONS OF SECURITY IN COMPUTING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Output Feedback Mode

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Output Feedback (OFB) Mode** is a mode of operation for a symmetric-key block cipher (such as AES or DES) that transforms the underlying block cipher into a **synchronous stream cipher**. In OFB mode, the block cipher is used to repeatedly encrypt an **Initialization Vector (IV)** to generate a **keystream**, which is then combined (typically via XOR) with the plaintext to produce the ciphertext.

The defining characteristic of OFB is that the **feedback path is derived from the output of the block cipher's encryption function, not from the ciphertext itself.** This is a critical distinction that sets it apart from Cipher Feedback (CFB) mode.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> "Output Feedback (OFB) mode is a confidentiality mode of operation that generates a keystream by repeatedly encrypting the Initialization Vector (or the previous output block) under the same secret key. The keystream is XORed with plaintext to produce ciphertext. Since the feedback is independent of the plaintext and ciphertext, OFB behaves as a synchronous stream cipher."

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **water wheel generator (keystream generator)** that powers a series of identical locks:

1. **The Secret Key (K)** is the master blueprint that governs how the generator produces power.
2. **The Initialization Vector (IV)** is the initial push that sets the water wheel in motion.
3. Each rotation of the wheel produces a new "energy unit" (output block $O_j$).
4. The next rotation of the wheel is **always powered by the previous energy unit**, NOT by anything that has been written or locked so far.
5. This energy unit is then **XORed with your message** to scramble it.

The crucial insight: **the water wheel does not look at the message or the lockbox (ciphertext) — it runs on its own internal rhythm (synchrony)**. If one energy unit is lost during transmission, all subsequent energy units become useless, requiring the system to resynchronize.

> [!NOTE]
> **Stream Cipher Analogy:**
> OFB converts a block cipher into a stream cipher where the cryptographic strength comes from the **pseudorandom keystream**, not from the block cipher's operation on the actual data. This is conceptually similar to how a Linear Feedback Shift Register (LFSR) generates keystreams, but OFB uses a much stronger cryptographic primitive (the block cipher) instead of simple bit-shifting.

## 1.3 Fundamental Notation & Symbols

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Notation used in KTU Board Examinations:**
> - $E_K$ → Block cipher encryption function using secret key $K$
> - $D_K$ → Block cipher decryption function using secret key $K$
> - $P_j$ → $j$-th plaintext block (b bits)
> - $C_j$ → $j$-th ciphertext block (b bits)
> - $O_j$ → $j$-th output (keystream) block (b bits)
> - $IV$ → Initialization Vector
> - $b$ → Block size of the underlying block cipher (e.g., **b = 64** for DES, **b = 128** for AES)
> - $n$ → Total number of plaintext blocks
> - $\oplus$ → Bitwise XOR (exclusive OR) operation
- **Key Length (k):** Typically **56 bits (DES)** or **128/192/256 bits (AES)**
- **Block Size (b):** **64 bits (DES)** or **128 bits (AES)**
- **IV Size:** Equal to the block size $b$

## 1.4 Operational Identity Equations

The OFB mode is governed by **two elegant equations**:

$$O_0 = IV$$

$$O_j = E_K(O_{j-1}) \quad \text{for } j = 1, 2, 3, \ldots, n$$

$$C_j = P_j \oplus O_j \quad \text{for } j = 1, 2, 3, \ldots, n$$

The decryption operation uses the **same keystream generation process** and reverses the XOR:

$$P_j = C_j \oplus O_j \quad \text{for } j = 1, 2, 3, \ldots, n$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight for KTU Examinations:**
> In OFB mode, **decryption uses $E_K$ (encryption function), NOT $D_K$ (decryption function)**. This is because the keystream $O_j$ is generated by encrypting, and the only reversible operation in the data path is the XOR.

## 1.5 Why "Feedback" of the Output?

The name "**Output Feedback**" explicitly describes the feedback mechanism:
- The **Output** ($O_{j-1}$) of the **previous** block cipher invocation
- is **Fed Back** as input to the **next** block cipher invocation

This is fundamentally different from Ciphertext Feedback (CFB), where the **ciphertext** $C_{j-1}$ is fed back, and from Electronic Code Book (ECB) or Counter (CTR) modes, which have no such feedback loop.

## 1.6 Block Size Considerations

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard OFB vs. Variable-Length OFB:**
> - **Standard OFB (Full-block OFB):** The feedback unit is the full block size $b$ (e.g., 64 or 128 bits). The keystream block size equals the block size.
> - **Variable-length OFB (OFB-s):** The feedback is $s$ bits where $1 \leq s \leq b$. The rightmost $s$ bits of $E_K(O_{j-1})$ are used as $O_j$, and only $s$ bits of plaintext are XORed at a time. This converts the cipher into a self-synchronizing stream cipher.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step Breakdown

The OFB mode operates in a **deterministic, iterative feedback loop**. Below is the structured logical flow:

### **Phase 1: Initialization (Pre-Encryption)**
- **Step 1:** Sender and receiver agree on a shared secret key $K$.
- **Step 2:** Sender selects a unique, unpredictable Initialization Vector (IV) of size $b$ bits.
- **Step 3:** Set $O_0 = IV$.

### **Phase 2: Keystream Generation**
- **Step 4:** For each block index $j = 1, 2, \ldots, n$:
  - Compute $O_j = E_K(O_{j-1})$ — encrypt the previous output block.
  - The block cipher is used **only for encryption**, never for decryption.

### **Phase 3: Encryption (Data Path)**
- **Step 5:** Compute $C_j = P_j \oplus O_j$.
- **Step 6:** Transmit $C_j$ to the receiver. **The keystream block $O_j$ is NOT transmitted.**

### **Phase 4: Receiver-Side Decryption**
- **Step 7:** Receiver, having the same $K$ and $IV$, independently regenerates $O_j$ by computing $O_j = E_K(O_{j-1})$.
- **Step 8:** Compute $P_j = C_j \oplus O_j$ to recover the plaintext.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Asymmetry:**
> In OFB, the receiver must perform **exactly the same keystream generation** as the sender. This is why OFB is classified as a **synchronous stream cipher** — both ends must remain in lock-step synchrony.

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Step

| Step | What Happens | Why It Matters |
|------|--------------|----------------|
| $O_0 = IV$ | Initialize feedback loop | Provides the seed for the keystream generator. IV must be **unique per session** to ensure keystream uniqueness. |
| $O_j = E_K(O_{j-1})$ | Generate keystream block | Applying $E_K$ repeatedly creates a **pseudorandom sequence** of blocks. Since $E_K$ is a permutation on $b$-bit blocks, the sequence is periodic but has a very long period. |
| $C_j = P_j \oplus O_j$ | Encrypt plaintext | XOR with pseudorandom keystream provides **confusion and diffusion** at the bit level. |
| Receiver regenerates $O_j$ | Decrypt ciphertext | Both sides generate **identical keystreams** (synchrony), enabling XOR-based reversal. |

## 2.3 The "How" of Synchronous Stream Cipher Behavior

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why OFB is a Synchronous Stream Cipher:**
> - The keystream $O_1, O_2, O_3, \ldots$ is generated **independently of the plaintext and ciphertext**.
> - Both sender and receiver must generate the **exact same keystream sequence**.
> - If any bit is lost or altered in transmission, **synchronization is broken**, and the receiver cannot recover subsequent plaintext blocks correctly.
> - This requires **explicit resynchronization** mechanisms (e.g., inserting new IVs at intervals or using a resynchronization trigger).

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Master these equations — they appear in nearly every KTU OFB question.**

| # | Equation | Description | Condition |
|---|----------|-------------|-----------|
| 1 | $O_0 = IV$ | Initial output block | Always |
| 2 | $O_j = E_K(O_{j-1})$ | Keystream generation | $j \geq 1$ |
| 3 | $C_j = P_j \oplus O_j$ | Encryption | $j = 1, \ldots, n$ |
| 4 | $P_j = C_j \oplus O_j$ | Decryption | $j = 1, \ldots, n$ |
| 5 | $P_j = C_j \oplus E_K(O_{j-1})$ | Decryption (expanded) | Using same $O_j$ generation |
| 6 | $\text{Keystream period} \leq 2^b$ | Maximum keystream cycle | Theoretical bound |
| 7 | $\text{Keystream period} \geq 2^{b/2}$ | Expected period | With high-quality block cipher |
| 8 | $O_j \in \{0,1\}^b$ | Output block size | Equal to block size |
| 9 | $IV \in \{0,1\}^b$ | IV size | Equal to block size |
| 10 | $O_j \neq O_0$ | Period detection | If repetition occurs, cycle detected |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mistake:**
> Students often write $O_j = E_K(P_{j-1})$ or $O_j = E_K(C_{j-1})$ — these are **WRONG** for OFB. The feedback input is the **previous OUTPUT block** $O_{j-1}$, not the plaintext or ciphertext.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application Domain | How OFB is Used |
|--------------------|-----------------|
