| **Satellite Communications** | OFB's lack of error propagation makes it suitable for noisy channels, provided resynchronization is managed. |
| **Voice/Video Streaming (VoIP)** | Synchronous stream cipher behavior enables real-time encryption of continuous media streams. |
| **Database Encryption (Record-Level)** | Each record can be encrypted with a unique IV, and the keystream can be generated on-the-fly without chaining dependencies. |
| **Wireless Protocols (Historical)** | Used in some legacy systems (e.g., OFB mode in OpenSSL) where stream-cipher behavior is desired from a block cipher. |
| **Pre-computation of Keystreams** | Since keystream depends only on $K$ and $IV$ (not on plaintext), it can be **precomputed in advance** and stored, enabling faster real-time encryption. |
| **File Encryption (Large Files)** | Allows random access decryption without needing to process all preceding blocks (unlike CBC or CFB). |

## 2.6 The Keystream Period Problem

Since $E_K$ is a deterministic function from $b$-bit input to $b$-bit output, the sequence $O_0, O_1, O_2, \ldots$ must eventually **repeat**. The number of unique values before repetition is the **period** of the keystream.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Period Bound:**
> The period of the OFB keystream is at most $2^b$ (the number of possible $b$-bit values). With a well-designed block cipher and random IV, the expected period is approximately $2^{b-1}$ on average. For AES-128 ($b = 128$), this period is astronomically large ($2^{127}$ on average), making collisions practically impossible for typical message sizes.
> **Critical Engineering Insight:**
> NIST recommends limiting the total number of plaintext blocks encrypted under a single $K$ and $IV$ pair to **no more than $2^{b/2}$ blocks** to minimize the probability of keystream collision. For AES-128, this is $2^{64}$ blocks, which is sufficient for virtually all practical applications.

## 2.7 Comparison with Other Modes (High-Yield for KTU)

| Property | ECB | CBC | CFB | **OFB** | CTR |
|----------|-----|-----|-----|---------|-----|
| **Feedback type** | None | Ciphertext | Ciphertext | **Output of $E_K$** | Counter |
| **Keystream dependence** | N/A | Plaintext-dependent | Ciphertext-dependent | **Independent** | Independent |
| **Error propagation** | None (per block) | 1 block + 1 bit | 1 block + $\lceil b/s \rceil$ bits | **None** (synchrony loss) | None (synchrony loss) |
| **Stream cipher?** | No | No | No (self-sync) | **Yes (sync)** | Yes (sync) |
| **Random access** | Yes | No (decrypt) | No | **Yes** | Yes |
| **Pre-computation** | No | No | No | **Yes** | Yes |
| **IV collision risk** | N/A | Catastrophic | Catastrophic | **Catastrophic** | Catastrophic |
| **Parallelizable (enc)** | Yes | No | No | **No** | Yes |
| **Parallelizable (dec)** | Yes | Yes | Yes | **No** | Yes |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Hot Spot:**
> Questions asking "Compare OFB and CFB" or "Why is OFB preferred over CFB for noisy channels?" are **extremely common**. The answer revolves around **error propagation**: in CFB, a single bit error corrupts one full block and one additional bit in the next; in OFB, a single bit error only corrupts one bit in the corresponding plaintext block (since keystream generation is independent of ciphertext).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Proof that OFB Decryption Recovers the Original Plaintext

**Given:**
- $O_0 = IV$
- $O_j = E_K(O_{j-1})$ for $j \geq 1$
- $C_j = P_j \oplus O_j$ for $j \geq 1$

**To Prove:** $P_j = C_j \oplus O_j$ recovers the original plaintext block.

**Step-by-Step Derivation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Decryption Operation:} \quad P_j' &= C_j \oplus O_j \\
\text{Substitute the encryption equation } C_j = P_j \oplus O_j: \quad P_j' &= (P_j \oplus O_j) \oplus O_j \\
\text{Apply the associativity of XOR:} \quad P_j' &= P_j \oplus (O_j \oplus O_j) \\
\text{Apply the self-inverse property of XOR (} x \oplus x = 0 \text{):} \quad P_j' &= P_j \oplus 0 \\
\text{Apply the identity property of XOR (} x \oplus 0 = x \text{):} \quad P_j' &= P_j
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** $\boxed{P_j' = P_j}$, confirming that OFB decryption correctly recovers the original plaintext. $\blacksquare$

> [!NOTE]
> This derivation uses three fundamental properties of XOR:
> 1. **Commutativity:** $A \oplus B = B \oplus A$
> 2. **Associativity:** $(A \oplus B) \oplus C = A \oplus (B \oplus C)$
> 3. **Self-Inverse:** $A \oplus A = 0$
> 4. **Identity:** $A \oplus 0 = A$

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Keystream Block Independence from Plaintext

**Claim:** The keystream block $O_j$ depends **only on** $K$ and the IV, not on any plaintext block.

**Derivation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
O_0 &= IV \quad \text{(depends only on IV)} \\
O_1 &= E_K(O_0) = E_K(IV) \quad \text{(depends only on } K \text{ and } IV) \\
O_2 &= E_K(O_1) = E_K(E_K(IV)) \quad \text{(depends only on } K \text{ and } IV) \\
O_j &= E_K(O_{j-1}) = E_K^{(j)}(IV) \quad \text{(} j \text{-fold composition of } E_K \text{ on } IV)
\end{aligned}
$$

By induction, every $O_j$ is a deterministic function of $K$ and $IV$ only, **completely independent of the plaintext blocks** $P_1, P_2, \ldots, P_n$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **mathematical foundation** for why OFB is a synchronous stream cipher and why its keystream can be precomputed.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Bit Error Propagation Analysis

**Scenario:** A single bit error occurs in ciphertext block $C_j$ during transmission. Specifically, bit position $k$ is flipped.

**Let** $\Delta C_j$ = error vector with a single 1 at position $k$ and 0 elsewhere.

**Receiver's recovered plaintext:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_j' &= C_j' \oplus O_j \\
C_j' &= C_j \oplus \Delta C_j \\
P_j' &= (C_j \oplus \Delta C_j) \oplus O_j \\
P_j' &= (P_j \oplus O_j \oplus \Delta C_j) \oplus O_j \\
P_j' &= P_j \oplus (O_j \oplus O_j) \oplus \Delta C_j \\
P_j' &= P_j \oplus \Delta C_j
\end{aligned}
$$

**For block $j+1$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{j+1}' &= C_{j+1} \oplus O_{j+1}
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $O_{j+1} = E_K(O_j)$ depends on $O_j$, and $O_j$ is generated **independently of $C_j$**, the error in $C_j$ does **not affect the keystream**. Therefore:

$$P_{j+1}' = P_{j+1} \quad \text{(no error propagation to next block)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** In OFB mode, a bit error in $C_j$ causes a **single bit error** in the recovered $P_j$ (at the same bit position) and **NO error in subsequent blocks**. This is the **key advantage of OFB over CFB** in noisy channels.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: OFB vs. CFB vs. CTR vs. CBC

| Property | **OFB** | CFB | CTR | CBC |
|----------|---------|-----|-----|-----|
| **Error in $C_j$ affects:** | $P_j$ only (1 bit) | $P_j$ (all bits) + $P_{j+1}$ (1 bit) | $P_j$ only (1 bit) | $P_j$ (all bits) + $P_{j+1}$ (all bits) |
| **Synchrony required?** | **Yes** | No (self-sync) | Yes | No |
| **Recover from lost block?** | No (resync needed) | Yes (after $b$ blocks) | No (resync needed) | No |
| **IV reuse catastrophe?** | Keystream reuse → two-time pad | Keystream reuse | Counter reuse | First block XOR collision |
| **Encryption uses $E_K$ or $D_K$?** | $E_K$ only | $E_K$ only | $E_K$ only | $E_K$ (for enc), $D_K$ (for dec) |
| **Decryption uses $E_K$ or $D_K$?** | $E_K$ (for keystream) | $E_K$ (for keystream) | $E_K$ (for keystream) | $D_K$ (for data) |

## 3.5 Numerical Worked Example (KTU Board Style)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Setup (typical KTU 2-mark or 7-mark question):**
> Suppose we have a block cipher with block size $b = 4$ bits, and the key-stream generation is given by:
> $O_0 = IV = 1010$, $O_j = E_K(O_{j-1})$, where $E_K$ is defined by the table: $0000 \to 1110$, $0001 \to 0100$, $0010 \to 1101$, $0011 \to 0001$, $0100 \to 0010$, $0101 \to 0111$, $0110 \to 1010$, $0111 \to 1000$, $1000 \to 0011$, $1001 \to 1011$, $1010 \to 1100$, $1011 \to 1111$, $1100 \to 0110$, $1101 \to 1001$, $1110 \to 0000$, $1111 \to 0101$.

**Given:** Plaintext $P_1 = 1100$, $P_2 = 0110$.

**Step 1: Compute $O_1$**

$$
\begin{aligned}
O_1 &= E_K(O_0) = E_K(1010) = 1100
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Compute $C_1$**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_1 &= P_1 \oplus O_1 = 1100 \oplus 1100 = 0000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Compute $O_2$**

$$
\begin{aligned}
O_2 &= E_K(O_1) = E_K(1100) = 0110
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Compute $C_2$**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_2 &= P_2 \oplus O_2 = 0110 \oplus 0110 = 0000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Verify Decryption**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_1 &= C_1 \oplus O_1 = 0000 \oplus 1100 = 1100 \quad \checkmark \\
P_2 &= C_2 \oplus O_2 = 0000 \oplus 0110 = 0110 \quad \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examination Tip:** When solving numerical OFB problems, always:
> 1. List the keystream equations first.
> 2. Compute $O_1, O_2, \ldots$ sequentially.
> 3. Apply XOR with plaintext to get ciphertext.
> 4. For decryption, regenerate the same keystream and XOR with ciphertext.

## 3.6 Security Analysis: The Catastrophe of IV Reuse

> [!WARNING]
> **The Two-Time Pad Problem in OFB:**
> If the **same $(K, IV)$ pair** is used to encrypt **two different messages**, the keystream blocks $O_1, O_2, \ldots$ will be **identical** for both messages. This creates a classical two-time pad vulnerability.

**Derivation of the attack:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_j^{(1)} &= P_j^{(1)} \oplus O_j \\
C_j^{(2)} &= P_j^{(2)} \oplus O_j \\
C_j^{(1)} \oplus C_j^{(2)} &= (P_j^{(1)} \oplus O_j) \oplus (P_j^{(2)} \oplus O_j) \\
C_j^{(1)} \oplus C_j^{(2)} &= P_j^{(1)} \oplus P_j^{(2)}
\end{aligned}
$$

The attacker obtains $P_j^{(1)} \oplus P_j^{(2)}$ — the **XOR of the two plaintexts** — which leaks significant information (e.g., if one plaintext is known, the other is trivially recovered, or statistical attacks become possible).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Rule:** Never reuse the same $(K, IV)$ pair. The IV must be **unique and unpredictable** for every encryption session under the same key.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 OFB Mode Encryption Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    IV["IV / O sub 0"] --> E1["E sub K Block Cipher"]
    E1 --> O1["O sub 1 Keystream Block 1"]
    O1 --> XOR1((XOR))
    P1["P sub 1 Plaintext Block 1"] --> XOR1
    XOR1 --> C1["C sub 1 Ciphertext Block 1"]
    O1 --> E2["E sub K Block Cipher"]
    E2 --> O2["O sub 2 Keystream Block 2"]
    O2 --> XOR2((XOR))
    P2["P sub 2 Plaintext Block 2"] --> XOR2
    XOR2 --> C2["C sub 2 Ciphertext Block 2"]
    O2 --> E3["E sub K Block Cipher"]
    E3 --> O3["O sub 3 Keystream Block 3"]
    O3 --> XOR3((XOR))
    P3["P sub 3 Plaintext Block 3"] --> XOR3
    XOR3 --> C3["C sub 3 Ciphertext Block 3"]

    style IV fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style E1 fill:#FF6B6B,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style E2 fill:#FF6B6B,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style E3 fill:#FF6B6B,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style O1 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style O2 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style O3 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style XOR1 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:3px
    style XOR2 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:3px
    style XOR3 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:3px
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Reading Guide:**
> - **Yellow node** = Initialization Vector (initial seed)
> - **Red nodes** = Block cipher encryption function $E_K$
> - **Blue nodes** = Keystream output blocks $O_j$
> - **Green nodes** = XOR combination points
> - Notice how the **feedback loop goes through $O_{j-1} \to E_K \to O_j$**, completely bypassing the plaintext and ciphertext.

## 4.2 OFB Mode Decryption Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    IV["IV / O sub 0"] --> E1["E sub K Block Cipher"]
    E1 --> O1["O sub 1 Keystream Block 1"]
    O1 --> DXOR1((XOR))
    C1["C sub 1 Ciphertext Block 1"] --> DXOR1
    DXOR1 --> P1["P sub 1 Plaintext Block 1"]
    O1 --> E2["E sub K Block Cipher"]
    E2 --> O2["O sub 2 Keystream Block 2"]
    O2 --> DXOR2((XOR))
    C2["C sub 2 Ciphertext Block 2"] --> DXOR2
    DXOR2 --> P2["P sub 2 Plaintext Block 2"]
    O2 --> E3["E sub K Block Cipher"]
    E3 --> O3["O sub 3 Keystream Block 3"]
    O3 --> DXOR3((XOR))
    C3["C sub 3 Ciphertext Block 3"] --> DXOR3
    DXOR3 --> P3["P sub 3 Plaintext Block 3"]

    style IV fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style E1 fill:#FF6B6B,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style E2 fill:#FF6B6B,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style E3 fill:#FF6B6B,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style O1 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style O2 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style O3 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style DXOR1 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:3px
    style DXOR2 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:3px
    style DXOR3 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:3px
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Observation:**
> The **decryption diagram is structurally identical** to the encryption diagram, except that the **plaintext and ciphertext roles are swapped at the XOR gates**. The keystream generation (top loop) is **exactly the same** in both diagrams. This is a hallmark of synchronous stream ciphers.

## 4.3 OFB Keystream Generation (Isolated Feedback Loop)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Seed["IV = O sub 0"]:::seed
    EncA["E sub K"]:::enc
    OutA["O sub 1"]:::out
    EncB["E sub K"]:::enc
    OutB["O sub 2"]:::out
    EncC["E sub K"]:::enc
    OutC["O sub 3"]:::out
    EncD["..."]:::enc
    OutD["O sub n"]:::out

    Seed --> EncA
    EncA --> OutA
    OutA --> EncB
    EncB --> OutB
    OutB --> EncC
    EncC --> OutC
    OutC --> EncD
    EncD --> OutD

    classDef seed fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    classDef enc fill:#FF6B6B,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    classDef out fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

> [!NOTE]
> This isolated diagram highlights the **pure feedback structure** of OFB keystream generation: $O_0 \xrightarrow{E_K} O_1 \xrightarrow{E_K} O_2 \xrightarrow{E_K} O_3 \xrightarrow{E_K} \cdots$

## 4.4 Comparative Block Diagram: OFB vs. CFB Feedback Paths

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph OFB_Mode["OFB MODE Output Feedback"]
        direction LR
        OFB_IV["IV"] --> OFB_Enc1["E sub K"]
        OFB_Enc1 --> OFB_O1["O sub 1"]
        OFB_O1 --> OFB_XOR1((XOR))
        OFB_P1["P sub 1"] --> OFB_XOR1
        OFB_XOR1 --> OFB_C1["C sub 1"]
        OFB_O1 -.-> OFB_Enc1
    end

    subgraph CFB_Mode["CFB MODE Ciphertext Feedback"]
        direction LR
        CFB_IV["IV"] --> CFB_Enc1["E sub K"]
        CFB_Enc1 --> CFB_O1["O sub 1"]
        CFB_O1 --> CFB_XOR1((XOR))
        CFB_P1["P sub 1"] --> CFB_XOR1
        CFB_XOR1 --> CFB_C1["C sub 1"]
        CFB_C1 -.-> CFB_Enc1
    end

    style OFB_Enc1 fill:#FF6B6B,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style CFB_Enc1 fill:#FF6B6B,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style OFB_O1 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style CFB_O1 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Visual Comparison Insight:**
> - In **OFB**, the feedback (dotted arrow) comes from the **output block $O_1$** back to the input of $E_K$.
> - In **CFB**, the feedback (dotted arrow) comes from the **ciphertext block $C_1$** back to the input of $E_K$.
> - This single architectural difference accounts for all behavioral differences between the two modes.

## 4.5 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow (Data + Control Path)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Control_Plane["CONTROL PLANE Keystream Generation Engine"]
        direction TB
        CP_IV["IV Register"] --> CP_Enc["E sub K Engine"]
        CP_Enc --> CP_Obuf["O sub j Register"]
        CP_Obuf --> CP_Feedback["Feedback Mux"]
        CP_Feedback --> CP_Enc
    end

    subgraph Data_Plane["DATA PLANE Encryption Decryption Engine"]
        direction TB
        DP_Pin["Plaintext Input P sub j"] --> DP_XOR((XOR))
        DP_Keystream["Keystream O sub j from Control Plane"] --> DP_XOR
        DP_XOR --> DP_Cout["Ciphertext Output C sub j"]
    end

    CP_Obuf -. "Provides O sub j" .-> DP_Keystream

    style CP_Enc fill:#FF6B6B,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style CP_IV fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style CP_Obuf fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style DP_XOR fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:3px
```

> [!NOTE]
> This block-level architecture map illustrates the **separation of concerns** in OFB: the keystream generation (control plane) is completely decoupled from the data encryption (data plane). The only interaction is the **delivery of $O_j$ from the control plane to the data plane's XOR operation**.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**"Define Output Feedback (OFB) mode of operation. Why is it classified as a synchronous stream cipher?"**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):**
Output Feedback (OFB) Mode is a confidentiality mode of operation for block ciphers in which a keystream is generated by repeatedly encrypting an Initialization Vector (IV) under the secret key $K$. The keystream blocks are then XORed with the plaintext blocks to produce ciphertext. The defining feature is that the feedback comes from the **output of $E_K$**, not from the ciphertext.

Formally:
- $O_0 = IV$
- $O_j = E_K(O_{j-1})$ for $j \geq 1$
- $C_j = P_j \oplus O_j$

**Synchronous Stream Cipher Classification (1 mark):**
OFB is classified as a **synchronous stream cipher** because:
1. The keystream $O_1, O_2, \ldots$ is generated **independently of the plaintext and ciphertext**.
2. Both sender and receiver must generate the **identical keystream sequence**.
3. If synchronization is lost, the receiver cannot decrypt correctly without **explicit resynchronization**.

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**"List any three advantages of OFB mode over CBC mode."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **No error propagation (1 mark):** In OFB, a bit error in one ciphertext block affects only the corresponding plaintext block (a single bit flip). In CBC, an error in $C_j$ corrupts all of $P_j$ and all of $P_{j+1}$.

2. **Pre-computation of keystream (1 mark):** Since the keystream in OFB depends only on $K$ and $IV$ (not on plaintext), it can be **precomputed and stored** before the plaintext is available. CBC requires the plaintext to compute the ciphertext.

3. **Random access decryption (1 mark):** In OFB, to decrypt $C_j$, only $O_j$ is needed, which can be generated independently of all other blocks. In CBC, decrypting $C_j$ requires $C_{j-1}$, necessitating sequential processing.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Adapted]`

**(a)** Explain the Output Feedback (OFB) mode of operation with a neat block diagram. Derive the equations for encryption and decryption. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare OFB mode with CFB mode with respect to: (i) feedback mechanism, (ii) error propagation, (iii) decryption function used. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Part (a):**

**Block Diagram (2 marks):**

The OFB mode encryption diagram shows the following flow:
- IV → $E_K$ → $O_1$ → $E_K$ → $O_2$ → $E_K$ → $O_3$ → ...
- Each $O_j$ is XORed with the corresponding $P_j$ to produce $C_j$.
- The feedback path takes $O_{j-1}$ back as input to $E_K$.

```
IV → [E_K] → O_1 → [E_K] → O_2 → [E_K] → O_3 → ...
              ↓                ↓                ↓
         P_1 ⊕           P_2 ⊕           P_3 ⊕
              ↓                ↓                ↓
             C_1              C_2              C_3
```

**Equations (3 marks):**

$$O_0 = IV \quad \text{(Initial seed)}$$

$$O_j = E_K(O_{j-1}) \quad \text{for } j = 1, 2, \ldots, n \quad \text{(Keystream generation)}$$

$$C_j = P_j \oplus O_j \quad \text{for } j = 1, 2, \ldots, n \quad \text{(Encryption)}$$

**Decryption (2 marks):**

Decryption is performed by regenerating the same keystream and XORing with ciphertext:

$$O_j = E_K(O_{j-1}) \quad \text{(same keystream generation)}$$

$$P_j = C_j \oplus O_j \quad \text{for } j = 1, 2, \ldots, n \quad \text{(Decryption)}$$

Note that **decryption also uses $E_K$** (not $D_K$) because the block cipher is only applied to generate the keystream, not to process the data.

---

#### **Model Solution for Part (b):**

**(i) Feedback Mechanism (2.5 marks):**

| Aspect | **OFB** | CFB |
|--------|---------|-----|
| **Feedback input** | Previous **output block** $O_{j-1}$ | Previous **ciphertext block** $C_{j-1}$ |
| **Feedback equation** | $O_j = E_K(O_{j-1})$ | $O_j = E_K(C_{j-1})$ |
| **Keystream dependence** | Independent of plaintext & ciphertext | Depends on ciphertext (which depends on plaintext) |

**(ii) Error Propagation (2.5 marks):**

| Aspect | **OFB** | CFB |
|--------|---------|-----|
| **Bit error in $C_j$** | Corrupts only 1 bit in $P_j$ (no propagation) | Corrupts all of $P_j$ and 1 bit of $P_{j+1}$ |
| **Lost/synchronized block** | Destroys all subsequent decryption (sync loss) | Self-synchronizing after $b/s$ blocks |
| **Noisy channel suitability** | **Better** (no error amplification) | Worse (errors cascade) |

**(iii) Decryption Function Used (2 marks):**

| Aspect | **OFB** | CFB |
|--------|---------|-----|
| **Block cipher function** | Uses $E_K$ for both encryption and decryption | Uses $E_K$ for both encryption and decryption |
| **Reason** | Block cipher only generates keystream; data path uses XOR | Same reason — keystream generation uses $E_K$ only |

**Key Difference:** Both OFB and CFB use $E_K$ (not $D_K$) for decryption. The distinction is in **what is fed back** (output vs. ciphertext) and **how errors propagate** (no propagation vs. limited propagation).

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Adapted]`

**(a)** With a suitable diagram, describe the OFB mode of operation. Explain why the Initialization Vector (IV) must be unique for each encryption session. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Consider AES-128 in OFB mode encrypting a 512-bit message. The block size is $b = 128$ bits. Determine: (i) the number of plaintext blocks, (ii) the number of block cipher invocations, (iii) the number of XOR operations, and (iv) whether the keystream can be precomputed. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Part (a):**

**Diagram (2 marks):**

The block diagram is identical to that described in Question A part (a). The key structural elements are:
- IV feeds into the first $E_K$ invocation.
- Each $O_j$ feeds into the next $E_K$ invocation (output feedback loop).
- Each $O_j$ is XORed with $P_j$ to produce $C_j$.

**Description (3 marks):**

In OFB mode, the block cipher $E_K$ is used to generate a pseudorandom keystream by repeatedly encrypting the IV. The keystream blocks $O_1, O_2, \ldots, O_n$ are independent of the plaintext. The ciphertext is computed as $C_j = P_j \oplus O_j$. The receiver regenerates the keystream using the same $K$ and $IV$ and recovers $P_j = C_j \oplus O_j$.

**Why IV Must Be Unique (2 marks):**

If the **same $(K, IV)$ pair** is used for two different messages $M^{(1)}$ and $M^{(2)}$, the keystream $O_1, O_2, \ldots$ will be **identical** for both encryptions. This means:

$$C_j^{(1)} \oplus C_j^{(2)} = P_j^{(1)} \oplus P_j^{(2)}$$

The attacker can compute the **XOR of the two plaintexts**, which is a catastrophic information leak. This is the classical **two-time pad attack**. To prevent this, the IV must be **unique (and ideally unpredictable)** for every encryption session under the same key.

---

#### **Model Solution for Part (b):**

**Given:**
- Algorithm: AES-128 in OFB mode
- Block size: $b = 128$ bits
- Message size: 512 bits

**(i) Number of plaintext blocks (2 marks):**

$$n = \frac{\text{Message size}}{\text{Block size}} = \frac{512}{128} = 4 \text{ blocks}$$

**(ii) Number of block cipher invocations (2 marks):**

In OFB mode, $E_K$ is invoked once per block to generate the keystream:
- 1 invocation to generate $O_1$ (from IV)
- 1 invocation to generate $O_2$ (from $O_1$)
- 1 invocation to generate $O_3$ (from $O_2$)
- 1 invocation to generate $O_4$ (from $O_3$)

$$\text{Total invocations} = n = 4$$

**(iii) Number of XOR operations (1.5 marks):**

One XOR per plaintext block to produce ciphertext:
$$\text{Total XOR operations} = n = 4$$

**(iv) Can the keystream be precomputed? (1.5 marks):**

**Yes**, the keystream can be fully precomputed **before** the plaintext is available. This is because $O_j = E_K(O_{j-1})$ depends only on $K$ and $IV$, not on the plaintext. The sender can generate $O_1, O_2, O_3, O_4$ in advance and store them. When the plaintext arrives, only the 4 XOR operations are needed for real-time encryption.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Confusing OFB with CFB (–2 marks):** Students frequently write $O_j = E_K(C_{j-1})$ for OFB. This is the CFB equation. For OFB, it is $O_j = E_K(O_{j-1})$.
> 2. **Writing $D_K$ for decryption (–1 mark):** OFB decryption uses $E_K$ to regenerate the keystream, not $D_K$. Only the XOR operation "reverses" the data.
> 3. **Forgetting $O_0 = IV$ (–1 mark):** Always state the initial condition explicitly.
> 4. **Missing the IV uniqueness requirement (–1 to –2 marks):** Any discussion of OFB security MUST mention that IV reuse leads to keystream reuse and the two-time pad attack.
> 5. **Confusing "error propagation" with "synchronization loss" (–1 mark):** OFB has **no error propagation** but is **sensitive to synchronization loss**. These are different concepts.
> 6. **Incorrect block count calculation (–1 mark):** For a 512-bit message with 128-bit blocks, there are exactly **4 blocks**, not 5 (which would imply padding) and not 3 (which would leave 128 bits unencrypted).

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for Output Feedback Mode**

- **Definition:** OFB mode generates a keystream by repeatedly encrypting the IV under key $K$, then XORs the keystream with plaintext.
- **Core Equations:**
  - $O_0 = IV$
  - $O_j = E_K(O_{j-1})$ for $j \geq 1$
  - $C_j = P_j \oplus O_j$ (encryption)
  - $P_j = C_j \oplus O_j$ (decryption)
- **Feedback Source:** The output block $O_{j-1}$ is fed back (NOT ciphertext, NOT plaintext).
- **Synchronous Stream Cipher:** OFB is a synchronous stream cipher — both ends must stay in lock-step.
- **Decryption Uses $E_K$:** The block cipher's encryption function is used for both encryption and decryption (only the data path uses XOR).
- **No Error Propagation:** A bit error in $C_j$ affects only 1 bit in $P_j$; no cascading errors.
- **Synchronization Loss:** If a ciphertext block is lost, all subsequent blocks are corrupted. Resynchronization is required.
- **Keystream Period:** The keystream is periodic with period at most $2^b$. NIST recommends limiting encryption to at most $2^{b/2}$ blocks per $(K, IV)$ pair.
- **IV Requirements:**
  - **Unique** for every encryption session under the same key.
  - **Unpredictable** (ideally from a CSPRNG).
  - **Size = block size** $b$.
  - **Not secret** (can be transmitted in plaintext).
- **IV Reuse Catastrophe:** Reusing $(K, IV)$ creates a two-time pad, leaking $P_j^{(1)} \oplus P_j^{(2)}$.
- **Pre-computation:** Keystream can be precomputed since it depends only on $K$ and $IV$.
- **Random Access:** Any $C_j$ can be decrypted independently by generating $O_j$ on demand.
- **Not Parallelizable (Enc or Dec):** The sequential dependency $O_j = E_K(O_{j-1})$ prevents parallelization of keystream generation.
- **Comparison with CFB:** OFB feeds back output; CFB feeds back ciphertext. OFB has no error propagation; CFB has limited error propagation.
- **Comparison with CTR:** CTR uses a counter as input to $E_K$; OFB uses the previous output. CTR is parallelizable; OFB is not.
- **Comparison with CBC:** OFB has no error propagation and allows random access; CBC has error propagation and requires sequential decryption.
- **Block Cipher Invocations per Block:** Exactly 1 invocation of $E_K$ per block of keystream generated.
- **Real-World Uses:** Satellite comms, VoIP, database encryption, pre-computed keystream scenarios.
- **Common KTU Mistake:** Writing $O_j = E_K(C_{j-1})$ (this is CFB, not OFB).
- **Variable-Length OFB (OFB-s):** Uses $s < b$ bits per step; converts OFB to a self-synchronizing stream cipher (rarely used in practice).
- **FIPS Approval:** OFB mode is approved in FIPS 81 and NIST SP 800-38A.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECAT595 - FOUNDATIONS OF SECURITY IN COMPUTING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Counter Mode. Advanced Encryption Standard (AES) - Basic Structure

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 2 | COUNTER MODE & ADVANCED ENCRYPTION STANDARD (AES)

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Counter (CTR) Mode — Formal Definition

**Counter (CTR) Mode** is a block cipher mode of operation in which a *counter* (a deterministic, sequentially incrementing value) is encrypted to produce a one-time **keystream block**, which is then XORed with a plaintext block to generate the corresponding ciphertext block. CTR mode effectively transforms a deterministic block cipher (e.g., AES) into a **synchronous stream cipher**, enabling **parallel encryption/decryption** and providing **randomized access** to any block in the ciphertext stream without sequential dependency.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** CTR mode is the most widely deployed block cipher mode in modern protocols (TLS 1.3, IPsec, SSH, AES-GCM) because of its **parallelizability**, **provable security (IND-CPA)**, and **no padding requirement**.

Formally, for plaintext block $P_i$ and counter $T_i$:

$$C_i = P_i \oplus E_K(T_i)$$

where $E_K(\cdot)$ is the block cipher (e.g., AES-128) under secret key $K$, and $T_i$ is the $i$-th counter value.

### 1.2 Advanced Encryption Standard (AES) — Formal Definition

The **Advanced Encryption Standard (AES)** is a symmetric-key block cipher standardized by the **National Institute of Standards and Technology (NIST)** in **FIPS PUB 197 (2001)**. AES is a **Substitution-Permutation Network (SPN)** cipher that operates on a fixed **128-bit block size** and supports key lengths of **128, 192, or 256 bits**, with the number of rounds being **10, 12, or 14** respectively. AES was selected from the Rijndael algorithm (designed by Joan Daemen and Vincent Rijmen) after a five-year international competition.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **CTR Mode Analogy — "The Numbered Stamp Factory":**
> Imagine a factory that produces unique, numbered stamps. Each stamp is made by encrypting a unique counter number (1, 2, 3, …) with a master secret die. The worker takes the first stamp, presses it on the first piece of paper (plaintext block) using XOR, the second stamp on the second paper, and so on. Because each stamp is **independent** of every other, multiple workers can press stamps in **parallel** on different papers. The receiver, knowing the counter sequence and the master die, regenerates the same stamp sequence and "unpresses" them to recover the original paper.

> [!NOTE]
> **AES Analogy — "The 10-Layer Lockbox Maze":**
> Picture a 10-floor lockbox maze. The plaintext enters the ground floor as a 4×4 grid of 16 cells. Each floor scrambles the cells in a different way (substitute → shift rows → mix columns → XOR with floor-key). After passing through 10 such floors, the plaintext is so thoroughly scrambled that even supercomputers would need billions of years to reverse it without the key. The "10 floors" grow to 12 or 14 if you use a 192-bit or 256-bit key.

### 1.4 Key Terminology & Physical Constants

| Term | Standard Value (KTU-Exam-Relevant) |
|---|---|
| AES Block Size | **128 bits (16 bytes)** |
| AES-128 Key | **128 bits (16 bytes)** — 10 rounds |
| AES-192 Key | **192 bits (24 bytes)** — 12 rounds |
| AES-256 Key | **256 bits (32 bytes)** — 14 rounds |
| AES State | **4 × 4 byte matrix** (Nb = 4) |
| CTR Counter Size | Typically **64–128 bits** |
| AES S-Box | **16 × 16 lookup table**, entries in GF(2⁸) |
| AES Irreducible Polynomial | **$m(x) = x^8 + x^4 + x^3 + x + 1$** |

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic for AES key→rounds mapping:** *"10-12-14, easy as A-B-C"*: AES-**128** → **10** rounds, AES-**192** → **12** rounds, AES-**256** → **14** rounds.

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Counter (CTR) Mode — Operational Breakdown

CTR mode converts a block cipher into a stream cipher. The cryptographic core is the encryption of a *counter block* $T_i$ under the secret key $K$. The keystream is then XORed with the plaintext.

### Step-by-Step Logic (Why & How)

1. **Initialization:** A unique **nonce** (number-used-once) is combined with an initial counter value $ctr_0$ to form the first counter block $T_1$. This guarantees **uniqueness across all messages** under the same key.
2. **Counter Increment:** For each subsequent block, the counter is incremented: $T_{i+1} = T_i + 1 \pmod{2^n}$ where $n$ is the counter width (often 64 or 128 bits).
3. **Block Cipher Encryption:** Each $T_i$ is independently encrypted: $O_i = E_K(T_i)$. Because the operation depends only on $T_i$ (not on $P_i$ or $C_i$), this step is **fully parallelizable**.
4. **XOR with Plaintext:** The keystream output $O_i$ is XORed with the plaintext block to produce the ciphertext: $C_i = P_i \oplus O_i$.
5. **Decryption Symmetry:** Decryption is **identical** to encryption: $P_i = C_i \oplus E_K(T_i)$, since XOR is its own inverse.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Security Property — Nonce Reuse Catastrophe:**
> If the same $(K, T_i)$ pair is ever used to encrypt two different plaintexts, an attacker trivially recovers $P_i \oplus P'_i = C_i \oplus C'_i$, leaking the **XOR of the two plaintexts**. This is the most common CTR-mode failure in production systems.

### 2.2 Counter Block Construction

| Construction | Formula | Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| Standard NIST CTR | $T_i = \text{nonce} \,\|\, (ctr_0 + i)$ | IPsec, SP 800-38A |
| Random IV with split | $T_i = \text{random}_{64} \,\|\, (i)$ | TLS-style usage |
| GCM-Specific | $T_i = J_0 + i$ | AES-GCM authenticated encryption |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why CTR mode is IND-CPA Secure (high-level):**
> As long as all $E_K(T_i)$ outputs are unique (guaranteed by unique counters), each $O_i$ is essentially a **one-time pad** of length 128 bits. Information-theoretically, an attacker gains zero information about $P_i$ given only $C_i$ and a uniform-random $O_i$.

### 2.3 AES — High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Concept | Equation / Rule | Engineering Utility |
|---|---|---|
| Block size | $B = 128$ bits | Fixed for all AES variants |
| Round count $N_r$ | $N_r = 6 + \max(N_k, N_b)$ where $N_b=4$ | Determines cipher strength |
| Word size | 8 bits (byte) | State = 4×4 byte matrix |
| Key schedule size | $N_b(N_r+1)$ words = $4(N_r+1) \cdot 4$ bytes | Pre-computed for all rounds |
| AddRoundKey | $s_{i,j} \leftarrow s_{i,j} \oplus k_{i,j}$ | Bitwise XOR with round key |
| SubBytes | $s_{i,j} \leftarrow \text{S-Box}[s_{i,j}]$ | Non-linear substitution |
| ShiftRows | Row $r$ rotated left by $r$ bytes | Inter-column diffusion |
| MixColumns | $\mathbf{s}'(x) = c(x) \cdot \mathbf{s}(x) \pmod{x^4+1}$ | Intra-column mixing |
| GF(2⁸) reduction | $m(x) = x^8 + x^4 + x^3 + x + 1$ | Field polynomial |
| MixColumns constant | $c(x) = \texttt{03}x^3 + \texttt{01}x^2 + \texttt{01}x + \texttt{02}$ | Fixed polynomial in GF(2⁸) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip — Round Count Formula:**
> $N_r = 6 + \max(N_k, N_b)$ where $N_b = 4$ always.
> - $N_k = 4$ (128-bit key) → $N_r = 6 + 4 = 10$
> - $N_k = 6$ (192-bit key) → $N_r = 6 + 6 = 12$
> - $N_k = 8$ (256-bit key) → $N_r = 6 + 8 = 14$

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **TLS 1.3:** Mandates **AES-GCM** (CTR + GHASH authentication) for nearly all cipher suites.
- **Disk Encryption:** AES-CTR underpins **BitLocker** and **VeraCrypt** full-disk encryption.
- **Wireless Security:** **WPA3** uses **AES-256-GCM** (CCMP-256).
- **Voting / Smart Cards:** CTR mode is preferred because it allows **random-access decryption** of any block.
- **Hardware Acceleration:** Modern CPUs (Intel AES-NI, ARMv8 Cryptographic Extensions) provide **single-cycle AES round instructions**, making CTR mode the fastest secure option.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 CTR Mode — Worked Example with AES-128

### Setup
- **Key** $K$ = (kept symbolic)
- **Nonce** = `0xA1B2C3D4` (32 bits)
- **Initial counter** $ctr_0$ = `0x0000000000000001` (64 bits)
- **Plaintext** $P$ = "COUNTERMODE" (split into 128-bit blocks)

> [!NOTE]
> For the purpose of illustration, we treat the counter block $T_i$ as 128 bits and the encryption $E_K(T_i)$ as a deterministic function returning a known keystream. The student should focus on the *structure* of the computation, not specific byte values.

### Block 1 Computation ($i = 1$)

Form the counter block:
$$T_1 = \text{nonce} \,\|\, ctr_0 = \texttt{0xA1B2C3D4 00000000 00000001}$$

Encrypt the counter under AES-128:
$$O_1 = E_K(T_1)$$

Suppose $O_1$ is given (for the sake of this derivation, take it as):
$$O_1 = \texttt{0x5A2E8F19 3C7B40D1 6E82A5C4 F0198B72}$$

XOR with plaintext block $P_1$ (first 16 bytes of "COUNTERMODE…" padded as needed):
$$C_1 = P_1 \oplus O_1$$

### Block 2 Computation ($i = 2$)

Increment the counter:
$$T_2 = T_1 + 1 = \texttt{0xA1B2C3D4 00000000 00000002}$$

Encrypt:
$$O_2 = E_K(T_2)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Observe that $T_1$ and $T_2$ differ by exactly one bit (a binary carry) in the low-order bit. This tiny change causes **every output bit** of $O_1$ and $O_2$ to be completely different — a property called the **avalanche effect** — guaranteed by AES's strong diffusion.

XOR with $P_2$:
$$C_2 = P_2 \oplus O_2$$

### Decryption Verification

Decryption is structurally identical:
$$P_i = C_i \oplus E_K(T_i)$$

We verify the algebra:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_i &= C_i \oplus E_K(T_i) \\
&= (P_i \oplus E_K(T_i)) \oplus E_K(T_i) \quad \text{(substituting } C_i \text{)} \\
&= P_i \oplus (E_K(T_i) \oplus E_K(T_i)) \quad \text{(XOR associativity)} \\
&= P_i \oplus 0 \\
&= P_i \quad \text{(XOR self-inverse)}
\end{aligned}
$$

✅ **Decryption recovers the original plaintext.**

## 3.2 AES — Detailed Round Structure (AES-128, 10 Rounds)

The AES encryption process for a 128-bit block proceeds as follows:

### Step 1: Initial Round (Round 0) — AddRoundKey Only

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{State} &\leftarrow P \quad \text{(plaintext loaded into 4×4 byte matrix, column-major)} \\
\text{State} &\leftarrow \text{State} \oplus \text{RoundKey}_0 \quad \text{(XOR with 0-th round key)}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2: Rounds 1 through 9 — Full Round Transformations

For each round $r = 1, 2, \dots, 9$:

**(a) SubBytes** — Non-linear byte substitution using the S-Box:
$$s'_{i,j} = S[s_{i,j}]$$

Each byte $s_{i,j}$ is replaced by its multiplicative inverse in $\text{GF}(2^8)$, followed by an affine transformation.

**(b) ShiftRows** — Cyclical left rotation of each row by its row index:
- Row 0: no shift
- Row 1: rotate left by 1 byte
- Row 2: rotate left by 2 bytes
- Row 3: rotate left by 3 bytes

**(c) MixColumns** — Matrix multiplication in $\text{GF}(2^8)$:

$$
\begin{bmatrix} s'_{0,j} \\ s'_{1,j} \\ s'_{2,j} \\ s'_{3,j} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 02 & 03 & 01 & 01 \\ 01 & 02 & 03 & 01 \\ 01 & 01 & 02 & 03 \\ 03 & 01 & 01 & 02 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} s_{0,j} \\ s_{1,j} \\ s_{2,j} \\ s_{3,j} \end{bmatrix} \pmod{m(x)}
$$

**(d) AddRoundKey** — XOR state with round key $W_{4r \dots 4r+3}$:
$$s'_{i,j} = s_{i,j} \oplus k_{i,j}^{(r)}$$

### Step 3: Final Round (Round 10) — No MixColumns

Round 10 performs only **SubBytes → ShiftRows → AddRoundKey** (no MixColumns). This asymmetry is intentional and **hardens against certain cryptanalytic attacks** (e.g., square attacks).

## 3.3 AES State Matrix Layout

The 16 bytes of the 128-bit block are loaded **column by column**:

$$
\begin{bmatrix} s_{0,0} & s_{0,1} & s_{0,2} & s_{0,3} \\ s_{1,0} & s_{1,1} & s_{1,2} & s_{1,3} \\ s_{2,0} & s_{2,1} & s_{2,2} & s_{2,3} \\ s_{3,0} & s_{3,1} & s_{3,2} & s_{3,3} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} b_0 & b_4 & b_8 & b_{12} \\ b_1 & b_5 & b_9 & b_{13} \\ b_2 & b_6 & b_{10} & b_{14} \\ b_3 & b_7 & b_{11} & b_{15} \end{bmatrix}
$$

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Why CTR + AES is the Gold Standard

| Property | ECB | CBC | **CTR** | CFB |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Parallelizable Encryption | ✗ | ✗ | ✅ | ✗ |
| Parallelizable Decryption | ✗ | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ |
| Random Access | ✅ | ✗ | ✅ | ✗ |
| Padding Required | ✅ | ✅ | ❌ | ❌ |
| Propagates Bit Errors | 1 block | Full chain | 1 block | Few blocks |
| Provably IND-CPA | ❌ | ✅ (random IV) | ✅ (unique nonce) | ✅ |
| Recommended by NIST (modern) | ❌ | ✅ (legacy) | ✅ | ⚠️ legacy |

> [!TIP]
> **Board Exam Mnemonic:** *"CTR is the **C**hampions **T**ournament of modes"* — it wins on **C**oncurrency, **T**hroughput, and **R**andom access.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 CTR Mode — Encryption & Decryption Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nonce[Nonce] --> ct[Counter Block T1]
    ctr0[Initial Counter ctr0] --> ct
    ct -->|T1| enc1[AES Encrypt EK]
    enc1 -->|O1| xor1{XOR}
    p1[Plaintext P1] --> xor1
    xor1 -->|C1| out1[Ciphertext Block 1]
    
    ct2[Counter Block T2 T1+1] -->|T2| enc2[AES Encrypt EK]
    enc2 -->|O2| xor2{XOR}
    p2[Plaintext P2] --> xor2
    xor2 -->|C2| out2[Ciphertext Block 2]
    
    style nonce fill:#e1f5e1
    style ctr0 fill:#e1f5e1
    style enc1 fill:#ffd6a5
    style enc2 fill:#ffd6a5
    style xor1 fill:#ffadad
    style xor2 fill:#ffadad
    style out1 fill:#a0c4ff
    style out2 fill:#a0c4ff
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Read this diagram left-to-right:** The **Nonce** and **Initial Counter** are concatenated to form $T_1$. Both $T_1$ and $T_2$ are encrypted **independently and in parallel** by AES. The resulting keystream blocks $O_1, O_2$ are XORed with $P_1, P_2$ to yield $C_1, C_2$. Decryption uses the same flowchart — replace $P_i$ with $C_i$ and $C_i$ with $P_i$.

## 4.2 AES — High-Level Round Structure (10-Round AES-128)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    pt[128-bit Plaintext Block] --> ark0[AddRoundKey Round 0]
    ark0 --> r1{Round 1 to 9}
    
    r1 --> sb[SubBytes]
    sb --> sr[ShiftRows]
    sr --> mc[MixColumns]
    mc --> ark1[AddRoundKey]
    ark1 --> r2{Round 2 to 9 continued}
    r2 --> sb
    
    r2 -->|after round 9| fr[Final Round 10]
    fr --> sb10[SubBytes]
    sb10 --> sr10[ShiftRows]
    sr10 --> ark10[AddRoundKey]
    ark10 --> ct[128-bit Ciphertext Block]
    
    style pt fill:#a0c4ff
    style ct fill:#a0c4ff
    style ark0 fill:#ffd6a5
    style ark1 fill:#ffd6a5
    style ark10 fill:#ffd6a5
    style mc fill:#bdb2ff
    style sb fill:#bdb2ff
    style sb10 fill:#bdb2ff
    style sr fill:#bdb2ff
    style sr10 fill:#bdb2ff
    style fr fill:#ffadad
```

## 4.3 AES Single Round — Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    state_in[State 4x4 bytes] --> sub[SubBytes S-Box lookup]
    sub --> shift[ShiftRows Cyclic rotation]
    shift --> mix[MixColumns GF 2^8 matrix]
    mix --> add[AddRoundKey XOR with round key]
    add --> state_out[New State]
    
    rk[Key Schedule] -->|expanded round key| add
    
    style state_in fill:#a0c4ff
    style state_out fill:#a0c4ff
    style sub fill:#bdb2ff
    style shift fill:#bdb2ff
    style mix fill:#bdb2ff
    style add fill:#ffd6a5
    style rk fill:#caffbf
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Observation:** The **AddRoundKey** operation is the only one that uses the **secret key material**. SubBytes, ShiftRows, and MixColumns are *fixed*, publicly known transformations. This is what makes AES a *Substitution-Permutation Network* (SPN) — it provides **confusion** (SubBytes) and **diffusion** (ShiftRows + MixColumns), while **AddRoundKey** provides the keying.

## 4.4 CTR Mode — Decryption Symmetry (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    c1[Ciphertext C1] --> xor1{XOR}
    c2[Ciphertext C2] --> xor2{XOR}
    
    nonce[Nonce] --> tc1[Counter T1]
    tc1 --> en1[AES Encrypt EK]
    en1 -->|O1| xor1
    
    tc1 -->|T1 + 1| en2[AES Encrypt EK]
    en2 -->|O2| xor2
    
    xor1 -->|P1| p1out[Plaintext P1]
    xor2 -->|P2| p2out[Plaintext P2]
    
    style c1 fill:#a0c4ff
    style c2 fill:#a0c4ff
    style en1 fill:#ffd6a5
    style en2 fill:#ffd6a5
    style p1out fill:#caffbf
    style p2out fill:#caffbf
```

> [!TIP]
> **Key Insight from the diagram:** Decryption requires **the same encryption hardware** as encryption — no separate decryptor is needed. This is why CTR mode is preferred in hardware-constrained environments like smart cards and IoT sensors.
