# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

### **Q1. List any three advantages of CTR mode over CBC mode.**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Parallelizability:** Both encryption and decryption can be performed in parallel across blocks because each block depends only on its own counter $T_i$, not on previous ciphertexts (unlike CBC, which chains $C_{i-1}$ into $P_i$).
2. **No Padding Required:** CTR mode generates a keystream of arbitrary length and XORs it bit-by-bit with the plaintext, so the plaintext does not need to be a multiple of the block size. This eliminates padding-oracle attacks and saves bandwidth.
3. **Random Access Decryption:** Any block $C_i$ can be decrypted independently by recomputing $E_K(T_i)$, enabling fast random reads — useful in disk encryption and database encryption.

*(3 marks — 1 mark per correct advantage with brief justification.)*

---

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

### **Q2. What is the role of the SubBytes step in AES? Why is it necessary for security?**

**Model Answer:**

The **SubBytes** transformation provides **non-linearity** in AES by replacing each byte of the state with its corresponding entry in a fixed 16×16 **S-Box**, computed as the multiplicative inverse in $\text{GF}(2^8)$ (using the irreducible polynomial $m(x) = x^8 + x^4 + x^3 + x + 1$) followed by an affine transformation over $\text{GF}(2)$.

**Why necessary:** Without non-linearity, AES would reduce to a system of linear equations in $\text{GF}(2)$, and an attacker could solve for the secret key using **Gaussian elimination** in roughly $2^{40}$ operations. The S-Box's non-linearity makes AES resistant to **linear cryptanalysis** and **differential cryptanalysis**, raising the practical attack complexity to $2^{128}$ or higher.

*(3 marks — 1 for definition, 1 for mechanism, 1 for security justification.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module 2 Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam - Model Paper Pattern 2024 Scheme]**

### **Question A (Choice 1):** Explain Counter (CTR) mode of operation in detail. With a neat diagram, describe the encryption and decryption processes. List its advantages over CBC mode. **[14 Marks]**

#### **Part (a) — CTR Mode Encryption & Decryption Processes [7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

CTR mode encrypts a plaintext $P$ divided into $m$ blocks $\{P_1, P_2, \dots, P_m\}$ as follows:

**Encryption Algorithm (symbolic):**

1. Generate a unique **nonce** (e.g., 64 or 96 bits) and combine it with an initial counter value $ctr_0$ to form the first counter block $T_1$.
2. For each $i = 1, 2, \dots, m$:
   - **Compute keystream block:** $O_i = E_K(T_i)$ where $K$ is the shared secret key and $E_K(\cdot)$ is the block cipher (typically AES-128).
   - **XOR with plaintext:** $C_i = P_i \oplus O_i$.
   - **Increment counter:** $T_{i+1} = T_i + 1 \pmod{2^{n}}$.
3. Output ciphertext $C = C_1 \mid\mid C_2 \mid\mid \dots \mid\mid C_m$.

**Decryption Algorithm (symbolic):**

Decryption is **identical** to encryption:

1. Recompute the counter sequence $T_1, T_2, \dots, T_m$ (deterministic from the nonce).
2. For each $i$:
   - Recompute keystream: $O_i = E_K(T_i)$.
   - Recover plaintext: $P_i = C_i \oplus O_i$.

**Mathematical proof of correctness:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_i &= C_i \oplus E_K(T_i) \\
&= (P_i \oplus E_K(T_i)) \oplus E_K(T_i) \\
&= P_i \oplus (E_K(T_i) \oplus E_K(T_i)) \\
&= P_i \oplus \mathbf{0} = P_i \quad \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

**Diagram** (refer to Section 4.1 for the Mermaid flowchart).

**Counter Block Construction (NIST SP 800-38A):**

$$T_i = \text{nonce}_{64} \,\,\vert\vert\,\, ctr_{64} \quad \text{where } ctr_i = ctr_0 + (i-1) \pmod{2^{64}}$$

*(7 marks — 2 for setup, 2 for encryption steps, 2 for decryption proof, 1 for diagram.)*

#### **Part (b) — Advantages of CTR Mode over CBC Mode [7 Marks]**

**Model Answer (tabular format for clarity):**

| Property | CTR Mode | CBC Mode |
|---|---|---|
| **Parallelizable encryption?** | ✅ Yes — each $O_i = E_K(T_i)$ is independent | ❌ No — $C_{i-1}$ feeds into $P_i$ |
| **Parallelizable decryption?** | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes (after computing all $C_i$) |
| **Random access?** | ✅ Yes — jump to block $i$ directly | ❌ No — must decrypt blocks 1…i−1 first |
| **Padding required?** | ❌ No — works on any plaintext length | ✅ Yes — plaintext must be a multiple of 128 bits |
| **Error propagation** | Localized — bit error in $C_i$ affects only $P_i$ | Catastrophic — bit flip in $C_{i-1}$ corrupts entire chain |
| **Performance on hardware** | Excellent — same operation per block | Requires feedback path |
| **Vulnerability to padding-oracle** | None (no padding) | Yes (e.g., POODLE, Lucky 13 attacks) |
| **Standard modern usage** | TLS 1.3, IPsec ESP, AES-GCM | TLS 1.2 (legacy) |

**Key Takeaway:** CTR mode offers superior performance, parallelizability, and security robustness in modern systems, making it the recommended block cipher mode in NIST SP 800-38A and contemporary protocols.

*(7 marks — 1 mark per row of the table + 1 for concluding recommendation.)*

---

### **Question B (Choice 2):** Describe the basic structure of the Advanced Encryption Standard (AES). Explain each transformation involved in a single round with a suitable diagram. **[14 Marks]**

#### **Part (a) — AES Basic Structure & Parameters [7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**AES Parameters Table:**

| Variant | Key Size $N_k$ (words) | Key Size (bits) | Block Size $N_b$ (words) | Block Size (bits) | Rounds $N_r$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| AES-128 | 4 | 128 | 4 | 128 | 10 |
| AES-192 | 6 | 192 | 4 | 128 | 12 |
| AES-256 | 8 | 256 | 4 | 128 | 14 |

**Round count formula:**

$$N_r = 6 + \max(N_k, N_b) = 6 + \max(N_k, 4)$$

**AES Algorithm Outline (AES-128):**

1. **Key Expansion:** Derive 11 round keys (176 bytes total) from the 128-bit cipher key using the Rijndael key schedule.
2. **Initial Round (Round 0):** `AddRoundKey(State, W[0..3])` — XOR plaintext with round key 0.
3. **Rounds 1 to 9:** For each round $r = 1, 2, \dots, 9$:
   - `SubBytes(State)`
   - `ShiftRows(State)`
   - `MixColumns(State)`
   - `AddRoundKey(State, W[4r..4r+3])`
4. **Final Round (Round 10):** `SubBytes → ShiftRows → AddRoundKey(State, W[40..43])`. *Note: No MixColumns.*
5. Output: 128-bit ciphertext.

**State representation:** The 16 input bytes $b_0 b_1 \dots b_{15}$ are arranged into a 4×4 byte matrix in **column-major order**:

$$
\text{State} = \begin{bmatrix} b_0 & b_4 & b_8 & b_{12} \\ b_1 & b_5 & b_9 & b_{13} \\ b_2 & b_6 & b_{10} & b_{14} \\ b_3 & b_7 & b_{11} & b_{15} \end{bmatrix}
$$

*(7 marks — 2 for parameter table, 2 for round formula, 2 for algorithm outline, 1 for state matrix.)*

#### **Part (b) — The Four AES Round Transformations [7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

For each round, the state undergoes four transformations. We describe each with its security purpose.

**1. SubBytes (Confusion):**

Each byte $s_{i,j}$ in the state is replaced by:
$$s'_{i,j} = S[s_{i,j}]$$

where $S$ is the AES S-Box. Computation: first compute the **multiplicative inverse** in $\text{GF}(2^8)$ modulo $m(x) = x^8 + x^4 + x^3 + x + 1$, then apply an **affine transformation**:

$$
s'_i = s_i \oplus s_{(i+4) \bmod 8} \oplus s_{(i+5) \bmod 8} \oplus s_{(i+6) \bmod 8} \oplus s_{(i+7) \bmod 8} \oplus c_i
$$

where $\mathbf{c} = \texttt{0x63} = (0,1,1,0,0,0,1,1)$.

**2. ShiftRows (Inter-column Diffusion):**

Row $r$ of the state is cyclically rotated **left** by $r$ bytes:

$$
\begin{bmatrix} s_{0,0} & s_{0,1} & s_{0,2} & s_{0,3} \\ s_{1,0} & s_{1,1} & s_{1,2} & s_{1,3} \\ s_{2,0} & s_{2,1} & s_{2,2} & s_{2,3} \\ s_{3,0} & s_{3,1} & s_{3,2} & s_{3,3} \end{bmatrix} \xrightarrow{\text{ShiftRows}} \begin{bmatrix} s_{0,0} & s_{0,1} & s_{0,2} & s_{0,3} \\ s_{1,1} & s_{1,2} & s_{1,3} & s_{1,0} \\ s_{2,2} & s_{2,3} & s_{2,0} & s_{2,1} \\ s_{3,3} & s_{3,0} & s_{3,1} & s_{3,2} \end{bmatrix}
$$

**3. MixColumns (Intra-column Diffusion):**

Each column of the state is treated as a polynomial of degree ≤ 3 in $\text{GF}(2^8)$ and multiplied by the fixed polynomial $c(x) = \texttt{03}x^3 + \texttt{01}x^2 + \texttt{01}x + \texttt{02}$ modulo $x^4 + 1$:

$$
\begin{bmatrix} s'_{0,j} \\ s'_{1,j} \\ s'_{2,j} \\ s'_{3,j} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 02 & 03 & 01 & 01 \\ 01 & 02 & 03 & 01 \\ 01 & 01 & 02 & 03 \\ 03 & 01 & 01 & 02 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} s_{0,j} \\ s_{1,j} \\ s_{2,j} \\ s_{3,j} \end{bmatrix} \pmod{m(x)}
$$

**4. AddRoundKey (Keying):**

The round key (derived from the key schedule) is XORed with the state:

$$s'_{i,j} = s_{i,j} \oplus k_{i,j}^{(r)}$$

**Why no MixColumns in the final round?**
The omission of MixColumns in Round 10 makes the cipher *asymmetric* between encryption and decryption at the round structure level, which thwarts certain **square and integral cryptanalytic attacks** that rely on predictable algebraic structure across all rounds.

*(7 marks — 1.5 marks per transformation, 0.5 for the no-MixColumns rationale.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing $N_b$ and $N_k$:** Many students write the round formula as $N_r = 6 + N_b$ instead of $N_r = 6 + \max(N_k, N_b)$. For AES, $N_b = 4$ always, but the formula must use $\max$ for correctness.
> 2. **Forgetting the nonce/nonce-reuse attack in CTR mode:** Examiners specifically award 2–3 marks for explaining *why nonce reuse breaks CTR mode*. Merely stating "CTR is parallel" is insufficient.
> 3. **MixColumns performed in the final round:** This is a structural error. Round 10 has *only* SubBytes, ShiftRows, and AddRoundKey. Examiners deduct 1 mark.
> 4. **Confusing CTR with OFB:** OFB uses $E_K(O_{i-1})$ to compute $O_i$ (chained feedback). CTR uses $E_K(T_i)$ where $T_i$ is a **counter**, not the previous output. Students often draw OFB diagrams instead of CTR diagrams and lose 3–4 marks.
> 5. **Wrong S-Box size or field polynomial:** AES uses a **16×16** S-Box and the irreducible polynomial **$x^8 + x^4 + x^3 + x + 1$** (= `0x11B`). Writing $x^8 + x^4 + x^3 + x^2 + 1$ is incorrect.
> 6. **Ignoring the $c(x) = 0x03, 0x01, 0x01, 0x02$ polynomial in MixColumns:** Examiners look for this exact constant. Vague statements like "mixes the columns" earn zero marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **🚀 Rapid-Revision Checklist (last-minute KTU prep)**

### **Counter (CTR) Mode — Key Points**
- ✅ CTR mode is a **synchronous stream cipher** built on top of any block cipher (commonly AES).
- ✅ Encryption: $C_i = P_i \oplus E_K(T_i)$, where $T_i$ is a deterministic counter (often `nonce || ctr`).
- ✅ Decryption: $P_i = C_i \oplus E_K(T_i)$ — **identical to encryption** (no separate decryption algorithm).
- ✅ **Fully parallelizable** for both encryption and decryption.
- ✅ **No padding required** — works on plaintexts of any length.
- ✅ **Random access** to any ciphertext block — critical for disk/database encryption.
- ✅ **Security requirement:** Counter/nonce **MUST be unique** per $(K, \text{message})$ pair. Nonce reuse leaks $P_i \oplus P'_i$.
- ✅ **Standardized as IND-CPA secure** by NIST SP 800-38A when used correctly.
- ✅ **Modern usage:** TLS 1.3, IPsec, WPA3, BitLocker — all use AES-CTR or AES-GCM (which is CTR + GHASH).

### **AES — Key Points**
- ✅ **Block size:** 128 bits (fixed). **Key sizes:** 128 / 192 / 256 bits.
- ✅ **Rounds:** 10 / 12 / 14 respectively. Formula: $N_r = 6 + \max(N_k, N_b)$.
- ✅ **State:** 4×4 byte matrix loaded in column-major order.
- ✅ **Four transformations** (in order): **SubBytes → ShiftRows → MixColumns → AddRoundKey**.
- ✅ **Final round (Round $N_r$):** No MixColumns — only SubBytes, ShiftRows, AddRoundKey.
- ✅ **SubBytes:** Non-linear byte substitution via S-Box = $\text{GF}(2^8)$ inverse + affine transform.
- ✅ **ShiftRows:** Row $r$ rotated left by $r$ bytes (0, 1, 2, 3).
- ✅ **MixColumns:** Each column multiplied by $c(x) = 0x03\,x^3 + 0x01\,x^2 + 0x01\,x + 0x02$ in $\text{GF}(2^8)$.
- ✅ **AddRoundKey:** XOR state with the round key (only key-dependent step).
- ✅ **Irreducible polynomial:** $m(x) = x^8 + x^4 + x^3 + x + 1$ (= `0x11B`).
- ✅ **AES is an SPN cipher** — provides **confusion** (SubBytes) and **diffusion** (ShiftRows + MixColumns).
- ✅ **Decryption uses inverse operations:** InvSubBytes, InvShiftRows, InvMixColumns, AddRoundKey.

### **CTR + AES — Why It's the Modern Standard**
- 🚀 **Speed:** AES-NI hardware gives ~10 GB/s throughput in CTR mode.
- 🛡️ **Security:** Provable IND-CPA security with unique nonces.
- ⚡ **Parallelism:** Trivially maps to multi-core CPUs and GPUs.
- 📦 **Compactness:** No padding overhead, no IV chaining.
- 🌐 **Ubiquity:** Default in TLS 1.3, WPA3, BitLocker, VeraCrypt, OpenSSL.

> **🎯 Final KTU Mantra:** *"CTR turns AES into a turbocharged, parallel, random-access encryption engine — but only if the nonce stays unique."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Transformation Functions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECAT595 - FOUNDATIONS OF SECURITY IN COMPUTING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Transformation Functions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Transformation Function** in the context of cryptographic systems is a well-defined, deterministic mathematical mapping that converts input data (plaintext) into a transformed output (ciphertext) using a secret key, such that the original input can be recovered only by an authorized party possessing the correct inverse transformation. In formal mathematical notation, an encryption transformation is expressed as:

$$E_k : P \rightarrow C$$

where $E_k$ is the encryption function parameterized by key $k$, $P$ is the plaintext space, and $C$ is the ciphertext space. The corresponding decryption transformation is:

$$D_k : C \rightarrow P$$

For a secure cryptographic system, $D_k$ must be the exact inverse of $E_k$, satisfying:

$$D_k(E_k(M)) = M \quad \text{for all } M \in P$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Transformation functions form the *mathematical backbone* of all symmetric-key ciphers. Every classical cipher (Caesar, Vigenère, Hill) and modern cipher (DES, AES) is built by composing elementary transformation functions.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

Imagine a **high-security mail sorting facility**. You place your letter (plaintext) into a machine that follows a specific set of rules (the transformation function) determined by a unique access code (the key). The machine doesn't just scramble the letters randomly — it applies a *systematic* and *reversible* procedure. Anyone with the corresponding decoder and the right access code can run the reverse procedure to get the original letter back. Without the code, the output looks like meaningless gibberish (ciphertext).

| Component | Real-World Analogy | Cryptographic Counterpart |
|---|---|---|
| Original Letter | Readable message | Plaintext $M$ |
| Sorting Machine Rules | Operational procedure | Transformation Function $E_k$ |
| Access Code | Authorization token | Secret Key $k$ |
| Scrambled Output | Obscured package | Ciphertext $C$ |
| Reverse Machine | Decoding device | Inverse Transformation $D_k$ |

> [!NOTE]
> A transformation function is **not** just any function — it must be:
> 1. **Deterministic** (same input + same key = same output every time)
> 2. **Invertible** (a unique inverse must exist)
> 3. **Key-dependent** (varying the key should completely change the output)
> 4. **Efficient** (computationally fast for legitimate users)

## 1.3 Classification of Transformation Functions

Transformation functions in cryptography are broadly classified into two primitive categories and one combined category:

- **Substitution Transformation (S-boxes)**: Replaces elements of the plaintext with other elements according to a defined mapping table.
- **Transposition Transformation (P-boxes)**: Rearranges the positions of elements in the plaintext without altering the elements themselves.
- **Product Transformation**: A cascade (composition) of two or more substitution and transposition stages.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Fundamental Properties (Shannon's Criteria)

**Claude Shannon**, in his seminal 1949 paper *"Communication Theory of Secrecy Systems,"* identified two critical properties that every secure transformation function must possess:

1. **Confusion**: The relationship between the key and the ciphertext must be made as complex and convoluted as possible. An attacker should not be able to derive the key by examining how plaintext and ciphertext relate.
2. **Diffusion**: The influence of a single plaintext bit should be spread (disseminated) over many ciphertext bits. Changing one bit in the plaintext should change roughly half the bits in the ciphertext (the *avalanche effect*).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Substitution provides **Confusion**, while Transposition provides **Diffusion**. Modern ciphers alternate between these two operations in multiple *rounds* to achieve both properties simultaneously.

## 2.2 Mathematical Properties of Transformation Functions

For a transformation function to be cryptographically useful, it must be a **bijection** (one-to-one and onto mapping) on the input space. Formally:

$$f : X \rightarrow X \text{ is bijective if } \forall y \in X, \exists! x \in X \mid f(x) = y$$

If $f$ is not bijective, decryption is *ambiguous* (multiple plaintexts could map to the same ciphertext), which destroys security.

**Composition of Transformations:**
If $f$ and $g$ are two transformation functions, the composition $h = f \circ g$ is defined as:

$$h(x) = f(g(x))$$

Modern ciphers are designed as compositions of many simple transformations, where even though each individual step is weak, the cumulative effect produces a strong cipher. This principle is captured in **Lai–Massey's Theorem** and **Luby–Rackoff's** work on pseudorandom function compositions.

## 2.3 The Feistel Cipher Structure

The most influential architectural framework for transformation functions is the **Feistel Network** (proposed by Horst Feistel at IBM in 1973, used in DES). A single Feistel round applies the following transformation to a plaintext block split into two halves $(L_i, R_i)$:

$$L_{i+1} = R_i$$

$$R_{i+1} = L_i \oplus F(R_i, K_i)$$

where $F$ is the **round function** (a key-dependent transformation), $K_i$ is the sub-key for round $i$, and $\oplus$ denotes the bitwise XOR operation.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why XOR?** The XOR operation is its own inverse: $A \oplus A = 0$ and $A \oplus 0 = A$. This makes XOR the ideal mixing operation in transformation functions, as it allows the same operation to be used for both encryption and decryption with a reversed key schedule.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Expression | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Encryption Transformation | $C = E_k(M)$ | Plaintext $M$ encrypted under key $k$ to produce ciphertext $C$ |
| 2 | Decryption Transformation | $M = D_k(C)$ | Ciphertext decrypted under key $k$ to recover plaintext |
| 3 | Inverse Property | $D_k(E_k(M)) = M$ | Decryption inverts encryption |
| 4 | Feistel Round (Left) | $L_{i+1} = R_i$ | Left half becomes previous right half |
| 5 | Feistel Round (Right) | $R_{i+1} = L_i \oplus F(R_i, K_i)$ | Right half is XOR of left with round function output |
| 6 | Composition | $h(x) = f(g(x))$ | Cascade of two transformations |
| 7 | Avalanche Effect | $\Delta C \approx \frac{n}{2}$ bits | Changing 1 bit in $M$ changes ~half of $n$ bits in $C$ |
| 8 | Substitution Box (S-box) | $S : \{0,1\}^m \rightarrow \{0,1\}^n$ | Maps $m$-bit input to $n$-bit output non-linearly |
| 9 | Permutation Box (P-box) | $P : \{0,1\}^n \rightarrow \{0,1\}^n$ | Rearranges bit positions (bijection) |
| 10 | Round Function | $F : \{0,1\}^{n/2} \times \{0,1\}^k \rightarrow \{0,1\}^{n/2}$ | Key-dependent nonlinear transformation |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Transformation functions are the *building blocks* of every secure communication system used today:

- **Banking Systems**: AES (Rijndael cipher) uses a complex product transformation with 10/12/14 rounds.
- **Internet Security**: TLS/SSL protocols use transformation functions like ChaCha20 and AES-GCM.
- **Mobile Communications**: A5/1 stream cipher (used in GSM) is built on LFSR-based transformations.
- **Password Storage**: bcrypt and Argon2 use iterated transformation functions.
- **Digital Signatures**: RSA and elliptic curve signatures rely on modular exponentiation transformations.
- **Blockchain**: SHA-256 and Keccak-256 are hash transformation functions critical to Bitcoin/Ethereum.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1: Composition of Two Simple Transformations

**Problem:** Given a plaintext alphabet $\{A, B, C, D, E\}$ and two transformation functions:
- $f$ (Substitution): $A \rightarrow D, B \rightarrow E, C \rightarrow A, D \rightarrow B, E \rightarrow C$
- $g$ (Transposition on positions): swaps position 1↔3, 2↔4, leaves position 5

Encrypt the plaintext "BACED" using the composed transformation $h = f \circ g$ (first apply $g$, then apply $f$).

**Step-by-step Solution:**

**Step 1: Identify the input plaintext block.**
The plaintext is $M = \text{"BACED"}$, which we represent as the ordered tuple:
$$M = (B, A, C, E, D)$$

**Step 2: Apply the transposition function $g$.**
The transposition rule swaps positions 1 and 3, and swaps positions 2 and 4. Position 5 is left unchanged.

Position mapping under $g$:
- Position 1 $\rightarrow$ Position 3
- Position 2 $\rightarrow$ Position 4
- Position 3 $\rightarrow$ Position 1
- Position 4 $\rightarrow$ Position 2
- Position 5 $\rightarrow$ Position 5

Applying $g$ to $(B, A, C, E, D)$:
- Element at position 1 ($B$) goes to position 3.
- Element at position 2 ($A$) goes to position 4.
- Element at position 3 ($C$) goes to position 1.
- Element at position 4 ($E$) goes to position 2.
- Element at position 5 ($D$) stays at position 5.

Reading from positions 1, 2, 3, 4, 5:
$$g(B, A, C, E, D) = (C, E, B, A, D)$$

**Step 3: Apply the substitution function $f$.**
Using the substitution table:
- $A \rightarrow D$
- $B \rightarrow E$
- $C \rightarrow A$
- $D \rightarrow B$
- $E \rightarrow C$

Applying $f$ element-wise to $(C, E, B, A, D)$:
- $C \rightarrow A$
- $E \rightarrow C$
- $B \rightarrow E$
- $A \rightarrow D$
- $D \rightarrow B$

$$f(C, E, B, A, D) = (A, C, E, D, B)$$

**Step 4: Final ciphertext.**
The composed transformation yields the ciphertext:
$$h(M) = f(g(M)) = (A, C, E, D, B) = \text{"ACEDB"}$$

**Step 5: Verification by decryption.**
To decrypt, we apply the inverse functions in reverse order. The inverse substitution $f^{-1}$ is:
- $D \rightarrow A, E \rightarrow B, A \rightarrow C, B \rightarrow D, C \rightarrow E$

The inverse transposition $g^{-1}$: Since $g$ is its own inverse (it is a permutation of order 2):
- $g^{-1}(A, C, E, D, B) = (E, D, A, C, B)$

Applying $f^{-1}$ to the ciphertext $(A, C, E, D, B)$:
- $A \rightarrow C, C \rightarrow E, E \rightarrow B, D \rightarrow A, B \rightarrow D$

$$f^{-1}(A, C, E, D, B) = (C, E, B, A, D)$$

Applying $g^{-1}$ (same as $g$) to $(C, E, B, A, D)$:
- Position 1→3, 2→4, 3→1, 4→2, 5→5
- Result: $(B, A, C, E, D)$

We recover $M = \text{"BACED"}$ ✓

## 3.2 Worked Example 2: Feistel Network Round Calculation

**Problem:** Consider a 4-bit Feistel cipher. The input block is split into two 2-bit halves. The current state is:
$$L_0 = 10_2, \quad R_0 = 11_2$$

The round function is defined as $F(R, K) = R \oplus K \oplus 01_2$, and the sub-key for round 1 is $K_1 = 01_2$. Compute $(L_1, R_1)$.

**Step-by-step Solution:**

**Step 1: Compute the round function output $F(R_0, K_1)$.**

$$F(R_0, K_1) = R_0 \oplus K_1 \oplus 01_2$$

$$F(11_2, 01_2) = 11_2 \oplus 01_2 \oplus 01_2$$

**Step 2: Evaluate XOR associativity.**

$$F(11_2, 01_2) = 11_2 \oplus (01_2 \oplus 01_2)$$

Since $01_2 \oplus 01_2 = 00_2$ (XOR of identical values yields zero):

$$F(11_2, 01_2) = 11_2 \oplus 00_2 = 11_2$$

**Step 3: Compute $L_1$.**

$$L_1 = R_0 = 11_2$$

**Step 4: Compute $R_1$.**

$$R_1 = L_0 \oplus F(R_0, K_1) = 10_2 \oplus 11_2$$

Performing bitwise XOR:
$$R_1 = (1 \oplus 1)(0 \oplus 1) = (0)(1) = 01_2$$

**Step 5: State the result.**

$$L_1 = 11_2, \quad R_1 = 01_2$$

The output block after round 1 is $(L_1, R_1) = (11_2, 01_2)$.

## 3.3 Worked Example 3: Avalanche Effect Verification

**Problem:** Given a simple 8-bit XOR-based transformation $C = M \oplus K$ where $K = 10110101_2$, demonstrate the avalanche effect by encrypting two plaintexts that differ in only 1 bit.

**Step-by-step Solution:**

**Step 1: Define the two plaintexts differing in 1 bit.**

$$M_1 = 01101001_2, \quad M_2 = 01101000_2$$

The plaintexts differ only in the **last bit** (LSB).

**Step 2: Encrypt both plaintexts using the same key.**

$$C_1 = M_1 \oplus K = 01101001_2 \oplus 10110101_2$$

Bitwise XOR:
- $0 \oplus 1 = 1$
- $1 \oplus 0 = 1$
- $1 \oplus 1 = 0$
- $0 \oplus 1 = 1$
- $1 \oplus 0 = 1$
- $0 \oplus 1 = 1$
- $0 \oplus 0 = 0$
- $1 \oplus 1 = 0$

$$C_1 = 11011100_2$$

**Step 3: Encrypt $M_2$.**

$$C_2 = M_2 \oplus K = 01101000_2 \oplus 10110101_2$$

Bitwise XOR:
- $0 \oplus 1 = 1$
- $1 \oplus 0 = 1$
- $1 \oplus 1 = 0$
- $0 \oplus 1 = 1$
- $1 \oplus 0 = 1$
- $0 \oplus 1 = 1$
- $0 \oplus 0 = 0$
- $0 \oplus 1 = 1$

$$C_2 = 11011101_2$$

**Step 4: Compute the Hamming distance (number of differing bits) between $C_1$ and $C_2$.**

$$C_1 \oplus C_2 = 11011100_2 \oplus 11011101_2 = 00000001_2$$

The Hamming distance is **1**, not $\approx n/2 = 4$.

**Step 5: Interpret the result.**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A single XOR with a key provides **zero avalanche effect** for single-bit changes in plaintext, because XOR is linear. This is why modern ciphers use *multiple rounds* of nonlinear transformations (S-boxes followed by P-boxes) to achieve the required avalanche effect. The avalanche property is measured by the **Strict Avalanche Criterion (SAC)**, which demands that every output bit changes with probability exactly **0.5** when any single input bit is flipped.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Transformation Function Comparison

| Property | Substitution (S-box) | Transposition (P-box) | Product Cipher | Feistel Network |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Provides Confusion? | ✓ Yes | ✗ No | ✓ Yes | ✓ Yes |
| Provides Diffusion? | ✗ No | ✓ Yes | ✓ Yes | ✓ Yes |
| Nonlinear? | ✓ Yes | ✗ No (linear) | ✓ Yes | ✓ Yes |
| Reversible by Same Structure? | ✗ (needs inverse table) | ✓ (inverse is another P-box) | Depends | ✓ (reversed key schedule) |
| Bijection Required? | Yes (for S-box) | Always | Always | Always |
| Computational Cost | Moderate | Low | High | Moderate |
| Used In | DES S-boxes, AES SubBytes | DES P-box, AES ShiftRows | AES, DES | DES, Blowfish, Twofish |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Generic Cryptographic Transformation Function

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Plaintext M] --> B[Key Input k]
    B --> C[Transformation Function E sub k]
    A --> C
    C --> D[Ciphertext C]
    D --> E[Inverse Transformation D sub k]
    B --> E
    E --> F[Recovered Plaintext M]

    style A fill:#1e90ff,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style B fill:#ff8c00,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style C fill:#8a2be2,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style D fill:#dc143c,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style E fill:#8a2be2,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style F fill:#228b22,stroke:#000,color:#fff
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram: Feistel Network Round Structure

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P[Plaintext Block] --> S1[Split into L0 and R0]
    S1 --> L0Node[L sub 0]
    S1 --> R0Node[R sub 0]
    R0Node --> F1[Round Function F]
    K[Sub Key K sub i] --> F1
    F1 --> X1[XOR]
    L0Node --> X1
    X1 --> R1Node[R sub i plus 1]
    R0Node --> L1Node[L sub i plus 1]
    L1Node --> S2[Output for next round]
    R1Node --> S2

    style P fill:#1e90ff,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style F1 fill:#8a2be2,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style X1 fill:#dc143c,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style K fill:#ff8c00,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style L0Node fill:#228b22,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style R0Node fill:#228b22,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style L1Node fill:#228b22,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style R1Node fill:#228b22,stroke:#000,color:#fff
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram: Composition of Substitution and Transposition (SP-Network)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Plaintext Input] --> B[Round 1 Substitution S]
    B --> C[Round 1 Permutation P]
    C --> D[Round 2 Substitution S]
    D --> E[Round 2 Permutation P]
    E --> F[Round N Substitution S]
    F --> G[Final Output Ciphertext]

    style A fill:#1e90ff,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style B fill:#8a2be2,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style C fill:#ff8c00,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style D fill:#8a2be2,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style E fill:#ff8c00,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style F fill:#8a2be2,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style G fill:#dc143c,stroke:#000,color:#fff
```

## 4.4 Architecture Diagram: Modern Block Cipher Transformation Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input Block 128 bits] --> B[Initial Key Addition XOR]
    B --> C[Round 1 S box Substitution]
    C --> D[Round 1 P box Permutation]
    D --> E[Round 2 S box Substitution]
    E --> F[Round 2 P box Permutation]
    F --> G[Round N S box Substitution]
    G --> H[Round N P box Permutation]
    H --> I[Final Key Addition XOR]
    I --> J[Output Ciphertext 128 bits]

    style A fill:#1e90ff,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style B fill:#ff8c00,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style C fill:#8a2be2,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style D fill:#ff8c00,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style E fill:#8a2be2,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style F fill:#ff8c00,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style G fill:#8a2be2,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style H fill:#ff8c00,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style I fill:#ff8c00,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style J fill:#dc143c,stroke:#000,color:#fff
```

> [!NOTE]
> The substitution boxes (S-boxes) provide **confusion** by introducing nonlinearity, while the permutation boxes (P-boxes) provide **diffusion** by spreading bit influence across the block. This SP-Network (Substitution-Permutation Network) design principle is the foundation of AES, Serpent, and other modern ciphers.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** Define a **transformation function** in cryptography. Differentiate between substitution and transposition transformation functions.

**Model Answer:**

A transformation function in cryptography is a mathematical operation $E_k : P \rightarrow C$ that converts plaintext $M \in P$ into ciphertext $C \in C$ using a key $k$, such that the inverse function $D_k$ can recover the original plaintext.

**Substitution Transformation:** Replaces each element (bit, byte, or character) of the plaintext with another element according to a fixed mapping table (e.g., S-box). The *content* of the message changes, but the position remains the same. It provides **confusion**.

**Transposition Transformation:** Rearranges the positions of elements in the plaintext without changing the elements themselves (e.g., P-box). The *position* changes, but the content remains the same. It provides **diffusion**.

**Example:** In the Caesar cipher (substitution), $A \rightarrow D$. In a columnar transposition (transposition), the letters of "HELLO" might become "HLOEL".

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam - May 2024]** What is the **avalanche effect** in transformation functions? Why is it important for cryptographic security?

**Model Answer:**

The avalanche effect is a desirable property of cryptographic transformation functions where a small change in the input (plaintext or key) produces a large, unpredictable change in the output (ciphertext). Formally, for a good $n$-bit cipher, flipping a single input bit should change approximately $n/2$ output bits.

**Importance:**
1. It ensures that ciphertexts of similar plaintexts are completely different, preventing pattern analysis.
2. It prevents **differential cryptanalysis**, where attackers study how input differences propagate to output differences.
3. It guarantees **diffusion** as defined by Shannon.
4. It makes the cipher behave like a *pseudorandom* function from the attacker's perspective.

**Example:** In DES, flipping 1 bit in the plaintext changes approximately 34 of the 64 ciphertext bits, demonstrating near-ideal avalanche behavior.

---

## 5.2 Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (Choice 1)

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper 2]** 

**(a)** Explain the **Feistel cipher structure** with a neat diagram. Show how a single round of the Feistel cipher transforms a 64-bit plaintext block split into two 32-bit halves. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** Design a 2-round Feistel cipher with 4-bit blocks using a simple round function $F(R, K) = R \oplus K$. Encrypt the plaintext $M = 10110010_2$ using sub-keys $K_1 = 1100_2$ and $K_2 = 0110_2$. Show all steps. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution for Part (a):**

The Feistel cipher is a symmetric-key block cipher architecture proposed by Horst Feistel (IBM, 1973). It is the structural foundation of the Data Encryption Standard (DES).

**Architecture of one Feistel round:**

The plaintext block of $n$ bits is split into two equal halves: a left half $L_i$ of $n/2$ bits and a right half $R_i$ of $n/2$ bits. Each round $i$ applies the following transformation:

$$L_{i+1} = R_i$$

$$R_{i+1} = L_i \oplus F(R_i, K_i)$$

where:
- $F$ is the **round function** (any deterministic, key-dependent function — does not need to be invertible).
- $K_i$ is the **sub-key** for round $i$, derived from the main key via the key schedule.
- $\oplus$ is the bitwise XOR operation.
- The subscript $i$ denotes the round number (0 for the initial state, 1 after the first round, etc.).

**Block diagram of one Feistel round:**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Li[L sub i] --> X[XOR]
    Ri[R sub i] --> R1[New L sub i+1]
    Ri2[R sub i] --> F[Round Function F]
    Ki[K sub i] --> F
    F --> X
    X --> R2[New R sub i+1]

    style Li fill:#228b22,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style Ri fill:#228b22,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style Ri2 fill:#228b22,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style Ki fill:#ff8c00,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style F fill:#8a2be2,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style X fill:#dc143c,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style R1 fill:#228b22,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style R2 fill:#228b22,stroke:#000,color:#fff
```

**Key properties of the Feistel structure:**

1. **The round function $F$ need not be invertible.** This is a major advantage — designers can use any function, even non-bijective ones, and the cipher remains invertible.
2. **Decryption uses the same structure as encryption**, but with the sub-keys applied in reverse order. This simplifies hardware/software implementation.
3. **Security increases with the number of rounds.** DES uses 16 rounds, Blowfish uses 16, and AES-equivalent Feistel variants use 20+.
4. **Diffusion and confusion are achieved** through repeated application of $F$ combined with the swap-and-XOR pattern.

**Model Solution for Part (b):**

**Given:**
- Plaintext: $M = 10110010_2$ (8 bits)
- $L_0 = 1011_2$ (left 4 bits)
- $R_0 = 0010_2$ (right 4 bits)
- Round function: $F(R, K) = R \oplus K$ (4-bit XOR)
- Sub-keys: $K_1 = 1100_2$, $K_2 = 0110_2$

**Round 1 (using $K_1 = 1100_2$):**

**Step 1:** Compute the round function output:
$$F(R_0, K_1) = R_0 \oplus K_1 = 0010_2 \oplus 1100_2 = 1110_2$$

Bitwise XOR verification:
- $0 \oplus 1 = 1$
- $0 \oplus 1 = 1$
- $1 \oplus 0 = 1$
- $0 \oplus 0 = 0$

Result: $F(R_0, K_1) = 1110_2$ ✓

**Step 2:** Compute new left half:
$$L_1 = R_0 = 0010_2$$

**Step 3:** Compute new right half:
$$R_1 = L_0 \oplus F(R_0, K_1) = 1011_2 \oplus 1110_2 = 0101_2$$

Bitwise XOR verification:
- $1 \oplus 1 = 0$
- $0 \oplus 1 = 1$
- $1 \oplus 1 = 0$
- $1 \oplus 0 = 1$

Result: $R_1 = 0101_2$ ✓

**After Round 1:** $(L_1, R_1) = (0010_2, 0101_2)$

**Round 2 (using $K_2 = 0110_2$):**

**Step 4:** Compute the round function output:
$$F(R_1, K_2) = R_1 \oplus K_2 = 0101_2 \oplus 0110_2 = 0011_2$$

Bitwise XOR verification:
- $0 \oplus 0 = 0$
- $1 \oplus 1 = 0$
- $0 \oplus 1 = 1$
- $1 \oplus 0 = 1$

Result: $F(R_1, K_2) = 0011_2$ ✓

**Step 5:** Compute new left half:
$$L_2 = R_1 = 0101_2$$

**Step 6:** Compute new right half:
$$R_2 = L_1 \oplus F(R_1, K_2) = 0010_2 \oplus 0011_2 = 0001_2$$

Bitwise XOR verification:
- $0 \oplus 0 = 0$
- $0 \oplus 0 = 0$
- $1 \oplus 1 = 0$
- $0 \oplus 1 = 1$

Result: $R_2 = 0001_2$ ✓

**After Round 2:** $(L_2, R_2) = (0101_2, 0001_2)$

**Step 7: Final ciphertext.**
$$C = L_2 \parallel R_2 = 0101_2 \parallel 0001_2 = 01010001_2$$

$$\boxed{C = 01010001_2}$$

---

### Question B (Choice 2 - Alternative)

**[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Supplementary Model Paper]**

**(a)** Explain **confusion** and **diffusion** as defined by Claude Shannon. Describe how substitution and transposition transformations contribute to these properties. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** A Hill cipher uses the encryption transformation $C = K \cdot M \pmod{26}$, where $K$ is a $2 \times 2$ key matrix and $M$ is a column vector of two plaintext letters. Given the key matrix $K = \begin{pmatrix} 3 & 3 \\ 2 & 5 \end{pmatrix}$, encrypt the plaintext "HI" using the Hill cipher. Verify the result by decryption. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution for Part (a):**

**Confusion (Shannon, 1949):**

Confusion refers to the principle that the relationship between the **key** and the **ciphertext** should be made as complex and involved as possible. An attacker examining the ciphertext should not be able to deduce the key, or partial information about the key, through simple statistical or algebraic analysis.

**How Substitution provides Confusion:** Substitution replaces plaintext elements with ciphertext elements according to a key-dependent lookup table (S-box). Since the mapping is nonlinear and key-controlled, the statistical relationship between plaintext and ciphertext is destroyed, making it computationally infeasible to recover the key by frequency analysis.

**Diffusion (Shannon, 1949):**

Diffusion refers to the principle that the influence of a **each plaintext digit** should be spread over **many ciphertext digits**. Equivalently, each ciphertext digit should be a function of many plaintext digits. This ensures that any redundancy or pattern in the plaintext is dissipated across the entire ciphertext.

**How Transposition provides Diffusion:** Transposition rearranges the positions of bits/characters. A single bit at position $i$ in the plaintext is moved to position $\sigma(i)$ in the ciphertext (where $\sigma$ is a permutation). When combined with multiple rounds and substitutions, the influence of a single plaintext bit propagates to many ciphertext positions, achieving the avalanche effect.

**Combined Effect in Modern Ciphers:**
Modern ciphers (AES, DES) use **product ciphers** — alternating substitution and transposition rounds. The substitution layers provide confusion by adding nonlinearity, and the transposition layers provide diffusion by spreading bit influence. After sufficient rounds, the cipher becomes a strong **pseudorandom permutation (PRP)**, indistinguishable from a truly random permutation to any computationally bounded attacker.

**Model Solution for Part (b):**

**Given:**
- Key matrix: $K = \begin{pmatrix} 3 & 3 \\ 2 & 5 \end{pmatrix}$
- Plaintext: "HI"

**Step 1: Convert plaintext letters to numerical values (A=0, B=1, ..., Z=25).**

$$H = 7, \quad I = 8$$

**Step 2: Form the plaintext column vector.**

$$M = \begin{pmatrix} 7 \\ 8 \end{pmatrix}$$

**Step 3: Perform matrix multiplication $K \cdot M$.**

$$K \cdot M = \begin{pmatrix} 3 & 3 \\ 2 & 5 \end{pmatrix} \begin{pmatrix} 7 \\ 8 \end{pmatrix}$$

Computing the first element (row 1, column 1):
$$(K \cdot M)_1 = (3 \times 7) + (3 \times 8) = 21 + 24 = 45$$

Computing the second element (row 2, column 1):
$$(K \cdot M)_2 = (2 \times 7) + (5 \times 8) = 14 + 40 = 54$$

**Step 4: Apply modulo 26 reduction.**

$$C_1 = 45 \bmod 26 = 45 - 26 = 19$$

$$C_2 = 54 \bmod 26 = 54 - 26 - 26 = 2$$

**Step 5: Form the ciphertext vector.**

$$C = \begin{pmatrix} 19 \\ 2 \end{pmatrix}$$

**Step 6: Convert numerical values back to letters (0=A, 1=B, ..., 25=Z).**

$$19 = T, \quad 2 = C$$

$$\boxed{\text{Ciphertext} = \text{"TC"}}$$

**Step 7: Verification by Decryption.**

The decryption requires the inverse of $K$ modulo 26. First, compute the determinant:

$$\det(K) = (3 \times 5) - (3 \times 2) = 15 - 6 = 9$$

Compute the modular inverse of 9 modulo 26. We seek $d$ such that $9d \equiv 1 \pmod{26}$:
- $9 \times 3 = 27 \equiv 1 \pmod{26}$ ✓

So $\det(K)^{-1} = 3$.

The adjugate matrix of $K$:
$$\text{adj}(K) = \begin{pmatrix} 5 & -3 \\ -2 & 3 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 5 & 23 \\ 24 & 3 \end{pmatrix} \pmod{26}$$

The inverse key matrix:
$$K^{-1} = 3 \cdot \begin{pmatrix} 5 & 23 \\ 24 & 3 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 15 & 69 \\ 72 & 9 \end{pmatrix} \equiv \begin{pmatrix} 15 & 17 \\ 20 & 9 \end{pmatrix} \pmod{26}$$

**Step 8: Decrypt the ciphertext.**

$$M = K^{-1} \cdot C = \begin{pmatrix} 15 & 17 \\ 20 & 9 \end{pmatrix} \begin{pmatrix} 19 \\ 2 \end{pmatrix}$$

$$M_1 = (15 \times 19) + (17 \times 2) = 285 + 34 = 319$$
$$319 \bmod 26 = 319 - 12 \times 26 = 319 - 312 = 7$$

$$M_2 = (20 \times 19) + (9 \times 2) = 380 + 18 = 398$$
$$398 \bmod 26 = 398 - 15 \times 26 = 398 - 390 = 8$$

$$M = \begin{pmatrix} 7 \\ 8 \end{pmatrix} \rightarrow \text{"HI"}$$

**Decryption verified** ✓

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Forgetting modular reduction:** Many students compute the matrix product correctly but forget to apply mod 26 at the end. This is the most common deduction (loss of **1-2 marks** per instance).
> 2. **Indexing convention confusion:** KTU typically uses **A=0** convention for the Hill cipher. Using A=1 will produce entirely different (and incorrect) ciphertexts. Always state your convention explicitly.
> 3. **Key matrix must be invertible mod 26:** The determinant of $K$ must be coprime with 26 (i.e., $\gcd(\det(K), 26) = 1$). If the determinant shares a factor with 26, decryption is impossible.
> 4. **Feistel round arithmetic:** In the Feistel network, students often confuse the order — remember the *new* right half is $L_i \oplus F(R_i, K_i)$, not the other way around.
> 5. **Avalanche effect miscalculation:** The avalanche effect applies to the *cipher*, not the *key schedule*. Showing 1 bit change in key changes 1 bit in ciphertext (via direct XOR) does **not** demonstrate avalanche — you must show the cascading effect through multiple rounds.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Transformation Function Definition:** A key-dependent mathematical mapping $E_k: P \to C$ that is deterministic, invertible, and computationally efficient for legitimate users.
- **Two Primitive Types:** *Substitution* (replaces elements → confusion) and *Transposition* (rearranges positions → diffusion).
- **Product Ciphers:** Modern ciphers cascade multiple substitution and transposition stages to achieve both confusion and diffusion simultaneously.
- **Shannon's Two Properties:** *Confusion* (obscure key-ciphertext relationship) and *Diffusion* (spread plaintext influence across ciphertext).
- **Feistel Network:** Splits block into halves, applies $L_{i+1} = R_i$ and $R_{i+1} = L_i \oplus F(R_i, K_i)$; uses same structure for decryption with reversed key schedule.
- **Round Function $F$:** Can be any function (not necessarily invertible) — this is the key design flexibility of Feistel ciphers.
- **XOR Properties:** $A \oplus A = 0$, $A \oplus 0 = A$, and $A \oplus B = B \oplus A$ (commutativity) — these make XOR the cornerstone mixing operation.
- **Avalanche Effect:** A 1-bit change in input should change ~50% of output bits; formalized by the *Strict Avalanche Criterion (SAC)*.
- **Bijection Requirement:** Transformation functions on the message space must be bijective (one-to-one and onto) to ensure unique decryption.
- **Composition Principle:** $h = f \circ g$ — composing simple weak transformations can yield a strong cipher (Kerckhoffs' compositional security).
- **Substitution-Permutation (SP) Networks:** The architectural pattern used in AES — alternates S-box (SubBytes) and P-box (ShiftRows, MixColumns) operations.
- **Real-World Ciphers:** DES = Feistel with 16 rounds; AES = SP-Network with 10/12/14 rounds; both are product ciphers combining substitution and permutation.
- **Block vs. Stream:** Transformation functions are used in both block ciphers (operating on fixed-size blocks) and stream ciphers (operating bit-by-bit via keystream XOR).
- **KTU Exam Focus:** Expect questions on (1) defining transformation functions, (2) computing Feistel rounds, (3) applying Hill cipher matrix multiplication, and (4) explaining Shannon's principles.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Key Expansion
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECAT595 - FOUNDATIONS OF SECURITY IN COMPUTING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Key Expansion

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

**Key Expansion** (also called the **Key Schedule**) is the deterministic cryptographic procedure used to generate a sequence of *round keys* (subkeys) from a single short secret **master key** before or during the execution of an iterative block cipher. Each round key is mathematically derived from the previous one using a combination of byte substitution, byte rotation, round constant XOR operations, and feedback XOR with prior words.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In modern symmetric ciphers such as **AES (Advanced Encryption Standard)** and legacy ciphers such as **DES (Data Encryption Standard)**, the input key is much smaller (e.g., **128, 192, 256 bits** for AES) compared to the total keying material required to encrypt a single block. The **Key Expansion algorithm** expands this short master key into the larger pool of round keys needed by the cipher's round function.

In formal notation, given a master key $K$ of length $k$ bits, the Key Expansion is a bijection:

$$E : \{0,1\}^k \longrightarrow \{0,1\}^{N \cdot b}$$

where $N$ is the number of rounds and $b$ is the round key size in bits.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a bank vault that requires a **different physical key for each of the 11 locked doors** leading to the main safe. Instead of giving you 11 separate keys, the bank issues you **one master key**. When you insert this master key into a small mechanical "key generator" machine, it produces the 11 individual door keys through a chained mechanical process. If an attacker steals one door key, he cannot reconstruct the other 10 without knowing how the machine transforms the input. This is exactly what **Key Expansion** does in a block cipher: the **master key** is the input, the **key generator** is the deterministic algorithm, and the **door keys** are the **round keys**.

> [!NOTE]
> Key Expansion must be **deterministic** (so the receiver can reproduce the round keys) but also **non-linear and diffusion-rich** (so the round keys look pseudo-random and resist related-key attacks).

## 1.3 Why Key Expansion is Necessary

| Reason | Description |
|---|---|
| **Increased Confusion** | Multiple different round keys ensure that no single key bit controls a large portion of the ciphertext. |
| **Resistance to Slide Attacks** | Independent round keys prevent attackers from sliding two encryptions against each other. |
| **Enables Key Whitening** | Initial and final round keys are used as XOR "whitening" material with the plaintext and ciphertext. |
| **Related-Key Security** | A well-designed schedule ensures that flipping one bit of the master key changes nearly every round key bit. |
| **Hardware Efficiency** | Round keys can be computed **on-the-fly** rather than storing the entire expanded key in memory. |

> [!TIP]
> For a typical AES-128 implementation, only **16 bytes (128 bits)** of master key are stored; the remaining **160 bytes (1280 bits)** of round key material is generated on demand.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Key Expansion in AES-128

The AES-128 key expansion expands a 128-bit master key into **11 round keys** of **128 bits each**, totaling **44 words** (each word = 32 bits = 4 bytes).

Let the master key be arranged into the first 4 words:

$$W[0] \;\vert\; W[1] \;\vert\; W[2] \;\vert\; W[3]$$

The remaining 40 words are generated using the recurrence relations:

$$W[i] = W[i-4] \oplus W[i-1] \quad \text{when } i \bmod 4 \neq 0$$

$$W[i] = W[i-4] \oplus g(W[i-1]) \quad \text{when } i \bmod 4 = 0$$

The non-linear function $g(\cdot)$ consists of three sub-operations applied to the input 4-byte word $A \;\vert\; B \;\vert\; C \;\vert\; D$:

1. **RotWord** — cyclic left shift of bytes: $B \;\vert\; C \;\vert\; D \;\vert\; A$
2. **SubWord** — apply the AES S-box to each of the 4 bytes independently
3. **XOR with Round Constant** — XOR the result with $Rcon[i/4]$

The round constant is defined as:

$$Rcon[j] = (RC[j],\; 00,\; 00,\; 00)$$

where $RC[j]$ is a byte in the finite field $GF(2^8)$ generated by:

$$RC[1] = 01, \qquad RC[j] = 02 \cdot RC[j-1] \text{ in } GF(2^8)$$

The first few values are: $RC[1]=01$, $RC[2]=02$, $RC[3]=04$, $RC[4]=08$, $RC[5]=10$, $RC[6]=20$, $RC[7]=40$, $RC[8]=80$, $RC[9]=1B$, $RC[10]=36$.

## 2.2 Key Expansion in AES-256 (Brief)

For AES-256, the schedule alternates with an extra `SubWord` step when $i \bmod 8 = 4$:

$$W[i] = W[i-8] \oplus \text{SubWord}(W[i-1]) \quad \text{when } i \bmod 8 = 4$$

This adds additional non-linearity to mitigate related-key attacks against AES-256.

## 2.3 Key Expansion in DES

DES uses a 56-bit effective key (64 bits with 8 parity bits) to generate **16 round keys of 48 bits each**. Each round key is obtained by:

1. **Permuted Choice 1 (PC-1)** — selects 56 of the 64 bits, splitting them into two 28-bit halves $C_0$ and $D_0$.
2. **Cyclic Left Shift** — at round $i$, both halves are left-rotated by shift amount $r_i$:

$$C_i = \text{ROL}_{r_i}(C_{i-1}), \qquad D_i = \text{ROL}_{r_i}(D_{i-1})$$

3. **Permuted Choice 2 (PC-2)** — selects 48 bits from the concatenated $C_i \;\vert\; D_i$ to form the round key $K_i$.

The shift schedule is: $r = (1, 1, 2, 2, 2, 2, 2, 2, 1, 2, 2, 2, 2, 2, 2, 1)$.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Parameter | AES-128 | AES-192 | AES-256 | DES |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Master Key Size (bits) | **128** | **192** | **256** | **64** (56 effective) |
| Block Size (bits) | **128** | **128** | **128** | **64** |
| Number of Rounds $N_r$ | **10** | **12** | **14** | **16** |
| Round Key Size (bits) | **128** | **128** | **128** | **48** |
| Total Expanded Key (bits) | **1408** | **1664** | **1920** | **768** |
| Words per Round Key | 4 | 4 | 4 | N/A |
| Total Words Generated | 44 | 52 | 60 | 16 subkeys |
| Non-linear Step | `SubWord` | `SubWord` | `SubWord` (twice) | PC-1 + PC-2 |
| Rotation Step | `RotWord` (1 byte) | `RotWord` (1 byte) | `RotWord` (1 byte) | Left shifts $r_i$ |
| Constant XOR | `Rcon` | `Rcon` | `Rcon` | None |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Favourite:** The AES S-box mapping for the first few rows is shown below. Memorize at least the S-box values for hex `0x00`–`0x0F` and `0xCF`, `0x4F`, `0x3C`, `0x09` (these are heavily used in solved problems).

| | x0 | x1 | x2 | x3 | x4 | x5 | x6 | x7 | x8 | x9 | xA | xB | xC | xD | xE | xF |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **0x** | 63 | 7C | 77 | 7B | F2 | 6B | 6F | C5 | 30 | 01 | 67 | 2B | FE | D7 | AB | 76 |
| **1x** | CA | 82 | C9 | 7D | FA | 59 | 47 | F0 | AD | D4 | A2 | AF | 9C | A4 | 72 | C0 |
| **2x** | B7 | FD | 93 | 26 | 36 | 3F | F7 | CC | 34 | A5 | E5 | F1 | 71 | D8 | 31 | 15 |
| **3x** | 04 | C7 | 23 | C3 | 18 | 96 | 05 | 9A | 07 | 12 | 80 | E2 | EB | 27 | B2 | 75 |
| **4x** | 09 | 83 | 2C | 1A | 1B | 6E | 5A | A0 | 52 | 3B | D6 | B3 | 29 | E3 | 2F | 84 |
| **5x** | 53 | D1 | 00 | ED | 20 | FC | B1 | 5B | 6A | CB | BE | 39 | 4A | 4C | 58 | CF |
| **6x** | D0 | EF | AA | FB | 43 | 4D | 33 | 85 | 45 | F9 | 02 | 7F | 50 | 3C | 9F | A8 |
| **7x** | 51 | A3 | 40 | 8F | 92 | 9D | 38 | F5 | BC | B6 | DA | 21 | 10 | FF | F3 | D2 |
| **8x** | CD | 0C | 13 | EC | 5F | 97 | 44 | 17 | C4 | A7 | 7E | 3D | 64 | 5D | 19 | 73 |
| **9x** | 60 | 81 | 4F | DC | 22 | 2A | 90 | 88 | 46 | EE | B8 | 14 | DE | 5E | 0B | DB |
| **Ax** | E0 | 32 | 3A | 0A | 49 | 06 | 24 | 5C | C2 | D3 | AC | 62 | 91 | 95 | E4 | 79 |
| **Bx** | E7 | C8 | 37 | 6D | 8D | D5 | 4E | A9 | 6C | 56 | F4 | EA | 65 | 7A | AE | 08 |
| **Cx** | BA | 78 | 25 | 2E | 1C | A6 | B4 | C6 | E8 | DD | 74 | 1F | 4B | BD | 8B | 8A |
| **Dx** | 70 | 3E | B5 | 66 | 48 | 03 | F6 | 0E | 61 | 35 | 57 | B9 | 86 | C1 | 1D | 9E |
| **Ex** | E1 | F8 | 98 | 11 | 69 | D9 | 8E | 94 | 9B | 1E | 87 | E9 | CE | 55 | 28 | DF |
| **Fx** | 8C | A1 | 89 | 0D | BF | E6 | 42 | 68 | 41 | 99 | 2D | 0F | B0 | 54 | BB | 16 |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Application of Key Expansion |
|---|---|
| **TLS / HTTPS** | AES session keys are expanded per-connection for every TLS handshake. |
| **Disk Encryption (BitLocker, FileVault)** | The disk's master key is expanded for each sector block. |
| **Wireless Security (WPA3)** | AES-128 CCM uses 11 round keys per packet encryption. |
| **Blockchain Wallets** | Hierarchical Deterministic (HD) wallets use a *seed expansion* principle analogous to cipher key schedules. |
| **Smart Cards** | Limited memory requires on-the-fly key expansion rather than storage. |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example: AES-128 Key Expansion (FIPS-197 Reference Vector)

This is the **FIPS-197 official test vector** for AES-128 and is the most frequently asked question in KTU Module 2.

**Master Key (128 bits in hex):**

$$K = \text{2b 7e 15 16 28 ae d2 a6 ab f7 15 88 09 cf 4f 3c}$$

**Initial Words:**

$$W[0] = \text{2B7E1516}, \quad W[1] = \text{28AED2A6}$$
$$W[2] = \text{ABF71588}, \quad W[3] = \text{09CF4F3C}$$

---

### Step 1: Compute $W[4]$ (i = 4, $i \bmod 4 = 0$)

**1a. Apply RotWord to $W[3] = \text{09CF4F3C}$:**

Cyclic left shift of bytes: $\text{CF4F3C09}$.

**1b. Apply SubWord to $\text{CF4F3C09}$:**

Look up each byte in the AES S-box:

- $S[\text{CF}] = \text{8A}$  (row C, col F)
- $S[\text{4F}] = \text{84}$  (row 4, col F)
- $S[\text{3C}] = \text{EB}$  (row 3, col C)
- $S[\text{09}] = \text{01}$  (row 0, col 9)

So $\text{SubWord}(\text{CF4F3C09}) = \text{8A84EB01}$.

**1c. XOR with Round Constant $Rcon[1]$:**

$$Rcon[1] = \text{01000000}$$

$$g(W[3]) = \text{8A84EB01} \oplus \text{01000000} = \text{8B84EB01}$$

**1d. Compute $W[4]$:**

$$W[4] = W[0] \oplus g(W[3]) = \text{2B7E1516} \oplus \text{8B84EB01}$$

Performing byte-wise XOR:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{2B} \oplus \text{8B} &= \text{A0} \\
\text{7E} \oplus \text{84} &= \text{FA} \\
\text{15} \oplus \text{EB} &= \text{FE} \\
\text{16} \oplus \text{01} &= \text{17}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$\boxed{W[4] = \text{A0FAFE17}}$$

---

### Step 2: Compute $W[5]$ (i = 5, $i \bmod 4 \neq 0$)

$$W[5] = W[1] \oplus W[4] = \text{28AED2A6} \oplus \text{A0FAFE17}$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{28} \oplus \text{A0} &= \text{88} \\
\text{AE} \oplus \text{FA} &= \text{54} \\
\text{D2} \oplus \text{FE} &= \text{2C} \\
\text{A6} \oplus \text{17} &= \text{B1}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$\boxed{W[5] = \text{88542CB1}}$$

---

### Step 3: Compute $W[6]$ (i = 6)

$$W[6] = W[2] \oplus W[5] = \text{ABF71588} \oplus \text{88542CB1}$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{AB} \oplus \text{88} &= \text{23} \\
\text{F7} \oplus \text{54} &= \text{A3} \\
\text{15} \oplus \text{2C} &= \text{39} \\
\text{88} \oplus \text{B1} &= \text{39}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$\boxed{W[6] = \text{23A33939}}$$

---

### Step 4: Compute $W[7]$ (i = 7)

$$W[7] = W[3] \oplus W[6] = \text{09CF4F3C} \oplus \text{23A33939}$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{09} \oplus \text{23} &= \text{2A} \\
\text{CF} \oplus \text{A3} &= \text{6C} \\
\text{4F} \oplus \text{39} &= \text{76} \\
\text{3C} \oplus \text{39} &= \text{05}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$\boxed{W[7] = \text{2A6C7605}}$$

---

### Step 5: First Round Key

$$K_1 = W[4] \;\vert\; W[5] \;\vert\; W[6] \;\vert\; W[7] = \text{A0FAFE17 88542CB1 23A33939 2A6C7605}$$

This **exactly matches** the FIPS-197 specification for the first round key. ✔

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Key Schedules

| Property | AES-128 | AES-256 | DES |
|---|---|---|---|
| Recurrence step | 4 words | 4 words (with extra SubWord at $i \bmod 8 = 4$) | 2 halves (28 bits each) |
| Non-linearity source | SubWord (S-box) | SubWord (twice) | S-boxes inside $f$ function (schedule is linear) |
| Round constant | Yes (Rcon) | Yes (Rcon) | No |
| Invertibility of schedule | One-way (forward only) | One-way (forward only) | Bijective (PC-1 and PC-2 are permutations) |
| Related-key resistance | Strong | Strong (with extra SubWord) | Weak |
| Storage cost (worst case) | 176 bytes | 240 bytes | 768 bits (16 × 48) |

## 3.3 Derivation of $RC[j]$ in $GF(2^8)$

The AES round constants are powers of $x = \text{02}$ in the AES finite field $GF(2^8)$ with the irreducible polynomial $m(x) = x^8 + x^4 + x^3 + x + 1$:

$$RC[1] = x^0 = \text{01}$$
$$RC[2] = x^1 = \text{02}$$
$$RC[3] = x^2 = \text{04}$$
$$RC[4] = x^3 = \text{08}$$
$$RC[5] = x^4 = \text{10}$$
$$RC[6] = x^5 = \text{20}$$
$$RC[7] = x^6 = \text{40}$$
$$RC[8] = x^7 = \text{80}$$
$$RC[9] = x^8 \bmod m(x) = x^4 + x^3 + x + 1 = \text{1B}$$
$$RC[10] = x^9 = x \cdot \text{1B} = \text{36}$$

> [!NOTE]
> Beyond round 10, the round constants are not used in AES-128. For AES-192, only $RC[1]$ through $RC[8]$ are used. For AES-256, only $RC[1]$ through $RC[7]$ are used.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: AES-128 Key Expansion

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Master Key 128 bits] --> B[Split into W0 W1 W2 W3]
    B --> C{i mod 4 equals 0}
    C -- Yes --> D[RotWord of W i-1]
    D --> E[SubWord using AES S-box]
    E --> F[XOR with Rcon round by 4]
    F --> G[XOR with W i-4]
    C -- No --> H[XOR with W i-4]
    H --> I[Word W i]
    G --> I
    I --> J{All 44 words generated}
    J -- No --> C
    J -- Yes --> K[Group into 11 Round Keys]
    K --> L[Use in AES round function]

    style A fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style K fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style L fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style D fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style E fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style F fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
```

## 4.2 Detailed Functional Flow: The g() Function

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Input Word: 09 CF 4F 3C] --> Q1[RotWord]
    Q1 --> Q2[Output: CF 4F 3C 09]
    Q2 --> R1[SubWord via S-box]
    R1 --> R2[Output: 8A 84 EB 01]
    R2 --> S1[XOR with Rcon 1]
    S1 --> S2[Output: 8B 84 EB 01]
    S2 --> T1[XOR with W i-4]
    T1 --> T2[Final Word W i]

    style P1 fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style S2 fill:#98FB98,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style T2 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

## 4.3 Architecture: DES Subkey Generation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[64-bit Key with Parity] --> B[PC-1 Permutation]
    B --> C1[C0 28 bits]
    B --> C2[D0 28 bits]
    C1 --> D1[Left Shift by r i]
    C2 --> D2[Left Shift by r i]
    D1 --> E[Concatenate C i D i]
    D2 --> E
    E --> F[PC-2 Permutation]
    F --> G[Round Key K i 48 bits]
    G --> H{16 rounds done}
    H -- No --> D1
    H -- Yes --> I[16 Round Keys ready]

    style A fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style I fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style G fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
```

## 4.4 Round Key Memory Layout (AES-128)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    K0[Round 0 Key: 2B7E1516 28AED2A6 ABF71588 09CF4F3C] --> R1[Round 1 Key: A0FAFE17 88542CB1 23A33939 2A6C7605]
    R1 --> R2[Round 2 Key]
    R2 --> R3[Round 3 Key]
    R3 --> R4[Round 4 Key]
    R4 --> R5[Round 5 Key]
    R5 --> R6[Round 6 Key]
    R6 --> R7[Round 7 Key]
    R7 --> R8[Round 8 Key]
    R8 --> R9[Round 9 Key]
    R9 --> R10[Round 10 Key]

    style K0 fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style R1 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style R10 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Trick:** Round Key 0 is the master key itself. Many students mistakenly generate 10 round keys in addition to the master key, making 11 total — this is correct, but they should always label them **K0 through K10**, not K1 through K11.

---
