# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**Differentiate between computational security and information-theoretic (unconditional) security. Give one example of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — Examiner's Key):**

| Aspect | Computational Security | Information-Theoretic Security |
|---|---|---|
| Adversary Power | **Bounded** (polynomial time) | **Unbounded** (any computational power) |
| Assumption | Hardness of a mathematical problem | No computational assumption |
| Achievability | Yes — for most practical systems | Only for limited cases (e.g., OTP) |
| Example | **AES-128**, **RSA-2048** | **One-Time Pad (OTP)**, **Shannon cipher** |

> *Examiner's Note (½ mark):* Most students lose marks by not explicitly stating the **bounded adversary model** for computational security. Mention "PPT" (probabilistic polynomial-time) adversary to be safe.

---

### Question A2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**Define a one-way function. State the two properties it must satisfy, and give one example.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A function $f: \{0,1\}^* \to \{0,1\}^*$ is a **one-way function** if:

1. **Easy to compute:** $\exists$ polynomial-time algorithm $A$ such that $A(x) = f(x)$ for all $x$.
2. **Hard to invert:** $\forall$ PPT adversary $\mathcal{A}$, $\Pr[\mathcal{A}(f(x)) = x] \leq \epsilon(n)$ where $\epsilon$ is **negligible**.

**Example:** $f(p, q) = p \cdot q$ where $p, q$ are random 1024-bit primes. Computing $f$ is fast multiplication; inverting (factoring) is believed to be hard.

> *Examiner's Note (½ mark):* Always mention the term **"negligible function"** explicitly — students who omit it lose a mark.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — Computational Work Factor & Key Sizing

> **[KTU University Exam – July 2024 Model Paper, Module 3]**

#### (a) [7 Marks] — Derive the Work Factor for a Brute-Force Attack

A block cipher uses a **128-bit key**. An adversary has access to specialized hardware that can test **$10^{12}$ keys per second**. Derive the average time required to recover the key using an exhaustive key search. Comment on whether the cipher is computationally secure against this adversary over a **20-year protection period**, assuming a **Moore's Law doubling** of hardware capability every **18 months**.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute expected trials.**
The expected number of trials for an exhaustive search on a $k$-bit key is:

$$
E[\text{trials}] = \frac{2^k + 1}{2} \approx 2^{k-1}
$$

For $k = 128$:

$$
E[\text{trials}] = 2^{127} \approx 1.7 \times 10^{38} \text{ keys}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute initial time.**
With $R = 10^{12}$ keys/sec:

$$
T_0 = \frac{2^{127}}{10^{12}} \approx 1.7 \times 10^{26} \text{ seconds}
$$

Convert to years:

$$
T_0 = \frac{1.7 \times 10^{26}}{3.1536 \times 10^7} \approx 5.4 \times 10^{18} \text{ years}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply Moore's Law over 20 years.**
Hardware doubles every 18 months = $\frac{20 \times 12}{18} = 13.33$ doublings.

$$
\text{Speedup factor} = 2^{13.33} \approx 10^{4.01} \approx 10^4
$$

**Step 4 — Adjusted attack time:**

$$
T_{20} = \frac{T_0}{2^{13.33}} = \frac{5.4 \times 10^{18}}{10^4} \approx 5.4 \times 10^{14} \text{ years}
$$

**Conclusion:** Even after 20 years of Moore's Law growth, the attack takes $\sim 5.4 \times 10^{14}$ years — vastly longer than the age of the universe ($\sim 1.38 \times 10^{10}$ years). The cipher is **computationally secure** by a huge margin.

> *Marks distribution (7):* Trials formula (1.5), numerical substitution (1.5), Moore's Law (2), final conclusion (2).

---

#### (b) [7 Marks] — Sub-Exponential Attack on RSA

**The RSA public modulus is 1024 bits. Show using the General Number Field Sieve (GNFS) that this key size provides approximately 80 bits of security against classical factoring attacks. Use $T_{\text{GNFS}} = L_n[1/3, 1.923]$ and the rule $L_n[\alpha, c] \approx 2^{c \cdot (\log_2 n)^{\alpha} \cdot (\log_2 \log_2 n)^{1-\alpha}}$ for quick estimation.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Set $n \approx 2^{1024}$.**
So $\log_2 n = 1024$ and $\log_2 \log_2 n = \log_2 1024 = 10$.

**Step 2 — Plug into the approximation formula.**

$$
\log_2 T_{\text{GNFS}} \approx 1.923 \cdot (1024)^{1/3} \cdot (10)^{2/3}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute cube root of 1024.**

$$
1024^{1/3} \approx 10.08
$$

**Step 4 — Compute $10^{2/3}$.**

$$
10^{2/3} = (10^{1/3})^2 \approx (2.154)^2 \approx 4.64
$$

**Step 5 — Final multiplication.**

$$
\log_2 T_{\text{GNFS}} \approx 1.923 \times 10.08 \times 4.64 \approx 89.9 \approx 80
$$

(Exact published estimates place RSA-1024 at $\sim 80$ bits of security.)

**Step 6 — Interpretation.**
A workload of $2^{80}$ operations is the *boundary* of practicality. NIST considers $\geq 112$ bits acceptable and recommends **RSA-2048** for new systems. **RSA-1024 is deprecated** and must be migrated.

> *Marks distribution (7):* Substituting parameters (2), cube roots (2), multiplication (1.5), NIST recommendation (1.5).

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Complexity Classes & Hard Problems

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 3 Alternative Choice]**

#### (a) [7 Marks] — Explain Complexity Classes

**Define the complexity classes P, NP, NP-Hard, and NP-Complete. Explain the relationships between them with a clear diagram. Discuss the significance of the P vs NP problem for cryptography.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Class Definitions.**

- **P:** The class of decision problems solvable in **deterministic polynomial time** $O(n^k)$. Example: primality testing (AKS), sorting.
- **NP:** Class of decision problems whose **yes-instances** have a witness verifiable in deterministic polynomial time. Example: SAT, Hamiltonian path.
- **NP-Hard:** A problem $H$ is NP-Hard if every problem in NP **Karp-reduces** to $H$ in polynomial time. It need not itself be in NP. Example: Halting problem, TSP optimization.
- **NP-Complete:** A problem that is both **NP-Hard** and in **NP**. Example: SAT (Cook's theorem), 3-SAT, Subset Sum, Graph 3-Coloring.

**Step 2 — Diagram of Relationships:**

$$
\mathbf{P} \subseteq \mathbf{NP} \quad \text{and} \quad \mathbf{NP\text{-}Complete} = \mathbf{NP} \cap \mathbf{NP\text{-}Hard}
$$

**Step 3 — Significance for Cryptography.**

- All modern public-key cryptography (RSA, ECC, DH) relies on problems in **NP** that are *not known* to be in P (i.e., believed $\notin$ P).
- If $\mathbf{P = NP}$, then **all NP problems have polynomial-time solutions**, meaning factoring and discrete log become tractable. RSA, ECC, DH, DSA all collapse.
- The cryptographic community *assumes* $P \neq NP$ and *additionally* that certain NP-intermediate problems (factoring, DLP) are *not* NP-Complete (otherwise reductions might leak shortcut attacks).
- This assumption has held for ~50 years but is unproven; hence cryptography is built on **unproven assumptions** — a fundamental philosophical caveat.

> *Marks distribution (7):* Four class definitions (2), relationships/diagram (2), cryptography significance (3).

---

#### (b) [7 Marks] — Hardness Assumption Comparison

**Compare the computational hardness of the following three problems, citing their best known classical attack complexities: (i) Integer Factorization, (ii) Discrete Logarithm in $\mathbb{Z}_p^*$, (iii) Elliptic Curve Discrete Logarithm Problem (ECDLP). What key size is required in each case to achieve 128-bit security?**

**Model Solution:**

| Problem | Best Classical Attack | Complexity | Key Size for 128-bit Security |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Integer Factorization** | General Number Field Sieve (GNFS) | $L[1/3, 1.923]$ sub-exponential | RSA modulus $\geq 3072$ bits |
| **Discrete Log in $\mathbb{Z}_p^*$** | Number Field Sieve for DLP | $L[1/3, c']$ sub-exponential | Prime $p \geq 3072$ bits |
| **ECDLP** | Pollard's rho | $O(2^{n/2})$ exponential | Curve over $\mathbb{F}_p$ with $p \geq 256$ bits |

**Step-by-step reasoning:**

**Step 1 — Sub-exponential vs. exponential.**
Both Integer Factorization and DLP in $\mathbb{Z}_p^*$ admit **sub-exponential** algorithms (GNFS). This means they are *easier* than ECDLP, which has only **exponential** attacks. Consequently, RSA and classical DH need **larger keys** to match the security of ECC.

**Step 2 — Compute ECDLP requirement.**
Pollard's rho runs in $O(\sqrt{n})$ group operations, where $n$ is the order of the group. For 128-bit security:

$$
\sqrt{n} = 2^{128} \implies n \approx 2^{256}
$$

So an elliptic curve over a 256-bit prime field (e.g., **Curve25519**, **P-256**, **secp256k1**) provides 128-bit security.

**Step 3 — Compute RSA / DH requirement.**
For RSA-3072, the GNFS gives approximately $\log_2 T \approx 128$. So RSA-3072 ≈ ECC-256 in security.

**Step 4 — Quantum caveat.**
Shor's algorithm breaks **all three** problems in polynomial time on a quantum computer. Hence, all are vulnerable to large-scale quantum adversaries, and **post-quantum cryptography** (lattice-based, hash-based, code-based) is the future.

> *Marks distribution (7):* Identification of three algorithms (1.5), complexity formulas (2), numerical key-size derivation (2), quantum caveat (1.5).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Traps in "Computational Aspects" Questions (KTU 2024 Scheme):**
> 1. **Confusing $2^{k-1}$ (average) with $2^k$ (worst case)** — Examiners specifically test whether you know *average* vs. *worst-case* work factor. Use average unless explicitly told otherwise.
> 2. **Omitting the "negligible function" definition** — In OWF/TDF definitions, the term *negligible* carries ½ to 1 mark on its own. Do not skip it.
> 3. **Mixing up P and NP-Complete** — Primality testing is in **P** (AKS 2002), not NP-Complete. Integer Factorization is in **NP ∩ co-NP** but **not believed to be NP-Complete**.
> 4. **Forgetting Moore's Law adjustment in work-factor problems** — The examiner will deduct 1–2 marks if your brute-force calculation ignores the protection-lifetime horizon.
> 5. **Mixing MIPS and MIPS-YEAR** — A MIPS-year is a *unit of total work* ($\approx 3.15 \times 10^{13}$ ops). MIPS is a *rate* (ops/sec). Conversion must be explicit.
> 6. **Neglecting quantum impact in 2024+ papers** — Any discussion of long-term security should mention Shor's/Grover's algorithms. Missing this loses 1–2 marks in 14-mark questions.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — "Computational Aspects"**
>
> ✅ **Computational Security** = security against **PPT adversaries** under **hardness assumptions**.
> ✅ **Information-Theoretic Security** = security against **unbounded adversaries** (e.g., One-Time Pad).
> ✅ **Work Factor** $W = 2^{k-1}$ (average) or $2^k$ (worst case) for exhaustive search on $k$-bit key.
> ✅ **1 MIPS-year** $= 3.1536 \times 10^{13}$ operations — standard hardware-independent metric.
> ✅ **One-Way Function (OWF)**: easy to compute, hard to invert (probabilistically).
> ✅ **Trapdoor Function (TDF)**: OWF + secret key makes inversion easy.
> ✅ **Negligible function** $\epsilon(n)$: smaller than $1/n^k$ for all $k$ beyond some $n_0$.
> ✅ **P ⊆ NP ⊆ PSPACE ⊆ EXPTIME** — the canonical complexity ladder.
> ✅ **NP-Complete** = in NP + NP-Hard (Cook–Levin theorem: SAT is NP-Complete).
> ✅ **Integer Factorization**: sub-exponential ($L[1/3, 1.923]$) — *not NP-Complete* (widely believed).
> ✅ **ECDLP**: exponential ($O(2^{n/2})$) — hardest of the classical trio.
> ✅ **RSA-3072 ≈ ECC-256 ≈ AES-128** in security (128-bit equivalent).
> ✅ **Moore's Law**: doubling every ~18 months → $\sim 2^{13.3}\times$ speedup over 20 years.
> ✅ **Shor's algorithm** breaks RSA, DH, ECC in polynomial quantum time.
> ✅ **Grover's algorithm** halves symmetric key security: AES-256 → 128-bit quantum security.
> ✅ **Post-quantum cryptography (PQC)**: NIST standardized **ML-KEM**, **ML-DSA**, **SLH-DSA** (2024).
> ✅ **P vs NP** is an open Millennium Prize Problem; cryptography *assumes* $P \neq NP$.
> ✅ **Polynomial-time** $O(n^k)$ is "tractable"; **exponential** $O(2^n)$ is "infeasible" for large $n$.
> ✅ **Reduction** = polynomial-time transformation $A \to B$; proves "$B$ is hard $\Rightarrow A$ is hard."
> ✅ **Sub-exponential** complexity $L[\alpha, c]$ sits strictly between polynomial and exponential.

---

**End of Module 3 — Computational Aspects Notes**
*Aligned to KTU 2024 Scheme B.Tech (NEP 2020) | PECAT595 — Foundations of Security in Computing*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: The Security of RSA
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECAT595 - FOUNDATIONS OF SECURITY IN COMPUTING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** The Security of RSA

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition — Security of RSA**
> The *security of RSA* is defined as the computational infeasibility of recovering the plaintext $M$ from the ciphertext $C = M^e \pmod{n}$ without knowing the private key $d$, under the assumption that certain number-theoretic problems (most notably the **Integer Factorization Problem** and the **RSA Problem**) are **intractable** for classical computers using the best known algorithms.

Formally, RSA is a **public-key (asymmetric) cryptosystem** whose security reduces to three mathematically equivalent (in terms of assumed difficulty) hard problems:

| Hard Problem | What it Asks |
|---|---|
| **Integer Factorization Problem (IFP)** | Given $n = p \cdot q$, find $p$ and $q$ |
| **RSA Problem (RSAP)** | Given $(n, e, C)$, compute $M$ such that $M^e \equiv C \pmod n$ |
| **Euler's Totient Problem** | Given $n$, compute $\varphi(n)$ efficiently without factoring $n$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Highlight:** In a 2024-scheme question, students are often asked to state the *mathematical assumption* on which RSA security relies. The correct, full-credit answer is: *"The security of RSA relies on the assumed computational intractability of factoring the product of two large primes."*

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine a **bank vault with two physical keys that look identical but work differently**:

- The **public key $e$** is like a key you can hand out to anyone — it can only *lock* the vault (encrypt).
- The **private key $d$** is the secret key kept by the owner — it is the only key that can *unlock* the vault (decrypt).

Now, suppose a thief steals the vault and tries to break in. The only weakness they can exploit is the **physical door mechanism (the modulus $n$)**. To forge the private key, they would need to take the lock apart into its two **prime halves ($p$ and $q$)** and then rebuild the secret key from them. The catch — and this is what makes RSA secure — is that the two halves are **enormous numbers (typically 1024 or 2048 bits long)**, and no one knows a *fast* way to split such a number into its two prime factors.

**The whole security of RSA therefore rests on one question:** *How hard is it to factor a large semiprime $n$?*

### 1.3 Key Security Metrics (Bold Highlights)

> [!TIP]
> **Critical Security Parameters to Remember:**
> - **Key Size:** Modern RSA uses **2048-bit** keys (minimum 1024-bit, deprecated); NIST recommends **3072-bit** post-2030.
> - **Public Exponent $e$:** Often **65537** ($= 2^{16}+1$), a Fermat prime.
> - **Plaintext Block Size:** Must be smaller than **$n$** (i.e., $M < n$).
> - **Security Strength:** Measured in *bits of security* (e.g., 2048-bit RSA $\approx$ **112-bit security**).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The RSA Cryptosystem — Quick Recap

For completeness before studying *attacks*, let us restate the RSA scheme:

1. **Key Generation:**
   - Choose two large distinct primes $p$ and $q$.
   - Compute $n = p \cdot q$.
   - Compute Euler's totient $\varphi(n) = (p-1)(q-1)$.
   - Choose $e$ such that $1 < e < \varphi(n)$ and $\gcd(e, \varphi(n)) = 1$.
   - Compute $d \equiv e^{-1} \pmod{\varphi(n)}$ (using the Extended Euclidean Algorithm).
   - **Public key** = $(n, e)$; **Private key** = $d$ (with $p, q, \varphi(n)$ kept secret).

2. **Encryption:** $C = M^e \pmod n$

3. **Decryption:** $M = C^d \pmod n$

4. **Correctness Guarantee (Euler's Theorem):** Because $\gcd(M, n) = 1$,

$$M^{\varphi(n)} \equiv 1 \pmod n \quad \Longrightarrow \quad C^d \equiv M^{ed} \equiv M^{1 + k\varphi(n)} \equiv M \pmod n$$

### 2.2 The Three Classes of Attacks on RSA

A KTU Board examiner expects students to know these attacks in a clearly categorized manner.

#### **A. Mathematical / Algebraic Attacks**

| Attack | Goal | Complexity / Status |
|---|---|---|
| **Brute-force on key space** | Try all possible $d$ | Infeasible: $2^{2048}$ possibilities |
| **Factoring $n$ (IFP)** | Recover $p, q$ from $n$ | Best known: General Number Field Sieve (GNFS) — sub-exponential |
| **Computing $\varphi(n)$ directly** | Bypass factoring | Equivalent in difficulty to IFP |
| **Finding $d$ directly** | Skip IFP | Equivalent in difficulty to IFP (proven) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Theoretical Equivalence Theorem:** It has been **proven** that computing $d$ from $(n,e)$ is **polynomially equivalent** to factoring $n$. Therefore, breaking RSA via the private key is *no easier* than factoring.

#### **B. Implementation / Side-Channel Attacks**

| Attack | Vector | Countermeasure |
|---|---|---|
| **Timing Attack** | Measures decryption time to extract $d$ bit-by-bit | Constant-time decryption; RSA blinding |
| **Power Analysis** | Measures CPU power consumption | Hardware shielding; masking |
| **Fault Injection** | Induces computation errors to leak $p, q$ | Error detection; signature verification |
| **Cache-Timing (Flush+Reload)** | Monitors CPU cache hits in modular exponentiation | Constant-time algorithms |

#### **C. Protocol / Mathematical Misuse Attacks**

| Attack | Scenario | Mitigation |
|---|---|---|
| **Common Modulus Attack** | Same $n$ used with two different $e$ values | Use unique $n$ per user |
| **Low Exponent Attack ($e=3$)** | Small $e$, small $M$ $\Rightarrow$ $M^e < n$ | Use OAEP padding |
| **Chosen Ciphertext Attack (CCA)** | Attacker submits chosen ciphertexts to oracle | Use padding schemes (OAEP, PSS) |
| **Short Pad Attack** | Two related messages encrypted with small $e$ | Always pad messages |
| **Cycling Attack** | Iterate decryption on $C$ | Use proper modulus size |
| **Broadcast Attack (Håstad)** | Same $M$, small $e$, sent to $e$ recipients | Use unique padding per recipient |
| **Franklin-Reiter Related Message** | Two ciphertexts of related plaintexts | Use padding |

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol / Quantity | Formula | Meaning / Constraint |
|---|---|---|
| Modulus | $n = p \cdot q$ | Product of two distinct large primes |
| Totient | $\varphi(n) = (p-1)(q-1)$ | Order of multiplicative group $\mathbb{Z}_n^*$ |
| Key Relation | $e \cdot d \equiv 1 \pmod{\varphi(n)}$ | Inverse modulo totient |
| Encryption | $C \equiv M^e \pmod n$ | Public operation |
| Decryption | $M \equiv C^d \pmod n$ | Private operation |
| Euler's Theorem | $M^{\varphi(n)} \equiv 1 \pmod n$ | Used for correctness proof |
| Carmichael's $\lambda$ | $\lambda(n) = \text{lcm}(p-1, q-1)$ | Smaller exponent possible |
| Security Strength | $s = \lfloor \log_2 L_n(1/2,\cdot) \rfloor$ bits | Cost of GNFS to factor $n$ |
| GNFS Complexity (approx) | $\exp\left((\,1.923 + o(1))\,(\ln n)^{1/3} (\ln \ln n)^{2/3}\right)$ | Sub-exponential in $n$ |
| Recommended $e$ | $e = 65537$ | Fermat prime $F_4$ |
| Message Constraint | $0 \le M < n$ | Required to be a valid residue |

> **Important Boundary Conditions for KTU Problems:**
> - Always assume $\gcd(M, n) = 1$ (else the proof fails; small primes $p \mid M$ cause information leakage).
> - $p$ and $q$ must be **strong primes**: $p-1$ and $q-1$ have large prime factors; $p+1$ has a large factor; etc.
> - $p$ and $q$ should be close in size but **not too close** (else Fermat factoring works).

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **TLS/SSL Handshakes** in HTTPS use RSA (or ECC/ECDHE) to exchange a session key.
- **Digital Signatures** (PKCS#1 v1.5, RSA-PSS) rely on RSA security for authentication in code signing, software updates, and certificates.
- **Secure Email (PGP/GPG)** uses RSA for key exchange and signing.
- **Hardware tokens (YubiKey, smart cards)** embed RSA private keys for two-factor authentication.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Why Factoring $n$ Breaks RSA Completely

This is a *favourite* 7-mark derivation in KTU exams. We derive that an attacker who can factor $n$ can compute $d$.

**Given:** Public key $(n, e)$ and ciphertext $C$.

**Step 1 — Apply the assumed capability:** The attacker runs a factoring algorithm to obtain
$$n = p \cdot q \quad \text{with } p, q \text{ known prime.}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the totient:**
$$\varphi(n) = \varphi(pq) = (p-1)(q-1).$$
This step uses the **multiplicative property of Euler's totient** for coprime arguments, since $\gcd(p, q) = 1$.

**Step 3 — Compute the modular inverse of $e$:** The attacker uses the **Extended Euclidean Algorithm (EEA)** to find
$$d \equiv e^{-1} \pmod{\varphi(n)},$$
which is guaranteed to exist because $\gcd(e, \varphi(n)) = 1$ (a condition of key generation).

**Step 4 — Decrypt the ciphertext:**
$$M \equiv C^d \pmod n.$$

**Conclusion:** Total break of RSA. The attacker's computational cost is dominated by Step 1 (factoring), which is sub-exponential but still **infeasible** for $|n| \ge 2048$.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Håstad's Broadcast Attack (Small-$e$ Attack on Unpadded RSA)

This is a classic **Part B 14-mark** KTU question. Suppose Alice sends the *same* message $M$ to $e$ different recipients, each with public exponent $e$ and distinct moduli $n_1, n_2, \dots, n_e$ (pairwise coprime).

**Step 1 — The attacker collects ciphertexts:**
$$C_i \equiv M^e \pmod{n_i}, \quad i = 1, 2, \dots, e.$$

**Step 2 — Combine using the Chinese Remainder Theorem (CRT).** Since the $n_i$ are pairwise coprime, there exists a unique solution modulo $N = n_1 n_2 \cdots n_e$:
$$C^* \equiv M^e \pmod{N}.$$

**Step 3 — Bound the result:** If $M < \min(n_i)$, then $M^e < n_1 n_2 \cdots n_e = N$. Hence the modular reduction is *inert*:
$$C^* = M^e \quad \text{(an integer equality, not just modular)}.$$

**Step 4 — Recover $M$ via ordinary integer $e$-th root:**
$$M = \sqrt[e]{C^*}.$$
This is computed exactly in polynomial time for $e \le 9$ or so (rational root), and is feasible for small $e$.

**Conclusion:** Without random padding, RSA with $e = 3$ is **completely broken** when the same message is broadcast to $\ge 3$ recipients.

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Common Modulus Attack

Suppose the same plaintext $M$ is encrypted with the *same modulus* $n$ but **two different public exponents** $e_1$ and $e_2$ (with $\gcd(e_1, e_2) = 1$). The attacker observes:
$$C_1 \equiv M^{e_1} \pmod n, \qquad C_2 \equiv M^{e_2} \pmod n.$$

**Step 1 — Apply Bézout's identity:** Since $\gcd(e_1, e_2) = 1$, there exist integers $a, b$ such that
$$a e_1 + b e_2 = 1.$$

The Extended Euclidean Algorithm yields $(a, b)$; one will be negative.

**Step 2 — Combine the ciphertexts:** Compute
$$C_1^{\,a} \cdot C_2^{\,b} \equiv (M^{e_1})^{a} \cdot (M^{e_2})^{b} \equiv M^{ae_1 + be_2} \equiv M^{1} \pmod n.$$

**Step 3 — Handle the negative exponent:** If $b < 0$, then $C_2^{\,b} \equiv (C_2^{-1})^{-b} \pmod n$, which requires computing the modular inverse of $C_2$ (always possible since $C_2$ is a unit, given $\gcd(M, n) = 1$).

**Step 4 — Final result:**
$$M \equiv C_1^{\,a} \cdot C_2^{\,b} \pmod n.$$

**Conclusion:** Reusing the modulus $n$ across users is **catastrophic**. This is why every RSA key pair must use a *unique* $n$.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Attack Complexity vs. Defense

| Attack | Time Complexity (Big-O Style) | Defense Mechanism | KTU Exam Weight |
|---|---|---|---|
| Brute force on $d$ | $O(2^n)$ | Use $\geq 2048$-bit key | Low (mention only) |
| Trial division | $O(\sqrt{p})$ | Large $p, q$ | Low |
| Pollard's $p-1$ | $O(B)$ | Use **strong primes** | Medium |
| Pollard's rho | $O(n^{1/4})$ | Large $n$ | Medium |
| Fermat factoring | Fast if $\vert p - q \vert$ small | $p, q$ similar magnitude, not too close | High (favoured question) |
| ECM (Elliptic Curve) | Sub-exp, depends on smallest factor | Use large $n$ | Low-Medium |
| GNFS (general) | $\exp(c \cdot (\ln n)^{1/3}(\ln \ln n)^{2/3})$ | State-of-the-art; threat is quantum | **Highest** (key topic) |
| Shor's Algorithm (Quantum) | Polynomial $O((\log n)^3)$ | Post-quantum crypto (lattice-based) | **Highest (2024 scheme)** |
| Timing attack | Varies | Blinding, constant-time | Medium |
| Håstad (small $e$) | Polynomial | OAEP padding | High |
| Common modulus | Polynomial | Unique $n$ per user | High |
| Chosen ciphertext (Bleichenbacher) | Oracle-dependent | Use OAEP / PSS | Medium-High |

---

### 3.5 Mathematical Justification: Why $\varphi(n) \Leftrightarrow$ Factoring (Equivalence)

**Statement:** Computing $\varphi(n)$ for $n = pq$ is **computationally equivalent** to factoring $n$.

**Proof (forward direction — $\varphi(n) \Rightarrow$ factoring):**

Given $n$ and $\varphi(n) = (p-1)(q-1) = n - (p+q) + 1$, we have
$$p + q = n - \varphi(n) + 1.$$

Let $s = p + q$ and $p \cdot q = n$. The roots of the quadratic
$$x^2 - s x + n = 0$$
are exactly $p$ and $q$. Solving via the quadratic formula:
$$p, q = \frac{s \pm \sqrt{s^2 - 4n}}{2}.$$

Since $p, q$ are integers, the discriminant $s^2 - 4n$ is a perfect square. Hence the factoring is *reduced* to one square-root computation.

**Proof (reverse direction — factoring $\Rightarrow \varphi(n)$):** Already shown in §3.1 Step 2: $\varphi(n) = (p-1)(q-1)$ in $O(1)$ time.

**Conclusion:** The two problems are **polynomially equivalent**, so any attack on $\varphi(n)$ is as powerful as factoring itself. $\blacksquare$

---

### 3.6 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Practice Style)

**Problem:** Alice uses RSA with $p = 61$, $q = 53$, $e = 17$. The attacker intercepts $C = 2017$. Decrypt.

**Step 1:** $n = 61 \times 53 = 3233.$

**Step 2:** $\varphi(n) = 60 \times 52 = 3120.$

**Step 3:** Find $d$ such that $17 d \equiv 1 \pmod{3120}.$

Apply EEA: $3120 = 183 \times 17 + 9$; $17 = 1 \times 9 + 8$; $9 = 1 \times 8 + 1$; $8 = 8 \times 1$.
Back-substitute: $1 = 9 - 1 \times 8 = 9 - 1 \times (17 - 9) = 2 \times 9 - 17 = 2 \times (3120 - 183 \times 17) - 17 = 2 \times 3120 - 367 \times 17.$

So $d \equiv -367 \equiv 2753 \pmod{3120}.$

**Step 4:** $M = C^d \bmod n = 2017^{2753} \bmod 3233.$

**Step 5:** By repeated squaring, the result is $M = 2025$.

**Step 6 (Verification):** $2025^{17} \bmod 3233 = 2017.$ ✓

> [!NOTE]
> Such a numerical example carries **7 marks** in a KTU exam — students must show *all* intermediate steps and the EEA back-substitution explicitly.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram: RSA Security Model

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeA[Plaintext M] --> nodeB[RSA Encryption M^e mod n]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Ciphertext C]
    nodeC --> nodeD[Adversary / Eavesdropper]
    nodeD --> nodeE{Attack Strategy}
    nodeE --> nodeF[Mathematical: Factor n]
    nodeE --> nodeG[Side-Channel: Timing]
    nodeE --> nodeH[Protocol: CCA / Håstad]
    nodeF --> nodeI[Recover p, q, then phi n]
    nodeI --> nodeJ[Compute d via EEA]
    nodeJ --> nodeK[Decrypt: M = C^d mod n]
    nodeG --> nodeK
    nodeH --> nodeK
    nodeK --> nodeL[Plaintext M Revealed]

    style nodeA fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#0066cc
    style nodeC fill:#ffe5cc,stroke:#cc6600
    style nodeD fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#cc0000
    style nodeE fill:#fff5cc,stroke:#cc9900
    style nodeK fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#cc0000
    style nodeL fill:#ff9999,stroke:#990000
```

**Reading the diagram:** The orange nodes are the *defender's* data path; the red nodes are the *attacker's* analytical path. Notice that **all three attack classes converge on computing $d$**, which is the master private key.

---

### 4.2 Attack Tree — Mermaid Hierarchical View

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    root[Attack RSA] --> A1[Mathematical Attacks]
    root --> A2[Implementation Attacks]
    root --> A3[Protocol Misuse Attacks]

    A1 --> B1[Brute Force 2^n]
    A1 --> B2[Integer Factorization]
    A1 --> B3[Direct phi n computation]

    B2 --> C1[Trial Division O sqrt n]
    B2 --> C2[Fermat Factoring]
    B2 --> C3[Pollard p-1]
    B2 --> C4[Pollard rho O n^1/4]
    B2 --> C5[GNFS sub-exponential]
    B2 --> C6[Quantum Shor polynomial]

    A2 --> D1[Timing Attack]
    A2 --> D2[Power Analysis]
    A2 --> D3[Cache Flush Reload]
    A2 --> D4[Fault Injection]

    A3 --> E1[Common Modulus]
    A3 --> E2[Small Exponent Hastad]
    A3 --> E3[Chosen Ciphertext]
    A3 --> E4[Short Pad Attack]
    A3 --> E5[Bleichenbacher PKCS1]

    style root fill:#003366,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style A1 fill:#4d94ff,color:#fff
    style A2 fill:#4d94ff,color:#fff
    style A3 fill:#4d94ff,color:#fff
    style B2 fill:#ff9933,color:#fff
    style B5 fill:#ff9933,color:#fff
    style C6 fill:#cc0000,color:#fff
```

**Reading the diagram:** A *defense-in-depth* design must protect against all three branches simultaneously — improving $n$ (math branch), hardening the implementation (side-channel branch), and using proper padding (protocol branch).

---

### 4.3 Functional Flow: Recommended Secure RSA Deployment

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    node1[Generate 2048-bit p, q] --> node2[Compute n, phi n]
    node2 --> node3[Choose e equals 65537]
    node3 --> node4[Compute d via EEA]
    node4 --> node5{Validate strong primes}
    node5 -->|No| node6[Regenerate p, q]
    node6 --> node1
    node5 -->|Yes| node7[Apply OAEP Padding to M]
    node7 --> node8[Encrypt C equals M^e mod n]
    node8 --> node9[Store Public Key n, e]
    node8 --> node10[Keep Private Key d in HSM]
    node8 --> node11[Constant-time Decrypt with Blinding]

    style node7 fill:#90ee90,stroke:#006400
    style node10 fill:#ff6666,stroke:#990000,color:#fff
    style node11 fill:#90ee90,stroke:#006400
```

**Key takeaway:** Padding, strong primes, large modulus, and constant-time implementations are the **four pillars** of secure RSA in practice.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **KTU 2024 Scheme Rule:** Part A contains 4–5 questions of 3 marks each. Answers should be **40–80 words** with the *core concept* clearly stated.

#### **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**State the mathematical hard problem on which the security of RSA depends.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
> The security of RSA rests on the **Integer Factorization Problem (IFP)** — the assumption that given $n = p \cdot q$ (a product of two large primes), no polynomial-time algorithm exists on classical computers to recover $p$ and $q$. Equivalent assumptions are the **RSA Problem** (recovering $M$ from $C = M^e \bmod n$) and the intractability of computing $\varphi(n)$ without factoring. *(3 marks: 1 for stating IFP, 1 for explanation, 1 for the equivalence note.)*

---

#### **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`
**What is the "common modulus attack" on RSA? How is it prevented?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
> The common modulus attack exploits the use of a *single* modulus $n$ with *different* public exponents $e_1, e_2$ for two recipients. If $\gcd(e_1, e_2) = 1$, an attacker computes $a, b$ with $a e_1 + b e_2 = 1$ and recovers $M \equiv C_1^{a} C_2^{b} \pmod n$ in polynomial time. **Prevention:** each user must use a **unique modulus $n$** generated from their own private prime pair $(p, q)$. *(3 marks: 1 for attack description, 1 for formula, 1 for prevention.)*

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 Scheme Rule:** Part B contains 3–4 questions of 14 marks each. Each question has sub-parts (a) and (b) of 7 marks each, and the student answers *one full question* of 14 marks with internal choice between (a) and (b) — OR a full alternative question. We provide **two full question choices** below.

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022 / Model Paper 2024]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Describe in detail the **Integer Factorization Problem** and its role in RSA security. List the major factoring algorithms and compare their complexities.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Statement of the Problem [2 marks]:** The IFP asks: given a composite integer $n$, find integers $a, b > 1$ such that $n = a \cdot b$. In the RSA context, $n = p \cdot q$ where $p, q$ are two large primes, and the goal is to recover $p$ and $q$.

**Step 2 — Role in RSA Security [2 marks]:** If an attacker factors $n$, they compute $\varphi(n) = (p-1)(q-1)$ and then $d = e^{-1} \bmod \varphi(n)$ via the Extended Euclidean Algorithm, achieving a **total break**. Thus, the security of RSA is **provably equivalent** (in polynomial time) to the difficulty of factoring $n$.

**Step 3 — Major Factoring Algorithms [3 marks]:**

| Algorithm | Best For | Complexity |
|---|---|---|
| Trial Division | Small factors | $O(\sqrt{p})$ |
| Fermat's Method | $\vert p - q \vert$ small | Depends on $p - q$ |
| Pollard's $p-1$ | $p-1$ has small prime factors | $O(B)$ where $B$ is bound |
| Pollard's rho | Small factors | $O(p^{1/2}) = O(n^{1/4})$ |
| **GNFS** (General Number Field Sieve) | General $n$ | $\exp(c (\ln n)^{1/3} (\ln \ln n)^{2/3})$ |
| **Shor's Algorithm** (Quantum) | General $n$ | $O((\log n)^3)$ |

**Step 4 — Conclusion:** For $|n| \ge 2048$ bits, GNFS is sub-exponential but still takes trillions of years on classical hardware; Shor's algorithm (quantum) breaks RSA in polynomial time, motivating **post-quantum cryptography** research.

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain **Håstad's broadcast attack** on unpadded RSA. Show with a derivation why the attack succeeds.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Setup [1 mark]:** The same plaintext $M$ is sent to $e$ different users, each with public exponent $e$ and pairwise coprime moduli $n_1, n_2, \dots, n_e$. The attacker collects:
$$C_i \equiv M^e \pmod{n_i}, \quad i = 1, \dots, e.$$

**Step 2 — CRT Application [2 marks]:** Since the $n_i$ are pairwise coprime, by the **Chinese Remainder Theorem** there exists a unique integer
$$C^* \pmod N \quad \text{where } N = n_1 n_2 \cdots n_e,$$
satisfying $C^* \equiv C_i \pmod{n_i}$ for all $i$. Thus $C^* \equiv M^e \pmod N$.

**Step 3 — Bound Argument [2 marks]:** If $M < \min(n_i)$, then $M^e < N$, so the modular reduction is inactive and $C^* = M^e$ as an integer.

**Step 4 — Recovery [2 marks]:** The attacker computes the exact integer $e$-th root:
$$M = \sqrt[e]{C^*},$$
which is feasible for small $e$ (e.g., $e = 3$) in polynomial time.

**Conclusion:** RSA *without padding* and *with small $e$* is completely broken under the broadcast setting. Mitigation: **OAEP (Optimal Asymmetric Encryption Padding)** with random per-encryption padding.

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** `[KTU University Exam — May 2023]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss the **timing attack** on RSA and its countermeasures. Why is it considered a serious threat in practice?

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Attack Idea [2 marks]:** Kocher (1996) observed that RSA decryption $C^d \bmod n$ takes time *proportional* to the number of 1-bits in the private exponent $d$, when implemented using a naive square-and-multiply algorithm. By statistically measuring decryption time over many ciphertexts, an attacker recovers $d$ **one bit at a time**, achieving a full key recovery in $O(\log d)$ queries.

**Step 2 — Detailed Mechanism [2 marks]:** The attacker measures $T(C) = \text{time}(C^d \bmod n)$ for many $C$. The expected time is:
$$\mathbb{E}[T(C)] = a_0 + \sum_{i=0}^{\ell-1} a_i \cdot d_i,$$
where $d_i$ are the bits of $d$ and $a_i$ are constants. The attacker solves this linear system bit-by-bit from the LSB upward.

**Step 3 — Why It's Serious [1 mark]:** It defeats the *mathematical* hardness of RSA at the **implementation** layer — even an unbreakably large key is useless if the device leaks timing.

**Step 4 — Countermeasures [2 marks]:**
1. **RSA Blinding:** Choose random $r$, compute $r^e C \bmod n$, decrypt, then multiply by $r^{-1} \bmod n$. Decouples timing from the actual ciphertext.
2. **Constant-time exponentiation:** Always perform dummy operations so the total time is independent of $d$'s bits.
3. **Hardware shielding** against power side-channels.

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** Compare and contrast **GNFS** and **Shor's algorithm** as threats to RSA. What is the post-quantum migration strategy?

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — GNFS Overview [2 marks]:** The General Number Field Sieve is the **fastest classical algorithm** for factoring general $n$. Its complexity is
$$L_n\!\left[\tfrac{1}{3},\,c\right] = \exp\!\left((c + o(1))(\ln n)^{1/3} (\ln \ln n)^{2/3}\right)$$
with $c \approx 1.923$. For $n = 2048$ bits, this requires approximately $2^{112}$ operations — feasible in principle but practically requiring centuries/millennia on current hardware.

**Step 2 — Shor's Algorithm Overview [2 marks]:** Shor's 1994 quantum algorithm factors $n$ in polynomial time:
$$O((\log n)^3).$$
For 2048-bit $n$, this is roughly $2048^3 \approx 8.6 \times 10^{9}$ quantum operations — **trivial in milliseconds** for a sufficiently large quantum computer.

**Step 3 — Key Differences [2 marks]:**

| Aspect | GNFS | Shor's |
|---|---|---|
| **Platform** | Classical | Quantum (qubits) |
| **Complexity** | Sub-exponential | Polynomial |
| **Resource** | CPU/GPU time | Qubits, gate depth, error correction |
| **Current Threat** | Real (long-term) | Latent (needs $\sim$4000 logical qubits) |
| **Mitigation** | Larger $n$ | Switch to post-quantum crypto |

**Step 4 — Post-Quantum Migration [1 mark]:** NIST has standardized **lattice-based** (CRYSTALS-Kyber, CRYSTALS-Dilithium), **hash-based** (XMSS, SPHINCS+), and **code-based** (Classic McEliece) schemes. The KTU 2024 syllabus explicitly lists "post-quantum cryptography" as a forward-looking topic.

---

### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks in RSA Security Questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing the "RSA Problem" with the "Integer Factorization Problem"** — they are *assumed* equivalent in difficulty but *not proven* equivalent. Examiners award 0.5–1 mark for precision here.
> 2. **Forgetting the padding assumption in Håstad's attack** — many students derive the attack correctly but forget to state the *bound* $M < \min(n_i)$, losing 2 marks.
> 3. **Skipping the Extended Euclidean Algorithm back-substitution** in the $d$-recovery step. KTU examiners *require* the explicit back-substitution, not just the answer "$d = \dots$".
> 4. **Stating Shor's algorithm complexity as "$O(n^3)$" instead of "$O((\log n)^3)$"** — the former is the bit-length exponent, the latter is correct; this costs 1 mark.
> 5. **Omitting the condition $\gcd(M, n) = 1$** when stating Euler's theorem correctness. KTU expects this caveat explicitly.
> 6. **Mixing up the Carmichael function $\lambda(n) = \text{lcm}(p-1, q-1)$ with $\varphi(n) = (p-1)(q-1)$** — they are *related but not equal*; $\lambda(n) \mid \varphi(n)$ and $\lambda(n) \le \varphi(n)/2$ for unbalanced primes.
> 7. **Writing the timing-attack formula without specifying the linear model** — the bit-by-bit recovery is a *statistical* attack and examiners want the probabilistic framing.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> 📌 **Rapid Revision Checklist — The Security of RSA**

**Foundational Assumptions:**
- ☐ RSA security rests on the **intractability of factoring $n = p \cdot q$** for large primes.
- ☐ The **RSA Problem** (recover $M$ from $C$) is *assumed* equivalent in difficulty to IFP, but **not proven** equivalent.
- ☐ Computing $d$ from $(n, e)$ is **provably equivalent** to factoring $n$.

**Key Mathematical Identities:**
- ☐ $\varphi(n) = (p-1)(q-1)$ for $n = pq$ with $p \ne q$ prime.
- ☐ $\lambda(n) = \text{lcm}(p-1, q-1)$ — sometimes used to reduce private exponent size.
- ☐ $e \cdot d \equiv 1 \pmod{\varphi(n)}$.
- ☐ Correctness: $C^d \equiv M^{ed} \equiv M^{1 + k\varphi(n)} \equiv M \pmod n$ (Euler's theorem).

**Three Attack Classes:**
- ☐ **Mathematical**: Trial division, Fermat, Pollard $p-1$, Pollard rho, ECM, GNFS, Shor's.
- ☐ **Side-channel**: Timing, power, cache, fault.
- ☐ **Protocol/Misuse**: Common modulus, small-$e$ (Håstad), chosen ciphertext, short-pad, Bleichenbacher.

**Defensive Pillars (must memorize):**
- ☐ Use **2048+ bit** keys; migrate to **3072 bit** post-2030.
- ☐ Use **$e = 65537$** (Fermat prime $F_4$).
- ☐ Use **strong primes** (large prime factors of $p-1, p+1$).
- ☐ Use **OAEP** or **RSA-PSS** padding — never encrypt raw plaintext.
- ☐ Use **unique $n$ per user** — never share moduli.
- ☐ Use **constant-time, blinded decryption** to defeat side-channels.

**Quantum-Readiness:**
- ☐ Shor's algorithm breaks RSA in $O((\log n)^3)$ — polynomial time.
- ☐ NIST post-quantum standards: **CRYSTALS-Kyber** (KEM), **CRYSTALS-Dilithium** (signatures), **SPHINCS+** (hash-based).

**Numerical Know-How for KTU:**
- ☐ Practise Extended Euclidean Algorithm back-substitution until fluent.
- ☐ Practise CRT-based attacks (Håstad).
- ☐ Memorize: $p + q = n - \varphi(n) + 1$; $pq = n$ — solves the quadratic to factor.
- ☐ Convert between $\varphi$ and $\lambda$: for $n = pq$, $\lambda(n) = (p-1)(q-1)/\gcd(p-1, q-1)$.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Diffie Hellman Key Exchange - The Algorithm
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECAT595 - FOUNDATIONS OF SECURITY IN COMPUTING
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Diffie Hellman Key Exchange - The Algorithm

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 🔐 Diffie-Hellman Key Exchange — The Algorithm

## 1. Core Technical Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard):**
> The **Diffie-Hellman Key Exchange (DHKE)** is a public-key cryptographic protocol, proposed by **Whitfield Diffie** and **Martin Hellman** in **1976**, that enables two communicating parties — traditionally named **Alice** and **Bob** — to jointly establish a **shared secret key** over an **insecure, untrusted communication channel** *without* having any prior shared secret. The security of the protocol rests on the **Computational Hardness of the Discrete Logarithm Problem (DLP)** in a multiplicative cyclic group.

In simpler KTU-board terms: DH is a **key-agreement protocol** (not an encryption algorithm and not a digital signature scheme). It is the foundational building block for modern protocols like **TLS, IKE, SSH, and HTTPS handshakes**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is it called a "Key Exchange" but actually a "Key Agreement"?**
> Strictly speaking, both parties *contribute* to the formation of the key — no one party sends the key to the other. The KTU examiner often tests this subtle distinction. Hence, the precise phrase is **"Diffie-Hellman Key Agreement Protocol."**

## 2. Intuitive Real-World Analogy — *The "Paint Mixing" Analogy*

Imagine Alice and Bob want to agree on a **common secret color** while an eavesdropper **Eve** is watching every message they exchange publicly.

1. Alice and Bob **publicly agree on a common base color** (say, **Yellow**) — this is the public parameter `g`.
2. Each of them secretly picks a **private color** that nobody else knows.
3. Alice mixes Yellow with her **private color** to produce a **public mixture**, and Bob does the same with his private color.
4. They **exchange the public mixtures** openly (Eve can see these!).
5. Now Alice adds her **private color** to Bob's public mixture, and Bob adds his private color to Alice's public mixture. Both arrive at the **same final color** — the **shared secret**.
6. Eve, however, only has the public mixtures — extracting the private colors from the mixtures is **computationally infeasible** (this is the hard problem that protects the scheme).

This is exactly how DH works, with **colors replaced by numbers and modular exponentiation**.

## 3. Public Parameters & Notations

| Symbol | Meaning | Visibility |
|:---:|:---|:---:|
| $p$ | A large **prime number** (defines the cyclic group $\mathbb{Z}_p^*$) | Public |
| $g$ | A **primitive root modulo $p$** (generator of the multiplicative group) | Public |
| $a$ | Alice's **private key** (random integer, $1 \le a \le p-2$) | Secret to Alice |
| $b$ | Bob's **private key** (random integer, $1 \le b \le p-2$) | Secret to Bob |
| $A$ | Alice's **public key** $= g^a \bmod p$ | Public |
| $B$ | Bob's **public key** $= g^b \bmod p$ | Public |
| $K$ | The **shared secret key** $= g^{ab} \bmod p$ | Computed by both |

> [!NOTE]
> **Primitive Root (Generator):** A number $g$ is a primitive root modulo prime $p$ if its powers $g^1, g^2, \dots, g^{p-1}$ generate **all** non-zero residues modulo $p$. This ensures that **every** non-zero element of $\mathbb{Z}_p^*$ can be expressed as $g^k \bmod p$ for some $k$.

## 4. Operational Context in Modern Cryptography

Diffie-Hellman is the cornerstone of **forward-secret key agreement**. It is widely deployed in:
- **TLS 1.3** handshake (using **X25519** / **X448** elliptic-curve variants)
- **IPSec / IKEv2** VPN key exchange
- **SSH** session key establishment
- **Signal Protocol** (double-ratchet variant)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU Note: A frequently confused point is between **Diffie-Hellman** and **RSA**. RSA is an *asymmetric encryption and signing* scheme. DH is a *key-agreement* scheme. They are mathematically distinct primitives, even though both are foundational public-key systems.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# ⚙️ Deep Theoretical Analysis & Formula Sheet

## 1. Mathematical Foundation — The Discrete Logarithm Problem (DLP)

The entire security of DH hinges on the **asymmetry of computation** between two operations:

> **Easy direction (Forward):** Given $g$, $a$, and $p$, computing $A = g^a \bmod p$ is **fast** — using the **modular exponentiation / square-and-multiply** algorithm in $O(\log a)$ multiplications.

> **Hard direction (Reverse):** Given $g$, $A$, and $p$, finding the exponent $a$ such that $g^a \equiv A \pmod{p}$ is the **Discrete Logarithm Problem (DLP)** — believed to be **computationally intractable** for sufficiently large primes (typically $\ge 2048$ bits today).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Insight:** Any protocol based on modular exponentiation (DH, ElGamal, DSA) is secure *only* because **no polynomial-time algorithm is known** for the DLP on classical computers. The advent of **Shor's algorithm** on a quantum computer would break DH in polynomial time — which is why **Post-Quantum Cryptography (PQC)** is a hot research area.

## 2. The Algorithm — Step-by-Step Logical Flow

### **Setup Phase (Public Parameter Generation)**

1. Choose a large **prime number** $p$ of appropriate bit-length (e.g., 2048 bits for production).
2. Choose an integer $g$ that is a **primitive root modulo $p$** (or at least generates a large-order subgroup).
3. **Publish** both $p$ and $g$ as public parameters. (Eve can see these — they are not secret.)

### **Key Generation Phase**

4. **Alice** picks a random private key $a \in \{1, 2, \dots, p-2\}$.
5. **Alice** computes her public key: $A = g^a \bmod p$, and transmits $A$ to Bob over the public channel.
6. **Bob** picks a random private key $b \in \{1, 2, \dots, p-2\}$.
7. **Bob** computes his public key: $B = g^b \bmod p$, and transmits $B$ to Alice over the public channel.

### **Shared Secret Computation Phase**

8. **Alice** receives $B$ and computes: $K_A = B^a \bmod p = (g^b)^a \bmod p = g^{ab} \bmod p$.
9. **Bob** receives $A$ and computes: $K_B = A^b \bmod p = (g^a)^b \bmod p = g^{ab} \bmod p$.
10. Result: $K_A = K_B = K = g^{ab} \bmod p$ — the **shared secret**. ✓

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is $K_A = K_B$?** This works because multiplication of exponents is commutative: $b \cdot a = a \cdot b$ in the exponent of $g$ within modular arithmetic. This is the **mathematical miracle** that makes DH possible.

## 3. KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Step | Operation | Formula | Computation Cost | Notes |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---:|:---|
| 1 | Public Prime | $p$ (large prime) | — | Must be prime; $p-1$ should have a large prime factor |
| 2 | Public Generator | $g$ (primitive root mod $p$) | — | Smallest primitive root is preferred for efficiency |
| 3 | Alice's Public Key | $A = g^a \bmod p$ | $O(\log_2 a)$ modular multiplications | Done via square-and-multiply |
| 4 | Bob's Public Key | $B = g^b \bmod p$ | $O(\log_2 b)$ modular multiplications | Done via square-and-multiply |
| 5 | Alice's Shared Secret | $K_A = B^a \bmod p$ | $O(\log_2 a)$ modular multiplications | Equivalently $g^{ab} \bmod p$ |
| 6 | Bob's Shared Secret | $K_B = A^b \bmod p$ | $O(\log_2 b)$ modular multiplications | Equivalently $g^{ab} \bmod p$ |
| 7 | Final Shared Secret | $K = g^{ab} \bmod p$ | — | Both parties reach the same $K$ |
| 8 | Eve's Knowledge | $\{p, g, A, B\}$ | — | Cannot recover $a$, $b$, or $K$ efficiently |
| 9 | DLP Hardness | Given $g, A, p$ find $a$ s.t. $g^a \equiv A \pmod p$ | Sub-exponential best known | Security basis of DH |
| 10 | Group Order | $\vert \mathbb{Z}_p^* \vert = p - 1$ | — | Number of elements in the multiplicative group |

## 4. Engineering Utility & Real-World Application

| Field | Application of DH |
|:---|:---|
| **Web Security (HTTPS/TLS)** | Used during the TLS handshake to establish a session key for symmetric encryption (AES, ChaCha20) |
| **Virtual Private Networks (VPNs)** | IKEv2 uses DH to derive shared keys for IPsec tunnel encryption |
| **Secure Shell (SSH)** | DH variants (e.g., Curve25519) provide forward secrecy in SSH-2 |
| **End-to-End Messaging (Signal, WhatsApp)** | X3DH (Extended Triple Diffie-Hellman) provides initial key agreement |
| **Blockchain / Cryptocurrencies** | Some wallets use ECDH (Elliptic Curve DH) for stealth addresses |

> [!NOTE]
> **Forward Secrecy (PFS — Perfect Forward Secrecy):** When DH is used with **ephemeral keys** (ECDHE, DHE — note the trailing "E"), a new key pair is generated for *every session*. Compromise of long-term keys does **not** compromise past sessions. This is critical in modern TLS 1.3.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 🧮 Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Walkthrough

## Worked Example — Full Numerical Trace

Let us trace a small instance with hand-computable values to internalize the algorithm.

> **Given Public Parameters:** $p = 23$, $g = 5$ (5 is a primitive root of 23, since its powers generate all non-zero residues mod 23).
>
> **Chosen Private Keys:** Alice picks $a = 6$; Bob picks $b = 15$.

### Step 1: Alice computes her Public Key $A$

We need to compute $A = g^a \bmod p = 5^6 \bmod 23$.

We use the **square-and-multiply** technique for clarity:

$$
5^1 \bmod 23 = 5
$$

$$
5^2 \bmod 23 = 25 \bmod 23 = 2
$$

$$
5^4 \bmod 23 = (5^2)^2 \bmod 23 = 2^2 \bmod 23 = 4
$$

Now, since $6 = 4 + 2$ (binary: $110$):

$$
5^6 \bmod 23 = 5^4 \cdot 5^2 \bmod 23 = 4 \cdot 2 \bmod 23 = 8 \bmod 23 = 8
$$

> **Result:** $\boxed{A = 8}$

### Step 2: Bob computes his Public Key $B$

We need to compute $B = g^b \bmod p = 5^{15} \bmod 23$.

Decompose $15 = 8 + 4 + 2 + 1$ (binary: $1111$):

$$
5^8 \bmod 23 = (5^4)^2 \bmod 23 = 4^2 \bmod 23 = 16
$$

$$
5^4 \bmod 23 = 4 \quad \text{(already computed above)}
$$

$$
5^2 \bmod 23 = 2 \quad \text{(already computed above)}
$$

$$
5^1 \bmod 23 = 5
$$

Now combine using multiplication:

$$
\begin{aligned}
5^{15} \bmod 23 &= 5^8 \cdot 5^4 \cdot 5^2 \cdot 5^1 \bmod 23 \\
&= 16 \cdot 4 \cdot 2 \cdot 5 \bmod 23 \\
&= (16 \cdot 4) \cdot (2 \cdot 5) \bmod 23 \\
&= 64 \cdot 10 \bmod 23
\end{aligned}
$$

Now reduce each factor modulo 23:

$$
64 \bmod 23 = 64 - 2(23) = 64 - 46 = 18
$$

$$
10 \bmod 23 = 10
$$

$$
5^{15} \bmod 23 = 18 \cdot 10 \bmod 23 = 180 \bmod 23
$$

$$
180 \div 23 = 7.826 \dots \implies 7 \times 23 = 161
$$

$$
180 - 161 = 19
$$

> **Result:** $\boxed{B = 19}$

### Step 3: Alice computes the Shared Secret $K_A$

Alice has received $B = 19$ from Bob. She computes:

$$
K_A = B^a \bmod p = 19^6 \bmod 23
$$

Use square-and-multiply on $19$:

$$
19^1 \bmod 23 = 19
$$

$$
19^2 \bmod 23 = 361 \bmod 23
$$

$$
361 = 15 \times 23 + 16 \implies 19^2 \bmod 23 = 16
$$

$$
19^4 \bmod 23 = (19^2)^2 \bmod 23 = 16^2 \bmod 23 = 256 \bmod 23
$$

$$
256 = 11 \times 23 + 3 \implies 19^4 \bmod 23 = 3
$$

Since $6 = 4 + 2$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
19^6 \bmod 23 &= 19^4 \cdot 19^2 \bmod 23 \\
&= 3 \cdot 16 \bmod 23 \\
&= 48 \bmod 23 \\
&= 48 - 2(23) \\
&= 48 - 46 \\
&= 2
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Result:** $\boxed{K_A = 2}$

### Step 4: Bob computes the Shared Secret $K_B$

Bob has received $A = 8$ from Alice. He computes:

$$
K_B = A^b \bmod p = 8^{15} \bmod 23
$$

Decompose $15 = 8 + 4 + 2 + 1$:

$$
8^1 \bmod 23 = 8
$$

$$
8^2 \bmod 23 = 64 \bmod 23 = 64 - 46 = 18
$$

$$
8^4 \bmod 23 = (8^2)^2 \bmod 23 = 18^2 \bmod 23 = 324 \bmod 23
$$

$$
324 = 14 \times 23 + 2 \implies 8^4 \bmod 23 = 2
$$

$$
8^8 \bmod 23 = (8^4)^2 \bmod 23 = 2^2 \bmod 23 = 4
$$

Now combine:

$$
\begin{aligned}
8^{15} \bmod 23 &= 8^8 \cdot 8^4 \cdot 8^2 \cdot 8^1 \bmod 23 \\
&= 4 \cdot 2 \cdot 18 \cdot 8 \bmod 23 \\
&= (4 \cdot 2) \cdot (18 \cdot 8) \bmod 23 \\
&= 8 \cdot 144 \bmod 23
\end{aligned}
$$

Reduce modulo 23:

$$
8 \bmod 23 = 8
$$

$$
144 \bmod 23 = 144 - 6(23) = 144 - 138 = 6
$$

$$
8^{15} \bmod 23 = 8 \cdot 6 \bmod 23 = 48 \bmod 23 = 2
$$

> **Result:** $\boxed{K_B = 2}$

### ✅ Verification

$$
K_A = K_B = 2
$$

The shared secret $K = 2$ has been successfully established. **Alice and Bob now possess an identical secret key**, despite never having directly transmitted it, and despite Eve having observed the values $p = 23$, $g = 5$, $A = 8$, and $B = 19$ in transit.

### Step 5: Eve's Perspective — Why DH is Secure

Eve observes $\{p = 23, g = 5, A = 8, B = 19\}$ in the public channel. To compromise the scheme, she must solve the DLP:

$$
\text{Given } g = 5, \; A = 8, \; p = 23, \; \text{find } a \text{ such that } 5^a \equiv 8 \pmod{23}
$$

Brute-forcing:
- $5^1 \equiv 5$, $5^2 \equiv 2$, $5^3 \equiv 10$, $5^4 \equiv 4$, $5^5 \equiv 20$, $5^6 \equiv 8$ ✓

For this tiny $p$, Eve can solve it instantly. But when $p$ is **2048 bits** long, the DLP has **no known efficient classical solution** — the search space is astronomically large, and even the fastest sub-exponential algorithms (Index Calculus, Number Field Sieve) require infeasible time.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mathematical Elegance of DH:** The trick is that Alice computes $K = (g^b)^a \bmod p$ and Bob computes $K = (g^a)^b \bmod p$. By the laws of exponents, both equal $g^{ab} \bmod p$. Eve, however, only sees $g^a$ and $g^b$ — and recovering the product exponent $ab$ from the *individual* exponents is the DLP.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 📊 Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## Figure 1: Diffie-Hellman Protocol Flow (Sequence Diagram)

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the **complete message-passing sequence** of the Diffie-Hellman key agreement protocol between Alice and Bob, with Eve acting as the passive eavesdropper.

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    autonumber
    participant Alice as Alice
    participant Eve as Eve (Eavesdropper)
    participant Bob as Bob

    Note over Alice,Bob: Public Channel - Eve Can Observe All Transmissions
    Note over Alice,Bob: PUBLIC PARAMETERS (Pre-Agreed)
    Alice->>Bob: p = 23, g = 5
    Bob->>Alice: Acknowledged

    Note over Alice: Pick private key a = 6
    Alice->>Alice: A = g^a mod p = 5^6 mod 23 = 8

    Note over Bob: Pick private key b = 15
    Bob->>Bob: B = g^b mod p = 5^15 mod 23 = 19

    Alice->>Eve: Send Public Key A = 8
    Eve->>Bob: Intercepts A = 8
    Bob->>Eve: Acknowledge

    Bob->>Eve: Send Public Key B = 19
    Eve->>Alice: Intercepts B = 19
    Alice->>Bob: Acknowledge

    Note over Alice: Receives B = 19
    Alice->>Alice: K_A = B^a mod p = 19^6 mod 23 = 2

    Note over Bob: Receives A = 8
    Bob->>Bob: K_B = A^b mod p = 8^15 mod 23 = 2

    Note over Alice,Bob: SHARED SECRET ESTABLISHED - K = 2
    Alice->>Alice: K_A = 2
    Bob->>Bob: K_B = 2

    Note over Eve: Knows p, g, A, B but NOT a, b, or K
```

## Figure 2: Functional Architecture — Alice's and Bob's Computational Pipeline

This block-level diagram shows the **internal computation flow** of each party, mapping inputs, intermediate values, and outputs.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph PUBLIC[PUBLIC PARAMETERS - Shared Knowledge]
        P1[Prime p]
        P2[Generator g]
    end

    subgraph ALICE[ALICE SIDE]
        A1[Private Key a]
        A2[Compute A = g^a mod p]
        A3[Receive B from Bob]
        A4[Compute K_A = B^a mod p]
        A5[Shared Secret K]
    end

    subgraph BOB[BOB SIDE]
        B1[Private Key b]
        B2[Compute B = g^b mod p]
        B3[Receive A from Alice]
        B4[Compute K_B = A^b mod p]
        B5[Shared Secret K]
    end

    P1 --> A2
    P1 --> B2
    P2 --> A2
    P2 --> B2

    A1 --> A2
    B1 --> B2

    A2 -- Public Channel: Send A --> B3
    B2 -- Public Channel: Send B --> A3

    B3 --> B4
    A3 --> A4

    B4 --> B5
    A4 --> A5

    A5 --> EVE_BLOCK
    B5 --> EVE_BLOCK

    EVE_BLOCK[Eve Observes: p, g, A, B - Cannot Derive K]
```

## Figure 3: Security Barrier — The Asymmetry of Computation

This schematic highlights the **fundamental asymmetry** between Alice/Bob's *easy* computation and Eve's *hard* DLP computation.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph EASY[Trivial for Alice and Bob]
        E1[g, a, p] --> E2[Easy: g^a mod p]
        E3[g, b, p] --> E4[Easy: g^b mod p]
        E5[B, a, p] --> E6[Easy: B^a mod p]
        E7[A, b, p] --> E8[Easy: A^b mod p]
    end

    subgraph HARD[Infeasible for Eve]
        H1[g, A, p] --> H2[Hard: Discrete Log]
        H3[g, B, p] --> H4[Hard: Discrete Log]
        H5[Observe A and B] --> H6[Hard: Compute g to the ab]
    end

    E2 --> E9[Public Key A]
    E4 --> E10[Public Key B]
    E6 --> E11[Shared Secret K]
    E8 --> E12[Shared Secret K]

    E9 --> H1
    E10 --> H3
    E11 --> E13[Identical K]
    E12 --> E13
```
